title: EXPOSURE XVIII THE GLOBAL DUALITY FORMULA

pages.title: EXPOSURE XVIII THE GLOBAL DUALITY FORMULA

blocks.html:
EXHIBITION XVIII
THE GLOBAL DUALITY FORMULAby P. Deligne
0. Introduction

0.1. This presentation is devoted to Poincaré duality. This duality is built on the VERDIER model [1], which deals with the case of locally compact separated topological spaces. We show a priori that the functor \(Rf_!\) of presentation XVII admits a right adjoint \(Rf^!\) and we establish many of its properties, before calculating it more explicitly in the case of smooth morphisms.
The possibility of such a construction seems to rely on the following properties of the functor \(Rf_!\)
a) For the existence of an adjoint \(Rf^!\) and its formal properties:

1. (I) the functor \(Rf_!\) commutes on base changes;
  2. (II) the functors \(R^i f_!\) commute at the filtering inductive limits; they suck for \(i\) big enough;
  3. (III) the functor \(Rf_!\) can be calculated "at the level of complexes", i.e. from a functor between categories of complexes.
b) For the calculation of this adjoint in the case where \(f\) is smooth:
1. (IV) the calculation of \(Rf_!((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_U)\) for \(U\) "small".

0.2. In § 3 no. 1, which is independent of § 1, 2, we construct the functor \(Rf^!\); we use constructions special to the situation, and not only (I), (II) and (III). This makes this number quite unpleasant; the editor confesses to not always having fully understood what was happening.
§ 2 is devoted to point (IV); it is purely formal from § 1, where the cohomology of curves is studied.
In § 1, we establish clearly more than is necessary for § 2, by also studying the case of continuous coefficients. This paragraph was deeply influenced by SERRE [1]. Useful points for the rest are:
a) n° 1.1, devoted to trace morphism in the case of curves, and generalized in 2.9;

b) the erasure theorem 1.6.9. The reader ready to admit a transcendent argument can read n° 1.6, starting from 1.6.6 (2nd demonstration), independently of n° 1.2 to 1.5. The essential ingredient of 1.6.9 is either form of Poincaré duality for smooth curves over an algebraically closed field, and acyclicity lemma XV 2.6, (preliminary to the smooth base change theorem XVI 1.1). His conclusion is generalized in 2.17.
No. 3.2 collects the fruits of § 2.

0.3. Verdier's method in Poincaré duality makes it possible to define the functor \(Rf^!\) for \(f\) compactifiable. This generality has the counterpart that the compatibilities to be checked are not familiar, and sometimes, even for the most trivial ones, such as 3.2.3, a preposterous demonstration. The editor admits to not having demonstrated as much as he should have.

The interested reader will be able without difficulty, from the appendix to presentation XVII, to extend the definition of \(Rf^!\) to the case of separated morphisms of finite goal type, a coherent schema (i.e. quasi-compact quasi-separated).

0.4. The main result 3.2.5 is stated in concrete terms which would allow us to formally return to the point of view which had been that of the oral seminar, where the functor \(Rf^!\) was defined only for morphisms limifiable via a factorization by a smooth morphism and an immersion. An outline of the demonstration of the oral seminar can be found in VERDIER [2].
1. Cohomology of curves.
1.1. The trace morphism.

1.1.1. Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geqslant 1\) and \(X\) be a schema on which \(n\) is invertible. The bundle \(\mu_n\) (IX 3.1) on \(X\) is then a locally free bundle of modules of rank 1 on the constant ring bundle \(\mathbb{Z}/n\). For \(i \in \mathbb{Z}\), we denote by the notation \(\mathbb{Z}/n\,(i)\) its tensor power \(i\)th.
\[(1.1.1.1)\qquad \mathbb{Z}/n\,(i) = \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbb{Z}/n, \mathbb{G}_m)^{\otimes i} \;.\]
If \(n = dn'\), the application of exponentiation by \(d\) establishes an isomorphism
\[\mu_n \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/n} \mathbb{Z}/n' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu_{n'} \;,\]
hence the so-called canonical isomorphisms:
\[(1.1.1.2)\qquad \mathbb{Z}/n\,(i) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/n} \mathbb{Z}/n' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb{Z}/n'\,(i) \;.\]
If \(F\) is an abelian sheaf such that \(nF = 0\), we set
\[(1.1.1.3)\qquad F(i) = F \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/n} \mathbb{Z}/n\,(i) \;.\]

If already \(n'F = 0\), the isomorphism (1.1.1.2) makes it possible to identify \(F \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/n} \mathbb{Z}/n\,(i)\) and \(F \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/n'} \mathbb{Z}/n'\,(i)\), so that \(F(i)\) only depends on \(F\) and not on the choice of \(n\). More generally, if \(F\) is a prime torsion beam with residual characteristics of \(X\) (XVII 0.13), and if \({}_m F\) is the kernel of the multiplication by \(m\) in \(F\), we define \(F(i)\) as being the inductive limit, for \(m\) invertible on \(X\) tending multiplicatively towards infinity
\[(1.1.1.4)\qquad F(i) = \varinjlim_m {}_m F(i) \;.\]

The functor \(F \mapsto F(i)\) is exact. If \(f : Y \to X\) is a schema morphism (resp. a quasi-separated quasi-compact schema morphism), and if \(F\) is a torsion beam on \(X\) (resp. on \(Y\)), prime to the residual characteristics of \(X\), we have \(f^*(F(i)) \simeq (f^*F)(i)\) (resp. we have \((f_*F)(i) \simeq f_*(F(i))\)). If \(\mathcal{A}\) is a ring bundle on \(X\), canceled by \(n\) invertible on \(X\), it extends to an exact functor \(K \mapsto K(i)\) of \(D(X,\mathcal{A})\) in \(D(X,\mathcal{A})\). This functor commutes to reciprocal image functors and direct image functors.
\(F \mapsto F(i)\) functors are sometimes called "Tate twist" functors.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.2

A flat curve on a diagram \(S\) is a flat \(f : X \to S\) morphism of finite and separated presentation with purely one-dimensional fibers.
The expression "purely one-dimensional" does not exclude the empty schema. Unlike EGA II 7.4.2, we accept here that a curve on a body is empty, but we require that it be separated.
When \(S\) is the spectrum of a body, we will simply speak of curve on \(S\); a curve on a body is quasi-projective.

1.1.3. Let \(X\) be a curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\) with characteristic exponent \(p\) prime to an integer \(n \geqslant 1\). Let us first assume \(X\) reduced, and let \(\overline{X}\) be a complete curve on \(k\) of which \(X\) is a dense open. The complement \(Y = \overline{X} - X\) is of dimension 0. The exact cohomology sequence (XVII 5.1.16.3) therefore induces an isomorphism
\[(1.1.3.1)\qquad H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^2_c(\overline{X}, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \;.\]
We have more than one isomorphism IX 4.7 (given by Kummer's theory)
\[(1.1.3.2)\qquad H^2_c(\overline{X}, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \simeq \operatorname{Pic}(\overline{X})/n \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n)^c\]
where \(c\) designates the set of irreducible components of \(X\), or of \(\overline{X}\), this amounts to the same thing.

If \(X\) is no longer necessarily reduced, and if \(c\) is the set of its irreducible components, let us denote, for \(i \in c\), by \(n_i\) the multiplicity of \(X\) at the generic point \(\eta_i\) of the \(i\)th irreducible component of \(X\):
\[n_i = \operatorname{lg}(\mathcal{O}_{X,\eta_i}) \;.\]

We know that the restriction application of \(H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1))\) in \(H^2_c(X_{\mathrm{red}}, \mathbb{Z}/n(1))\) is an isomorphism, hence, via (1.1.3.1) and (1.1.3.2) applied to \(X_{\mathrm{red}}\) a canonical isomorphism between \(H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1))\) and \((\mathbb{Z}/n)^c\). We will designate by \(\mathrm{Tr}_X\) or simply \(\mathrm{Tr}\) the compound arrow
\[(1.1.3.3)\qquad \mathrm{Tr} : H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathbb{Z}/n)^c \xrightarrow{\ t\ } \mathbb{Z}/n \;,\]
where
If \(F\) is an abelian group canceled by \(n\), we have an isomorphism
\[H^2_c(X, F(1)) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } F \otimes H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1))\]
and therefore via (1.1.3.3) of a trace morphism
If \(n = n'd\), the diagram

\[(1.1.3.5)\qquad \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d]^{x^d} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]^{x^n} \ar[d]^{x^d} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_{n'} \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]_{x^{n'}} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & 0 }\]
is commutative and therefore also the diagram
\[(1.1.3.6)\qquad \xymatrix{ H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & (\mathbb{Z}/n)^c \ar[d] \\ H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n'(1)) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & (\mathbb{Z}/n')^c }\]
according to definition IX 4.7 of isomorphism (1.1.3.2).

The arrow (1.1.3.4) therefore only depends on \(F\), and not on the choice of the integer \(n\) first to \(p\) such that \(nF = 0\). By passing to the limit, we define it for any abelian group \(F\) of torsion prime to \(p\).
The following lemma follows immediately from the definitions.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.4

Under the previous hypotheses, if, for \(i \in c\), \(U_i\) is a non-empty open of \(X\) contained in the \(i\)th irreducible component, the diagram

\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=0.6em{ \bigoplus_{i \in c} H^2_c(U_i, F(1)) \ar[rr]^-{\sim} \ar[dr]_{\bigoplus \mathrm{Tr}_{U_i}} & & H^2_c(X, F(1)) \ar[dl]^{\mathrm{Tr}_X} \\ & F & }\]

is commutative.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.5

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two curves on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(u : X \to Y\) a quasi-finite and flat morphism and \(n\) a prime integer with the characteristic exponent \(p\) from \(k\). The following diagram, in which \(\mathrm{Tr}_u\) is the arrow (XVII 6.2.3), is then commutative

\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=0.6em{ H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{Tr}_u} \ar[dr]_{\mathrm{Tr}_X} & & H^2_c(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[dl]^{\mathrm{Tr}_Y} \\ & \mathbb{Z}/n & }\]

Lemma 1.1.4 allows us to reduce to the case where \(X\) and \(Y\) are irreducible, \(X_{\mathrm{red}}\) and \(Y_{\mathrm{red}}\) being smoother. If \(e\) (resp. \(f\)) is the multiplicity of \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) at the generic point, and if \(e = df\), the triangles marked \(+\) of the following diagram are commutative:

\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=0.5em{ H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[rr]^-{\sim} \ar[dd]_{\mathrm{Tr}_u} \ar[dr]^-{\mathrm{Tr}_X} & & H^2_c(X_{\mathrm{red}}, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[dd]^{d.\mathrm{Tr}_{u_{\mathrm{red}}}} \ar[dl]_-{e.\mathrm{Tr}_{X_{\mathrm{red}}}} \\ & \mathbb{Z}/n & \\ H^2_c(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[rr]_-{\sim} \ar[ur]_-{\mathrm{Tr}_Y} & & H^2_c(Y_{\mathrm{red}}, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[ul]^-{f.\mathrm{Tr}_{Y_{\mathrm{red}}}} }\]
Its contour is commutative, as deduced from the commutativity of the diagram

\[\xymatrix{ u_! \mathbb{Z}/n_X(1) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{\mathrm{Tr}_u} & u_{\mathrm{red}!}\, \mathbb{Z}/n_{X_{\mathrm{red}}}(1) \ar[d]^{d.\mathrm{Tr}_{u_{\mathrm{red}}}} \\ \mathbb{Z}/n_Y(1) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \mathbb{Z}/n_{Y_{\mathrm{red}}}(1) }\]
Just check this last compatibility at the generic point of \(Y\), which is trivial.

Let \(\overline{X}\) and \(\overline{Y}\) be the complete non-singular curves containing \(X_{\mathrm{red}}\) and \(Y_{\mathrm{red}}\) as dense open (EGA II 7.4.11). The morphism \(u_{\mathrm{red}}\) extends to a flat morphism \(\overline{u} : \overline{X} \to \overline{Y}\) (EGA II 7.4.9) and, according to 1.1.4, just check 1.1.5 for \(\overline{u}\).
According to (XVII 6.3.18.2), the beam diagram on \(Y\), in which \(N\) denotes the standard,

\[\xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{u}_*\mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\mathrm{Tr}_u} & \overline{u}_*\mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]^{x^n} \ar[d]^N & \overline{u}_*\mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar[d]^N & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_n \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]_{x^n} & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & 0 }\]
is commutative, so also the diagram
\[\xymatrix{ \operatorname{Pic}(X)/n \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_N & H^2_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) \ar[d]^{\mathrm{Tr}_u} \\ \operatorname{Pic}(Y)/n \ar[r]_-{\sim} & H^2_c(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)) }\]

and it remains to note that if \(\mathcal{L}\) is an invertible beam of degree 1 on \(X\), its norm is still of degree 1 (this fact resulting from EGA IV 21.10.7 compatibility, taking into account the EGA IV 21.5.5 definition of the norm of a divider).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.1.6

It is in one and only one way possible to define, for any compactifiable flat curve \(f : X \to S\) and for any torsion beam \(F\) on \(S\), prime with the residual characteristics of \(S\), a trace morphism

\[\mathrm{Tr}_f : R^2 f_!(f^*F(1)) \to F \;,\]

functional in \(F\), of formation compatible with any change of base (cf. XVII 6.2.3, (VAR 2)) and which, for \(S\) the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, coincides with the trace morphism (1.1.3.4).
The trace morphism \(\mathrm{Tr}_f\) is given to us fiber by fiber; its uniqueness is therefore clear.

Let \(p : \mathbb{P}^1_S \to S\) be the projective line on \(S\) and \(n\) be an invertible integer on \(S\). We have \(\operatorname{Pic}_S(\mathbb{P}^1_S) = \mathbb{Z}_S\), hence by Kummer's theory (IX 3.2) a morphism \(\mathbb{Z}/n \to R^2 p_* \mathbb{Z}/n(1)\), which we verify fiber by fiber (XII 5.2) that it is an isomorphism. Its inverse \(\mathrm{Tr}_p\) induces fiber by fiber the trace morphism (1.1.3.4).

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a flat curve on \(S\) such that there exists a \(S\)-quasi-finite and flat morphism \(u\) of \(X\) in \(\mathbb{P}^1_S\). By virtue of 1.1.5., the compound morphism \(\mathrm{Tr}_p \circ \mathrm{Tr}_u\) (XVII 6.2.3) admits for fiber at any geometric point of \(S\) the trace morphism (1.1.3.4). In particular, it does not depend on the choice of \(u\).

Suppose that \(X\) is a union of open subschemas \(U_i\) such that there exists a quasi-finite and flat morphism of \(U_i\) into \(\mathbb{P}^1_S\). If \(\alpha_i\) (resp. \(\alpha_{ij}\)) is the inclusion of \(U_i\) (resp. \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\)) in \(X\), the rest
\[\sum R^2(f\alpha_{ij})_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \rightrightarrows \sum R^2(f\alpha_i)_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \to R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \to 0 \;.\]

is exact, according to (XVII 6.2.8) and the correctness to the right of \(R^2 f_!\) (since \(R^3 f_! = 0\)). We verify fiber by fiber, by 1.1.5., that the sum of the trace morphisms \(\sum_i R^2(f\alpha_i)_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \to \mathbb{Z}/n\), is factorized by a trace morphism \(\mathrm{Tr}_f\) of \(R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) which, fiber by fiber, induces (1.1.3.4).

For a general \(X\), let \(j : X' \to X\) be the largest open of \(X\) such that \(f|X'\) is a Cohen-Macaulay morphism (cf. EGA IV 12.1.1 (vi)). Every point \(x\) of \(X'\) has a neighborhood \(U_x\) in \(X'\) such that there exists a quasi-finite \(S\)-morphism \(u_x\) of \(U_x\) in \(\mathbb{P}^1_S\). This morphism is automatically flat (EGA IV 11.3.10 and 15.4.2) and \(X'\) therefore verifies the previous hypotheses. Additionally, \(X'\) is dense fiber by fiber in \(X\); the morphism of \(R^2(fj)_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)\) into \(R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1)\) is therefore an isomorphism as we see fiber by fiber and the compound arrow
\[\mathrm{Tr}_f : R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } R^2(fj)_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{Tr}_{fj}\ } \mathbb{Z}/n\]
armature fiber by fiber (1.1.3.4).
If \(F\) checks \(nF = 0\), we have (XVII 5.2.6)
\[(1.1.6.1)\qquad R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \otimes F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^2 f_!(f^*F(1))\]
and we define \(\mathrm{Tr}_f\) as the compound arrow
\[\mathrm{Tr}_f : R^2 f_!(f^*F(1)) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } R^2 f_! \, \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \otimes F \to F \;.\]

In the general case, we define \(\mathrm{Tr}_f\) as the inductive limit of the trace morphisms relating to the sheaves \(F_n\) for \(n\) invertible on \(S\). These arrows induce fiber by fiber (1.1.3.4) and therefore commute at any change of base, cqfd.
We check fiber by fiber using 1.1.4, 1.1.5 and (1.1.6.1), the following results:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.7

Let \(f : Y \to S\) be a flat curve compactifiable on \(S\), \(u : X \to Y\) a flat quasi-finite morphism of finite presentation, and \(F\) a torsion beam on \(S\), from first torsion to the residual characteristics of \(S\). The following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ R^2(fu)_!(fu)^*F(1) = R^2 f_!(u_! u^*)f^*F(1) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Tr}_u} \ar[d]_{\mathrm{Tr}_{fu}} & R^2 f_! f^* F(1) \ar[d]^{\mathrm{Tr}_f} \\ F \ar@{=}[r] & F }\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.8

Let \(u : Y \to S\) be a flat quasi-finite morphism of finite presentation, \(f : X \to Y\) be a flat \(S\)-compactifiable curve (XVII 3.2.1), and \(F\) as in 1.1.7. The following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ R^2(uf)_!(uf)^*F(1) = u_! R^2 f_! f^* u^* F(1) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Tr}_f} \ar[d]_{\mathrm{Tr}_{uf}} & u_! u^* F \ar[d]^{\mathrm{Tr}_u} \\ F \ar@{=}[r] & F }\]
We check the same from (1.1.3.2):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.9

If \(f : X \to S\) is a smooth compactifiable curve, and if the geometric fibers of \(f\) are irreducible, then the trace morphism (1.1.6) is an isomorphism.
1.2. \(1\)-acyclicity of projective space.
In this issue, we always use the fppf topology (XVII 0.10).

1.2.1. Let \(\varepsilon\) be a locally free Module on a diagram \(S\), assumed everywhere of rank \(\geqslant 2\), \(p : \mathbb{P}(\varepsilon) \to S\) the corresponding projective bundle and \(G\) an abelian sheaf on the large fppf site of \(S\) (XVII 0.10). Each pair \((K_0, \varphi)\) formed of a torsor \(K_0\) under \(G\) and a homomorphism \(\varphi : \mathbb{G}_{m,S} \to G\) defines a \(p^*G\)-torsor \(K(K_0, \varphi)\) on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\), sum of \(K_0\) and the image by \(\varphi\) of the \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-torsor \(\Theta(1)\).
\[(1.2.1.1)\qquad K(K_0, \varphi) = K_0 + \varphi\Theta(1) \;.\]
We make couples \((K_0, \varphi)\) a category by posing

\[\operatorname{Hom}((K_0, \varphi), (K_0', \varphi')) = \begin{cases} \varnothing & \text{si } \varphi \neq \varphi' \\ \operatorname{Hom}(K_0, K_0') & \text{si } \varphi = \varphi' \;. \end{cases}\]

The torso \(K(K_0, \varphi)\) then depends functionally on \((K_0, \varphi)\). It is compatible with localization, and therefore defines a functor of the field on \(S\) of the couples \((K_0, \varphi)\) in the field on \(S\) of the \(p^*G\)-torsors on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\) (\(=\) the field having sections on \(U/S\) the \(p^*G\)-torsors on \(U \times_S \mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\)). These fields are groupoid fields (\(=\) any morphism above a \(U/S\) is an isomorphism).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2.2

Under hypotheses 1.2.1, if \(G\) is a flat commutative group diagram of finite presentation on \(S\), then the functor \(K\) of 1.2.1 is an equivalence.
1.2.2.1 This statement is equivalent to the conjunction of
\[G \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } p_*p^*G \quad\text{et}\quad \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^1 p_* p^*G \;.\]

Indeed, a morphism of fields into a groupoid is an equivalence if and only if it induces an isomorphism on the sheaf generated by the presheaf of the isomorphy classes of objects (2nd formula) and an isomorphism on the sheaf of the automorphisms of any local object (1st formula).
We will give two demonstrations (1.2.4 and 1.2.5) of full fidelity.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.3

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a clean and flat morphism. We assume that \(f_*\mathcal{O}_X = \mathcal{O}_S\) universally and \(R^1 f_*\mathcal{O}_X = 0\) universally. Any \(S\)-morphism of \(X\) in a \(S\)-group schema of finite type \(G\) is then factorized by \(f\) and a section of \(G\); more precisely, \(G \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f_*f^*G\).
It is enough to prove the 1st assertion.

According to the rigidity lemma (Mumford [1, 6.1 pg 115]; note that Mumford does not use the Noetherian hypothesis made on \(S\)), it is enough to treat the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. Let us first assume that \(G\) is an abelian scheme, and let \(G^\vee\) be the dual abelian scheme. Since \(G = G^{\vee\vee}\), the data of \(\varphi : X \to G\) is equivalent to the data of an invertible beam \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X \times G^\vee\), trivialized along \(X \times \{e\}\) and algebraically equivalent to zero on the fibers of the projection \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) of \(X \times G^\vee\) on \(X\). If \(x : S \to X\) is a rational point of \(X\), we have \(\mathcal{L} \sim \operatorname{pr}_2^*(x \times G^\vee)^*\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}\), with \(\mathcal{M}\) trivialized along \(x \times G^\vee\) and \(X \times \{e\}\). The data of \(\mathcal{M}\) is equivalent to the data of a schema morphism from \(G^\vee\) into \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\), transforming \(e\) into \(e\). Since \(H^1(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = 0\), we have \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S} = 0\), \(\mathcal{M} \sim 0\) and \(\mathcal{L} \sim \operatorname{pr}_2^*\mathcal{L}'\) with \(\mathcal{L}'\) equivalent to zero on \(G^\vee\), therefore defining \(g \in G(S)\) such that \(\varphi = g \circ f\).
In the general case, \(G\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(A\) by an affine group \(G_0\). The previous result brings us back to the case where \(A = 0\), and we then have
\[\operatorname{Hom}_S(\operatorname{Spec}(H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)), G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}_S(X, G) \;.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.4

Under assumptions 1.2.1, if \(G\) is a finite presentation group scheme on \(S\) or is defined by a quasi-consistent sheaf on \(S\), then the functor (1.2.1.1) is fully faithful.

A. Let us prove that if \(K(K_0, \varphi)\) is isomorphic to \(K(K_0', \varphi')\), then \(\varphi = \varphi'\). For \(G\) quasi-consistent, we have \(\varphi = \varphi' = 0\), and the assertion is empty. For \(G\) of finite presentation, according to (SGA 3 IX 5.1), it is enough to prove the assertion for \(S\) spectrum of an algebraically closed body \(k\). Let then \(T\) be the largest subtorus of \(G\), and \(H = G/T\). The exact cohomology sequence provides

\[0 \to H^0(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), T) \to H^0(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), G) \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } H^0(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), H) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } H^1(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), T) \xrightarrow{\ \beta\ } H^1(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), G) \;.\]

According to 1.2.3. \(H^0(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), G) \sim G(k)\) and \(H^0(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), H) \sim H(k)\), so that \(\alpha\) is surjective and \(\delta\) null. If \(Y(T) = \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, T)\), we have \(T \sim \mathbb{G}_m \otimes Y(T)\) and \(H^1(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), T) \sim Y(T)\). The injectivity of \(\beta\) therefore expresses the injectivity of the canonical arrow, defined by \(\Theta(1)\), \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, G) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, T) \to H^1(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon), G)\), and this proves the assertion.

B. Proving that functor 1.2.1.1 is fully faithful is a local question on \(S\). We therefore come back to supposing that \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)/S\) admits a section \(s\). If \(K(K_0, \varphi)\) is isomorphic to \(K(K_0', \varphi')\), we have \(\varphi = \varphi'\) by A and \(K_0 \sim s^*K(K_0, \varphi) \sim s^*K(K_0', \varphi') \sim K_0'\). Thanks to the formula
\[(1.2.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(K(K_0, \varphi), K(K_0', \varphi')) \sim \operatorname{Hom}(K(K_0 - K_0', \varphi - \varphi'), G_{\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)}) \;,\]
all we have to do is show that
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{tors}}(G, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{tors}}(p^*G, p^*G) \;.\]
This follows from 1.2.3.
1.2.5. Here is another, more Kunnutesque, demonstration of full fidelity. The question is local to \(S\); according to (1.2.4.1), it is therefore enough to check that
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)}(G, K(G, \varphi)) \begin{cases} = \varnothing & \text{si } \varphi \neq 0 \\ \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } G & \text{si } \varphi = 0 \;. \end{cases}\]
This is exactly what the following lemma expresses:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.6

Let \(\varepsilon\) be a locally free module everywhere of rank \(\geqslant 2\) on \(S\), \(G\) a schema in groups of finite presentation on \(S\), \(\varphi : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\) a homomorphism of groups, \(V(\varepsilon)\) the vector bundle defined by \(\varepsilon\), \(V(\varepsilon)^\times\) the corresponding blunt vector bundle and \(\Psi : V(\varepsilon)^\times \to G\) a \(S\)-morphism verifying the following identity between \(S\)-morphisms of \(\mathbb{G}_m \times V(\varepsilon)^\times\) in \(G\):

\[\Psi(\lambda v) = \varphi(\lambda)\Psi(v) \;.\]

Then, \(\varphi = e\), and \(\Psi\) is factorized by the projection \(p\) of \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\) onto \(S\).

It is enough to prove the second assertion. We immediately return to the Noetherian \(S\) case, then, by (SGA 3 IX 5.1) and the rigidity lemma (Mumford [1, 6.1 pg 115]) applied to \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\) in the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a body. Let \(n\) be an integer, \(W_n\) and \(G_n\) the infinitesimal neighborhoods of the \(n\)th order of the zero section in \(V(\varepsilon)\) and \(G\), \(\mathfrak{g}_n\) the affine algebra of \(G_n\) and \(P_n(\Psi) : V(\varepsilon)^\times \to \operatorname{Hom}_S(W_n, G)\) the main part of the \(n\)th order of \(\Psi\). Any section of \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(W_n, G)\) defines, by translating to the right by the inverse of the image of the null section, a homomorphism of \(W_n\) in \(G_n\) which transforms \(o\) into \(e\), this defines
\[\Psi_1 = P_n(\Psi).\Psi^{-1} : V(\varepsilon)^\times \to \operatorname{Hom}_S(W_n, G_n) \;.\]

The hypothesis implies that \(\Psi_1\) commutes to homotheties, which are made to act on the second member by structure transport from their action on \(W_n \subset V(\varepsilon)\). The affine algebra of \(W_n\) is \(\sum_{i=0}^n \operatorname{Sym}^i(\varepsilon)\). Given its homogeneity, \(\Psi_1\) is therefore defined by a section, on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\), of the \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)}\)-module
\[\sum_{i=0}^n p^*\operatorname{Hom}(\mathfrak{g}_n, \operatorname{Sym}^i(\varepsilon))(-i) \;.\]
The index components \(i > 0\) of this module have no other section than the zero section, and we conclude that
This, valid for all \(n\), implies that \(\Psi\) is factorized by a section of \(G\).
This second demonstration extends to prove the following analytical variant of 1.2.4. When \(G\) is affine, the proof of 1.2.2 can be done independently of this variant.

1.2.7. Let \(L\) be the field of fractions of a complete discrete valuation ring \(\mathcal{O}\), \(U_m\) the rigid analytical group "generic fiber" of the formal complement of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and \(\widehat{\Theta(1)}\) the \(U_m\)-torsor on \(\mathbb{P}^r_L\) formal complement of \(\Theta(1)\). If \(G\) is an analytical rigid group on \(L\), the functor \((K_0, \varphi) \mapsto p^*K_0 + \varphi\widehat{\Theta(1)}\) (cf. 1.2.1) is a fully faithful functor of the category of couples \((K_0, \varphi)\) formed of a \(G\)-torsor on \(\operatorname{Spec}(L)\) and of \(\varphi \in \operatorname{Hom}(U_m, G)\), in the category of \(p^*G\)-torsors on \(\mathbb{P}^r_L\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.8

Let \(k\) be a characteristic exponent body \(p\) and \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) be a proper scheme on \(k\) satisfying \(H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = k\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau_X = 0\). For any finite type commutative affine group scheme \(G\) over \(k\), not containing tori, we have \(R^1 f_*(f^*G) = 0\).

It suffices to show that after any change of base \(c : S \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\), the functor \(f^*\) of the category of \(G\)-torsors on \(S\) in that of \(G\)-torsors on \(X_S\) is an equivalence. This problem is local on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), so that to solve it we can reduce it to the case where \(X\) has a rational point, where \(G\) is a successive extension of groups \(\mathbb{G}_a\), \(\alpha_p\), \(\mu_p\), \(\mathbb{Z}/p\) and \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell_i\) for \(\ell_i\) first to \(p\) and where \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell_i \sim \mu_{\ell_i}\).

We know that \(\operatorname{Lie}(\operatorname{Pic}_X) = H^1(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = 0\). According to 1.2.3 and the existence of a section, the functors \(f^*\) considered are fully faithful; it is therefore sufficient to prove that \(R^1 f_*(f^*G) = 0\), or, by unscrewing, that
\[R^1 f_*\mathbb{G}_a = R^1 f_*\alpha_p = R^1 f_*\mu_p = R^1 f_*\mathbb{Z}/p = R^1 f_*\mathbb{Z}/\ell_i = 0 \;.\]

For \(\mathbb{G}_a\), this results from \(H^1(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = 0\). The case of \(\alpha_p\) (resp. \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)) is deduced via 1.2.3., by the exact long cohomology sequence defined by the exact sequence
\[0 \to \alpha_p \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{\ x^p\ } \mathbb{G}_a \to 0 \qquad (\text{resp. } 0 \to \mathbb{Z}/p \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{\ x - x^p\ } \mathbb{G}_a \to 0 \ (p \neq 1)) \;.\]

The case of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell_i\) (resp. of \(\mu_p\)) is deduced from the isomorphism \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell_i \sim \mu_{\ell_i}\), of 1.2.3. and the long exact sequence deduced from the Kummer exact sequence
\[0 \to \mu_n \to \mathbb{G}_m \to \mathbb{G}_m \to 0 \;.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.9

Let \(S\) be a local Artinian schema, \(\bar{s}\) be a geometric point of \(S\), \(f : X \to S\) be a clean and flat morphism, and \(G\) be a flat commutative group schema of finite type on \(S\). We assume \(f\) smooth or \(G\) refined. If \(H^0(X_{\bar{s}}, \mathcal{O}) = k(\bar{s})\), if \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau_{X_{\bar{s}}} = 0\) and \(G_{\bar{s}}\) does not contain a subgroup \(\mathbb{G}_m\), then the functor \(f^*\) of the category of \(G\)-torsors on \(S\) in that of \(f^*G\)-torsors on \(X\) is an equivalence.

a. The problem posed is local on \(S\) when we take finite and flat \(u : S' \to S\) as localization morphisms. This allows us to assume that \(f\) admits a section \(x\). The functor \(f^*\) is then fully faithful, according to 1.2.3 and the existence of \(x\), and it remains to show that any torsor, trivialized along \(x\), is trivial.

b. Suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a body. If \(G\) is affine, the assertion follows from 1.2.8. If \(X\) is smooth, and if \(G\) is an extension of an abelian variety \(A\) by an affine group \(G_0\), let us denote by \(G^n\) the reciprocal image of \({}_n A\) in \(G\). The group \(H^1(X, A)\) is of torsion (Raynaud [1, XIII 2.6]), therefore meeting the images of \(H^1(X, {}_n A)\). The group \(H^1(X, G)\) is therefore a collection of images from \(H^1(X, G^n)\). The groups \(G^n\) being affine, we conclude again with 1.2.8.

c. Let us prove the general case (assuming the existence of a section \(x\)) by induction over the length of \(S\). Let \(i : S_0 \hookrightarrow S\) be a subschema defined by an ideal \(I\) of zero square and length 1.

The \(G\)-torsors \(P\) on \(X\), such that \(x^*P\) is trivial, are according to the hypothesis of recurrence of the deformations of the \(G\)-torsor \(P_0\) on \(X \times S_0\). If \(G\) is smooth, and if \(L\) is the Lie algebra of \(G_{\bar{s}}\), these deformations are classified by
which proves 1.2.9 in this case.

For \(G\) flat on \(S\), this argument generalizes in terms of the Lie complex \(L\) of \(G_{\bar{s}}\). If \(\mathrm{L}\Lambda(G_s/k(s))\) is the relative cotangent complex of \(G_s\), this complex \(L \in D^b(k(s))\) is the dual of \(\mathrm{L}e^*(\mathrm{L}\Lambda(G_s/k(s)))\). We have \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i \neq 0, 1\).

The deformations on \(S\) (resp. on \(X\)) of the \(G\)-trivial twist on \(S_0\) (resp. on \(X_0\)) are classified by \(H^1(L \otimes I)\) (resp. by \(H^1(X_s, f^*(L \otimes I))\)) (Illusion [1]). By hypothesis, we have \(H^1(X_s, H^0(f^*(L \otimes I))) = 0\) (because \(H^1(X_s, \mathcal{O}) = 0\)) and \(H^1(L \otimes I) \sim H^0(X_s, \underline{H}^1(f^*(L \otimes I)))\). We conclude that
\[H^1(L \otimes I) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^1(X_s, f^*(L \otimes I)) \;,\]

which means that any deformation on \(X\) of the \(G\)-trivial twistor on \(X_0\) is the reciprocal image of one and only one deformation at \(S\) of the \(G\)-trivial twistor on \(S_0\). If this deformation is trivial along a section, it is therefore trivial, which proves 1.2.9.

1.2.10. Let us prove 1.2.2 when \(S\) is Artinian. Let \(T\) be the largest subtorus of \(G\); since we already have full fidelity (1.2.4), the problem is local on \(S\) for the sparse topology: we can assume \(T\) diagonalizable: \(T \sim \mathbb{G}_m^n\). By 1.2.3., and 1.2.9 the exact cohomology sequence provides
\[R^1 p_* T \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^1 p_* G \;,\]
and the assertion results, since it is trivial for \(G = T\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, T) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, G)\) (cf. 1.2.2.1).
1.2.11. Let us prove 1.2.2 when \(S\) is complete local Noetherian. Let \(s\) be a section of \(p\).

If \(K\) is a \(G\)-torsor on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\), there exists by 1.2.10 a unique pair \((\widehat{\varphi}, \widehat{\psi})\) formed of a formal homomorphism \(\widehat{\varphi} : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\) and a formal isomorphism \(\widehat{\psi} : p^*s^*K + \widehat{\varphi}\Theta(1) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \widehat{K}\). If \(G\) is affine, then \(\widehat{\psi}\) is algebraizable (SGA 3 IX 7.1). Let us provisionally admit that, as we will prove in (1.2.2.13), \(\widehat{\varphi}\) is always algebraizable in \(\varphi : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\).

If \(K_0 = s^*K\), and if \(K_1 = p^*K_0 + \varphi\Theta(1)\), we have a formal isomorphism \(\widehat{\psi} : \widehat{K_1} = \widehat{K(K_0, \varphi)} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \widehat{K}\), i.e. a formal trivialization of \(K - K_1\). Under formal GAGA, this section is algebraic, which proves 1.2.11. More precisely, if \(G\) is quasi-affine, then \(K - K_1\) is representable and we use EGA III 5.1.4. In the general case, we proceed in the same way with the algebraic space \(K - K_1\).
1.2.2.12. Algebraicity of \(\widehat{\varphi}\): preliminary verification for a basic trait.
Suppose \(S\) is a complete line, \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(V)\), and \(L\) is the body of fractions of \(V\).
a) On \(L\), there exists a unique triple \((K_{0L}, \varphi_L, \psi_L)\):
\[\psi_L : K(K_{0L}, \varphi_L) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } K|\operatorname{Spec}(L) \;.\]

b) According to (1.2.10) (1.2.2.11), and with the notations of 1.2.7, we have an analytical rigid map \(\widehat{\varphi_L} : U_m \to \widehat{G}\), a \(\widehat{G}\)-torsor \(\widehat{K_0}\) and an analytical rigid isomorphism
\[\widehat{\psi} : p^*\widehat{K_0} + \widehat{\varphi_L}\,\widehat{\Theta(1)} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \widehat{K} \;.\]

According to uniqueness assertion 1.2.7, \(\widehat{\varphi_L}\) identifies with the application deduced from \(\varphi_L\). The formal morphism \(\widehat{\varphi}\) of 1.2.11 therefore induces on \(\mu_n\) an application which coincides with the application deduced from the algebraic application \(\varphi_L : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\).
1.2.2.13. Algebraicity of \(\widehat{\varphi}\).
The formal application \(\widehat{\varphi} : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\) of 1.2.11 induces, for \(\ell\) invertible on \(S\), applications
\[\varphi_\ell : T_\ell(\widehat{\mathbb{G}_m}) = T_\ell(\mathbb{G}_m) \to T_\ell(G) \;.\]

According to (1.2.2.12) and (EGA II 7.1.9), for any point \(t\) of \(S\), there exists an algebraic morphism \(\varphi_t : \mathbb{G}_{m_t} \to G_t\) such that \(T_\ell(\varphi_t) = T_\ell(\varphi)_t\). According to (SGA 3 XV 4.1 bis) \(\widehat{\varphi}\) is therefore algebraizable.

1.2.14. Let's prove 1.2.2. By passing to the limit, we return to the \(S\) Noetherian case, then \(S\) local Noetherian affine algebra \(A\). Let \(s\) be a section of \(p\). Let \(K\) be a \(G\)-torsor on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon)\). On \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{A})\), there exists one and only one homomorphism \(\varphi' : \mathbb{G}_{m,S} \to G_S\), such that the reciprocal image \(K'\) of \(K\) on \(\mathbb{P}(\varepsilon) \times_S S'\) is of the form \(K(K_0', \varphi')\). This homomorphism \(\varphi'\) descends to \(\varphi : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\). To prove the essential surjectivity of functor 1.2.1, it is possible to replace \(K\) by \(K - \varphi\Theta(1)\), i.e. to assume that \(\varphi = 0\). On \(S'\), there then exists one and only one isomorphism \(\psi : p'^*s^*K' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } K'\), inducing the identity on the section \(s\). This morphism goes down to \(S\) (fpqc descent of fppf torsor morphisms), and this proves 1.2.2.

blocks.title: Variants
blocks.html:
Variants 1.2.15

The conclusion of 1.2.2 is still verified in the following cases.

(i) \(G\) is defined by a quasi-consistent Module on \(S\).

(ii) \(G\) is the reciprocal image of a torsion beam on the small flat site of \(S\).
In case (i), the arrows (1.2.2.1) are isomorphisms according to the base change theorem for a proper morphism and the simple connectedness of \(\mathbb{P}^r_k\) for \(k\) algebraically closed.
In case (ii), that the arrows (1.2.2.1) are isomorphisms is standard, given that the fppf cohomology of \(G\) coincides with its cohomology for the ital topology.
1.3. Integration of torsors.
1.3.1 Let \(S\) be a diagram and \(G\) be an abelian beam on the large fppf site of \(S\), satisfying the following 3 conditions.
\((1.3.1.1)\) (i) For any projective space \(p : \mathbb{P}_S \to S\), with \(r \geqslant 1\), we have, in the large fppf site of \(S\) (XVII 0.10)
\[G \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } p_*p^*G \quad\text{et}\quad \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{G}_m, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^1 p_*p^*G \;.\]

(ii) For all \(n\) and all \(S\)-smooth affine morphism \(f : X \to T\), if \(K_1\) and \(K_2\) are two \(G\)-torsors on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\), the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(K_1, K_2)\) is identified with the set of symmetric morphisms of the reciprocal image of \(K_1\) on \((X/T)^n\) in the reciprocal image of \(K_2\) on \((X/T)^n\).
This condition is equivalent to the conjunction of:
(ii') \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X), G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}_S((X/T)^n, G)^{\mathfrak{S}_n}\), and
(ii") If the inverse image on \((X/T)^n\) of a torso \(K\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) admits a symmetric section, then \(K\) is trivial.
(iii) \(G\) checks either the condition (XVII 6.3.21.1), or the conditions (XVII 6.3.24.1) and (XVII 6.3.24.2).
The conclusion of 1.2.2 is therefore verified (see 1.2.2.1), as well as form XVII 6.3.26.

Condition (i) is discussed in 1.2.2 and 1.2.15. Condition (ii) is verified as soon as \(G\) is representable of finite presentation, where reciprocal image of a beam on the small flat site of \(S\), or quasi-coherent flat. Condition 1.3.1.1 is therefore verified in each of the following cases.
\((1.3.1.2)\) (a) \(G\) is smooth of finite presentation (1.2.2 and XVII 6.3.21.1).
(b) \(G\) is the reciprocal image of a torsion beam on the small flat site of \(S\) (1.2.15 (ii) and XVII 6.3.21.1).
(c) \(G\) is affine, and kernel of an epimorphism of smooth groups (1.2.2 and XVII 6.3.3.1).
(d) \(G\) is defined by a flat quasi-coherent beam on \(S\) (1.2.15 (i) and XVII 6.3.21.1).

1.3.2. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a projective and smooth curve on a diagram \(S\), with non-empty connected geometric fibers. Let \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(X\) "sufficiently" relatively ample in the following sense.
\((1.3.2.1)\) For any geometric point \(s\) of \(S\), if \(X_s\) is of kind \(g\), then
\[\deg_{X_s}(\mathcal{L}) > 2g - 2 \;,\]
\[\deg_{X_s}(\mathcal{L}) \geqslant 1 \text{ si } g = 0, \text{ et } \deg_{X_s}(\mathcal{L}) \geqslant 2 \text{ si } g = 1 \;.\]

We then have \(R^1 f_*\mathcal{L} = 0\), and \(f_*\mathcal{L}\) is locally free of formation compatible with any change of base, and everywhere of rank \(\geqslant 2\). If a section \(s\) of \(f_*\mathcal{L}\) does not vanish at any point, the scheme \(D_s\) of the zeros of \(s\), considered as a section of \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\), is a relative divisor (EGA IV 21.15.2), finite locally free on \(S\) since \(X/S\) is of relative dimension 1. Canonically we have
\[s : \mathcal{O}(D_s) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L} \;.\]

The divisor \(D_s\) only depends on \(s\) when multiplied by a section up to \(\mathcal{O}_S^\times\). The previous construction therefore provides us with a relative divisor \(Z_\mathcal{L}\) on the diagram deduced from \(X\) by the change of base
\[p : \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L})^\vee) \to S \;.\]

Let \(K\) be a torso under \(G_X\), and let \(K|Z_\mathcal{L}\) be its reciprocal image on \(Z_\mathcal{L}\). The trace of this torso (XVII 6.3.26), from \(Z_\mathcal{L}\) to \(\mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L})^\vee)\) is a torso \(K_\mathcal{L}\) on \(\mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L})^\vee)\):
\[K_\mathcal{L} = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z_\mathcal{L}/\mathbb{P}}(K|Z_\mathcal{L}) \;.\]

By virtue of 1.2.2, there exists a triple, unique up to a unique isomorphism, formed of a \(G\)-torsor \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) on \(S\), a morphism \(\varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K} : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\) and an isomorphism
\[K_\mathcal{L} \simeq p^*\langle\mathcal{L}, K] + \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K}\Theta(1) \;.\]
1.3.3. If we note additively the composition of the torsors, we canonically have
\[(1.3.3.1)\qquad \langle\mathcal{L}, K_1 + K_2] = \langle\mathcal{L}, K_1] + \langle\mathcal{L}, K_2]\]
\[(1.3.3.2)\qquad \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K_1 + K_2} = \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K_1} + \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K_2} \;.\]

Let \(\mathcal{L}_1\) and \(\mathcal{L}_2\) be two invertible beams on \(X\) satisfying (1.3.2.1), and \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2\). The tensor product of the sections defines a morphism

\[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=2.4em{ & \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L}_1)^\vee) \times_S \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L}_2)^\vee) \ar[rr]^-{\pi} \ar[dl]_{\operatorname{pr}_1} \ar[d]|{\operatorname{pr}_2} \ar@/^3pc/[dd]|(0.65){p_{12}} & & \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L})^\vee) \ar[dd]^{p} \\ \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L}_1)^\vee) \ar[dr]_{p_1} & \mathbb{P}(f_*(\mathcal{L}_2)^\vee) \ar[d]|{p_2} & & \\ & S \ar@{=}[rr] & & S }\]
We have \(\pi^*Z \simeq Z_{\mathcal{L}_1} + Z_{\mathcal{L}_2}\), hence an isomorphism (XVII 6.3.27.1)
\[(1.3.3.3)\qquad \pi^*K_\mathcal{L} \simeq \operatorname{pr}_1^*K_{\mathcal{L}_1} + \operatorname{pr}_2^*K_{\mathcal{L}_2} \;.\]
We have a canonical isomorphism
\[\pi^*\Theta(1) \simeq \operatorname{pr}_1^*\Theta(1) \otimes \operatorname{pr}_2^*\Theta(1) \;,\]
so that (1.3.3.3) is rewritten

\[\begin{aligned} \pi^*(p^*\langle\mathcal{L}, K] + \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K}\Theta(1)) &\simeq p_{12}^*\langle\mathcal{L}, K] + \operatorname{pr}_1^*\varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K}\Theta(1) + \operatorname{pr}_2^*\varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K}\Theta(1) \\ &\simeq p_{12}^*\langle\mathcal{L}_1, K] + p_{12}^*\langle\mathcal{L}_2, K] + \operatorname{pr}_1^*\varphi_{\mathcal{L}_1, K}\Theta(1) + \operatorname{pr}_2^*\varphi_{\mathcal{L}_2, K}\Theta(1) \;. \end{aligned}\]
We deduce, by a double application of 1.2.2, a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.3.3.4)\qquad \langle\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2, K] \simeq \langle\mathcal{L}_1, K] + \langle\mathcal{L}_2, K]\]
and an identity
\[\varphi_{\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2, K} = \varphi_{\mathcal{L}_1, K} = \varphi_{\mathcal{L}_2, K} \;.\]
We therefore see that \(\varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K}\) only depends on \(K\), and we set
\[(1.3.3.5)\qquad \deg(K) = \varphi_{\mathcal{L}, K} \;.\]
So we have
\[(1.3.3.6)\qquad K_\mathcal{L} \simeq p^*\langle\mathcal{L}, K] + \deg(K)\Theta(1) \;.\]

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a locally invertible beam direct factor \(f_*\mathcal{L}\), defining a section \(u\) of \(\mathbb{P}((f_*\mathcal{L})^\vee)\). Let \(D_u\) be the relative divisor defined by the invertible local sections of \(\mathcal{U}\).
\[u_S^*\Theta(1) \simeq \mathcal{U}_S^\vee\]
and we therefore deduce from 1.3.3.6 that
\[(1.3.3.8)\qquad u^*K_\mathcal{L} = \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K) \simeq \langle\mathcal{L}, K] - \deg(K)\mathcal{U} \;.\]
We leave it to the reader to check compatibility:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3.4

The following diagram of isomorphisms 1.3.3.1 and 1.3.3.4 is commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=2.6em{ \langle\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2, K_1 + K_2] \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar@{=}[d] & \langle\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2, K_1] + \langle\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2, K_2] \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ \langle\mathcal{L}_1, K_1 + K_2] + \langle\mathcal{L}_2, K_1 + K_2] \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \langle\mathcal{L}_1, K_1] + \langle\mathcal{L}_2, K_1] + \langle\mathcal{L}_1, K_2] + \langle\mathcal{L}_2, K_2] }\]

The isomorphisms (1.3.3.1) and (1.3.3.4) are also compatible with the isomorphisms of associativity and commutativity in a sense that we leave to the reader to explain. The formula (1.3.3.4) then allows the definition of \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) to be extended by "linearity" when \(\mathcal{L}\) no longer necessarily satisfies the condition (1.3.2.1).
1.3.5. For any relative divisor \(D\) on \(X\), we set
\[(1.3.5.0)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K) = \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K|D) \;.\]

If \(\mathcal{O}(D)\) satisfies (1.3.2.1), section 1 of \(\mathcal{O}(D)\) is a section of \(f_*\mathcal{O}(D)\), and as such generates an invertible beam locally direct factor of \(f_*\mathcal{O}(D)\), of which it defines a trivialization. Isomorphism 1.3.3.8 therefore provides us with an isomorphism
\[(1.3.5.1)\qquad \langle\mathcal{O}(D), K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K) \;.\]
We immediately check that the diagrams

\[(1.3.5.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2em{ \langle\mathcal{O}(D), K_1 + K_2] \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]^{\wr} & \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K_1 + K_2) \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(D), K_1] + \langle\mathcal{O}(D), K_2] \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K_1) + \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K_2) }\]
and

\[(1.3.5.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2em{ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_1 + D_2), K] \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]^{\wr} & \operatorname{Tr}_{D_1 + D_2/S}(K) \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_1), K] + \langle\mathcal{O}(D_2), K] \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \operatorname{Tr}_{D_1/S}(K) + \operatorname{Tr}_{D_2/S}(K) }\]
(XVII 6.3.27.1) are commutative. This allows, by linearity, to define the isomorphism (1.3.5.1) for any relative divisor.
1.3.6. Let \(D\) and \(E\) be two effective relative Cartier divisors, and let \(f\) be a rational function such that
\[\operatorname{div}(f) = D - E \;,\]
i.e. an isomorphism between \(\mathcal{O}(D)\) and \(\mathcal{O}(E)\). The isomorphism \(f\) between \(\mathcal{O}(D)\) and \(\mathcal{O}(E)\) defines, by (1.3.5.1), an isomorphism
\[(1.3.6.1)\qquad \langle f, K] : \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K) \;.\]
We have, by 1.3.5.2 for the second formula:
\[\langle fg, K] = \langle f, K].\langle g, K]\]
\[\langle f, K_1 + K_2] = \langle f, K_1] + \langle f, K_2] \;.\]

If \(\lambda\) is a section of \(\mathcal{O}_S^\times\), then, for any invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) over \(X\), multiplication by \(\lambda\) is an automorphism of \(\mathcal{L}\). By structure transport, this automorphism induces automorphisms on the two members of 1.3.3.8 which are deduced from each other via the isomorphism 1.3.3.8. We deduce that
\[\langle\lambda, K] = \deg(K)(\lambda) \;,\]
if \(\deg(K) \neq 0\), the isomorphism \(\langle f, K]\) between \(\operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K)\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K)\) therefore depends on the choice of \(f\).

Let \(\lambda \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S^\times)\), \(g \in \Gamma(S, G)\), \(\mathcal{L}\) be an invertible beam on \(X\) and \(K\) be a \(G\)-torsor on \(X\). If \(\langle\lambda, g]\) is the automorphism of \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) deduced by structure transport from the automorphisms \(\lambda\) of \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(g\) of \(K\), then, \(\langle\lambda, g]\), identified at a section of \(G\), is given by \(\langle\lambda, g] = \langle\lambda, K] + \langle\mathcal{L}, g]\); since \(\langle\mathcal{O}(D), g] = \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(g)\), we draw from the previous formula that
\[(1.3.6.2)\qquad \langle\lambda, g] = \deg(K)(\lambda) + \deg(\mathcal{L}).g \;.\]

1.3.7. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a projective and smooth curve on \(S\) and \(G\) be a group on \(S\) satisfying 1.3.1.1. If \(X \xrightarrow{f_0} T \xrightarrow{\pi} S\) is the Stein factorization of \(f\), then \(\pi\) is a flat cover of \(S\) and the beam \(f_*\mathbb{G}_m\) is the torus \(\prod_{T/S}\mathbb{G}_m\). Locally on \(S\) (for the flat topology), \(X\) is the sum of a family of curves \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) on \(S\) with non-empty connected geometric fibers. If \(K\) is a torsor under \(G\) and \(\mathcal{L}\) an invertible beam on \(X\), we then set
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, K] = \sum_i \langle\mathcal{L}|X_i, K|X_i]\]

and we define the degree of \(K : f_*\mathbb{G}_m = \mathbb{G}_m^I \to G\) as having the coordinates \(\deg(K|X_i) : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\). Local constructions are globalized and provide functors and morphisms
\[(1.3.7.1)\qquad \langle\mathcal{L}, K]_{X/S} = \operatorname{Tr}_{T/S}(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]_{X/T})\]
\[(1.3.7.2)\qquad \deg(K) : f_*\mathbb{G}_m = \prod_{T/S}\mathbb{G}_m \to G : \deg_{X/S}(K) = \prod_{T/S} \deg_{X/T}(K) \;.\]
For \(\lambda\) an automorphism of \(\mathcal{L}\), defined by an element \(\lambda_0\) of \(\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T^\times)\), we have
\[(1.3.7.3)\qquad \langle\lambda, K] = \deg(K)(\lambda) \;.\]
We define the total degree of \(K\) as the compound
\[\operatorname{deg\,tot}(K) : \mathbb{G}_m \to f_*\mathbb{G}_m \xrightarrow{\ \deg(K)\ } G \;.\]
The preceding results provide:

Form 1.3.8.: For any projective and smooth curve \(f : X \to S\), we have a functor \(\langle\ ,\ ]\), of formation compatible with any change of base, which has an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and a torsor \(K\) under \(f^*G\) associates a torso \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) under \(G\) on \(S\). This functor has the following additional structures and satisfies the following conditions:

(1.3.8.1) \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) is biadditive in \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(K\); biadditivity isomorphisms are compatible with associativity and commutativity isomorphisms, base changes, check compatibility (1.3.4) and are compatible with isomorphisms (1.3.8.2) below (via XVII 6.3.25.3 and XVII 6.3.27.1).
(1.3.8.2) We have, for any effective relative divisor \(E\) on \(X/S\), a canonical isomorphism
\[\langle\mathcal{O}(E), K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K|E) \;.\]
(1.3.8.3) Degree 1.3.7.2 checks for \(\lambda \in f_*\mathbb{G}_m\) automorphism of \(\mathcal{L}\)
\[\langle\lambda, K] = \deg(K)(\lambda) \;.\]
For \(\lambda \in \mathbb{G}_m(S)\), the total degree checks
\[\langle\lambda, K] = \operatorname{deg\,tot}(K)(\lambda) \;.\]
Similarly, for \(g \in G(S)\) we have \(\langle\mathcal{L}, g] = \deg(\mathcal{L}).g\).
We deduce a canonical isomorphism:
\[(1.3.8.4)\qquad \langle f^*\mathcal{L}, K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{deg\,tot}(K)(\mathcal{L}) \;.\]
It immediately follows from the definitions that for any homomorphism \(\Psi : G \to G'\), we have a canonical isomorphism, compatible with the preceding data:
\[(1.3.8.5)\qquad \langle\mathcal{L}, \Psi(K)] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Psi(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]) \;.\]
(1.3.8.6) Let \(u : X \to Y\) be a flat morphism between projective and smooth curves on \(S\). We then have isomorphisms compatible with biadditivity and base changes:
— For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(X\) and \(K\) a \(G_Y\)-torsor on \(Y\), we have
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, u^*K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle N_{X/Y}(\mathcal{L}), K] \;.\]
— For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(Y\) and \(K\) a \(G_X\)-torsor on \(X\), we have
\[\langle u^*\mathcal{L}, K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{L}, \operatorname{Tr}_{X/Y}K] \;.\]
Moreover, these isomorphisms verify an obvious compatibility for a compound \(uv\) of curve morphisms; for \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}(E)\), they identify with isomorphisms (XVII 6.3.27.2)
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(u^*K) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Tr}_{u_*E/S}(K)\]
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{u^*E/S}(K) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S} \operatorname{Tr}_{X/Y}K \;.\]

Remains to prove 1.3.8.6. We return to the case \(X\) and \(Y\) with non-empty connected geometric fibers and we note that the explicit formulas which precede provide, for each section \(s\) of \(\mathcal{L}\), which does not cancel as a section of the direct image of \(\mathcal{L}\), an isomorphism \(\Psi_s\) of the desired type, homogeneous in \(s\), and therefore independent of \(s\) for \(\mathcal{L}\) verifying (1.3.2.1). The general case follows from this.

1.3.9. Under hypotheses 1.3.7, we saw (1.3.8) that every \(G\)-torsor \(K\) on \(X\) defines a functor \(\langle\times, K]\), designated below by \(F(\times)\), provided with the following additional data and checking the following conditions:
(i) \(F\) is an fppf field morphism on \(S\) (XVII 0.10 and Giraud [1])
— from source the field whose \(T\)-objects, for \(T\) a \(S\)-schema, are the invertible beams on \(X_T\)
— of goal the field of \(G\)-torsors.

(ii) The functor \(F\) is provided with additivity isomorphisms \(F(\mathcal{L}_1 \otimes \mathcal{L}_2) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(\mathcal{L}_1) + F(\mathcal{L}_2)\), functorial, compatible with associativity and commutativity isomorphisms, and compatible with base changes.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.3.10

Under the assumptions of 1.3.7, the functor

\[\Phi : K \mapsto \langle\times, K]\]

is an equivalence between the category of \(G\)-torsors on \(X\) and the category of field morphisms considered in (1.3.9).
We easily return to the case where \(X\) has non-empty connected fibers.

Let \(F\) verifying 1.3.9 (i) and (ii). For any \(S\)-schema \(T\) and any section \(t\) of \(X_T/T\), let us again denote by \(t\) the relative divisor \(t(S)\). \(F(\mathcal{O}(t))\) is then a \(G_T\)-torsor on \(T\). We will designate by \(\Psi(F)\) the torsor thus obtained on \(X\), for \(t\) the "universal point" of \(X\), with value in \(X\), defined by the identical application of \(X\) in \(X\). For any section \(t\) as above, we have
\[(1.3.10.1)\qquad F(\mathcal{O}(t)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } t^*\Psi(F) \;.\]

Isomorphism 1.3.8.2 provides an isomorphism of functors \(\Psi \circ \Phi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Id}\). Let's construct an isomorphism \(\Phi \circ \Psi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Id}\).
For each section \(t\) from \(X_T\) to \(T\), the isomorphism (1.3.10.1) is an isomorphism
\[F(\mathcal{O}(t)) \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(t), \Psi(F)] \;.\]
By addition, each family \(t_1 \ldots t_n\) of sections of \(X_T\) on \(T\) defines an isomorphism
\[F(\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i)) \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i), \Psi(F)] \;,\]
and according to 1.3.9 (ii), this isomorphism does not depend on the order of \(t_i\).

Let's take \(T = (X/S)^n\) and for \(t_1 \ldots t_n\) the \(n\)-universal tuple of sections. If \(T' = \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\), we then have on \(X_T\) a divisor \(\sum t_i\), whose divisor \(\sum t_i\) on \(X_{T'}\) is a reciprocal image. On \(T\), \(\alpha\) is a symmetric isomorphism
\[\alpha : F(\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i), \Psi(F)]\]
between torsors reciprocal image of torsors on \(T'\). By hypothesis (1.3.1.1)(ii), this isomorphism comes from one and only one isomorphism on \(T'\)
\[\alpha : F(\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(\textstyle\sum t_i), \Psi(F)] \qquad (\text{sur } T') \;.\]
According to XVII 6.3., we thus have for any relative divisor \(D\) on \(X\) an isomorphism
\[(1.3.10.2)\qquad \alpha_D : F(\mathcal{O}(D)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \Psi(F)] \;,\]
additive in \(D\), and training compatible with any change of base.

Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sufficiently large invertible beam on \(X\) in sense 1.3.2.1. Each everywhere non-zero section \(s\) of \(f_*\mathcal{L}\) defines, with the notations of 1.3.2, an isomorphism
\[s : \mathcal{O}(D_s) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L} \;,\]
hence, by (1.3.10.2), an isomorphism
\[(1.3.10.3)\qquad \alpha_s : F(\mathcal{L}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{L}, \Psi(F)] \;.\]
Let us prove that this isomorphism does not depend on \(s\). Indeed:
(i) The formation of \(\alpha_s\) is compatible with any change of base
(ii) There exists a homomorphism \(\varphi : \mathbb{G}_m \to G\) such that
\[\alpha_{\lambda s} = \varphi(\lambda).\alpha_s\]
(according to the functoriality of the two members of (1.3.10.3)).

We necessarily have \(\varphi = 0\), otherwise the torso on \(\mathbb{P}((f_*\mathcal{L})^\vee)\) reciprocal image of \(F(\mathcal{L}) - \langle\mathcal{L}, \Psi(F)]\) would be of degree \(\neq 0\). The \(\alpha_s\) therefore define a morphism of \(\mathbb{P}((f_*\mathcal{L})^\vee)\) into \(G\), and the latter is constant by hypothesis ((1.3.1.1)(i)). We can therefore put \(\alpha_\mathcal{L} = \alpha_s\).

The isomorphisms \(\alpha_\mathcal{L}\) therefore form a functor isomorphism between the restrictions of the functors \(F\) and \(\langle\times, \Psi(F)]\) to sufficiently ample sheaves. This isomorphism is compatible with additivity isomorphisms, which allows it to be extended by linearity to all invertible sheaves and completes the construction of
\[\alpha : \Phi \circ \Psi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Id} \;.\]

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.3.11

: \(G = \mathbb{G}_m\). Under hypotheses 1.3.7, and in the particular case where \(G = \mathbb{G}_m\), we will write \(\langle\ ,\ \rangle\) rather than \(\langle\ ,\ ]\); if \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(\mathcal{U}\) are two invertible beams on \(X\), then \(\langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{U}\rangle\) is an invertible beam on \(S\). We have more isomorphisms (1.3.8.2)

\[\langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{U}\rangle \simeq N_{D/S}(\mathcal{U}) \;.\]
If \(D\) and \(E\) are disjoint effective relative divisors, then \(\mathcal{O}(E)|D\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\), hence an isomorphism
\[(1.3.11.1)\qquad \sigma_{D,E} : \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \simeq N_{D/S}(\mathcal{O}(E)|D) \simeq N_{D/S}(\mathcal{O}) \simeq \mathcal{O} \;.\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3.12

Let \((D_i)_{i=1,2}\) and \(E\) be effective relative divisors, with \(D_i\) disjoint from \(E\) (\(i = 1, 2\)). Let \(f\) be a rational function satisfying \(\operatorname{div}(f) = D_1 - D_2\), i.e. an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{O}(D_1) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{O}(D_2)\). The following isomorphism diagrams are commutative

\[(1.3.12.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_1), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\sigma_{D_1, E}} \ar[d]_{\langle f, \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle} & \mathcal{O} \ar[d]^{N_{E/S}(f)} \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_2), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[r]_-{\sigma_{D_2, E}} & \mathcal{O} }\]

\[(1.3.12.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{O}(D_1)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\sigma_{E, D_1}} \ar[d]_{\langle\mathcal{O}(E), f\rangle} & \mathcal{O} \ar[d]^{N_{E/S}(f)} \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{O}(D_2)\rangle \ar[r]_-{\sigma_{E, D_2}} & \mathcal{O} }\]
The formula (1.3.12.2) simply expresses the commutativity of the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ N_{E/S}(\mathcal{O}(D_1)) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{N_{E/S}(f)} & \mathcal{O} \ar[d]^{N_{E/S}(f)} \\ N_{E/S}(\mathcal{O}(D_2)) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \mathcal{O} }\]

For any \(S\)-scheme \(T\) and any invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X_T\), let us set \(F(\mathcal{L}) = N_{E_T/T}(\mathcal{L})\). The hypotheses of 1.3.10 are verified by this functor, so that there exists a unique invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) provided with a functor isomorphism compatible with biadditivity:
\[N_{E_T/T}(\mathcal{L}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle \;.\]
Moreover, if \(d : T \to X_T\) is a section of \(X\) on \(T\), we canonically have
\[d^*\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } N_{E_T/T}(\mathcal{O}(d)) \;.\]
Taking for \(d\) the universal section, of \(X\), \(\Delta : X \to X_X\), we find
\[\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } N_{E_X/X}(\mathcal{O}(\Delta)) \;.\]
Locally on \(S\), \(E\) is a sum of sections \(E = \sum t_i\) (XVII 6.3); for such \(E\), we have
\[N_{E_X/X}(\mathcal{O}(\Delta)) = \bigotimes t_i^*\mathcal{O}(\Delta) = \sum \mathcal{O}(t_i) = \mathcal{O}(E) \;,\]
and this isomorphism, being independent of the order of \(t_i\), defines a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.3.12.3)\qquad N_{E_X/X}(\mathcal{O}(\Delta)) \sim \mathcal{O}(E) \;,\]
so that
\[(1.3.12.4)\qquad N_{E_T/T}(\mathcal{L}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \;.\]
The isomorphism (1.3.12.3) is compatible with biadditivity, base changes and is characterized by the fact that, moreover, for any section \(d\) of \(X_T\) over \(T\), the isomorphism
\[N_{E/T}(\mathcal{O}(d)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(d), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } d^*\mathcal{O}(E)\]
is reciprocal image of the universal isomorphism (1.3.12.3). In particular, for \(d\) disjoint from \(E\), the diagram

\[\xymatrix{ N_{E/T}(\mathcal{O}(d)) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar@{=}[d] & \langle\mathcal{O}(d), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\sim} & d^*\mathcal{O}(E) \ar@{=}[d] \\ N_{E/T}(\mathcal{O}(d)) \ar@{=}[rr] & & d^*\mathcal{O}(E) }\]
is commutative. By addition, we deduce that for \(D\) disjoint from \(E\), the diagram

\[(1.3.12.5)\qquad \xymatrix{ N_{E/T}(\mathcal{O}(D)) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar@{=}[d] & \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\sim} & N_{D/T}(\mathcal{O}(E)) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \mathcal{O} \ar@{=}[rr] & & \mathcal{O} }\]
is commutative. The arrow \(\sigma_{E, D_1}\) is therefore composed of the inverse of 1.3.12.4 and an obvious arrow
\[\langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } N_{E/S}(\mathcal{O}(D)) \to \mathcal{O}\]
and 1.3.12.2 results immediately, since 1.3.12.4 is functional.
We deduce from 1.3.12 (cf. SERRE [1] Ch III prop 7 pg 46)

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.13

Let \((D_i)_{i=1,2}\) and \((E_i)_{i=1,2}\) be effective relative divisors, with \(D_i\) disjoint from \(E_j\), \(f\) a rational function satisfying \(\operatorname{div}(f) = D_1 - D_2\) and \(g\) a rational function satisfying \(\operatorname{div}(g) = E_1 - E_2\). We then have

\[N_{E_1/S}(f).N_{E_2/S}(f)^{-1} = N_{D_1/S}(g).N_{D_2/S}(g)^{-1}\]

or briefly

\[(1.3.13.1)\qquad f(\operatorname{div}(g)) = g(\operatorname{div}(f)) \;.\]
This formula, in the form
expresses, via (1.3.12.1) and (1.3.12.2) the commutativity of the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_1), \mathcal{O}(E_1)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\langle\mathcal{O}(D_1), g\rangle} \ar[d]_{\langle f, \mathcal{O}(E_1)\rangle} & \langle\mathcal{O}(D_1), \mathcal{O}(E_2)\rangle \ar[d]^{\langle f, \mathcal{O}(E_2)\rangle} \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(D_2), \mathcal{O}(E_1)\rangle \ar[r]_-{\langle\mathcal{O}(D_2), g\rangle} & \langle\mathcal{O}(D_2), \mathcal{O}(E_2)\rangle }\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3.14

For \(G = \mathbb{G}_m\), the degree (1.3.7.2) coincides with the usual degree of invertible beams.
Just compare the formulas (1.3.7.3) and (1.3.12.1) for \(f\) reciprocal image of \(f_0 \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S^\times)\).
1.3.15. Whatever \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(\mathcal{M}\), sufficiently ample, there exist locally on \(S\) disjoint divisors \(D\) and \(E\) and isomorphisms
\[\alpha : \mathcal{L} \to \mathcal{O}(D) \quad\text{et}\quad \beta : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{O}(E) \;.\]
Let us denote by \(\tau\) the isomorphism making the diagram commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle \ar[r]^-{\langle\alpha,\beta\rangle} \ar[d]_{\wr}^{\tau} & \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[r]^-{\sigma_{D,E}} & \mathcal{O} \ar@{=}[d] \\ \langle\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \ar[r]_-{\langle\beta,\alpha\rangle} & \langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{O}(D)\rangle \ar[r]_-{\sigma_{E,D}} & \mathcal{O} }\]
It follows from 1.3.12 that this isomorphism does not depend on the particular choice of \(D, E\), \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\); it is therefore globalized into a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.3.15.1)\qquad \tau : \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \;.\]
This isomorphism verifies \(\tau^2 = 1\); it is compatible with biadditivity, since (1.3.11.1) is, and extends by linearity to arbitrary invertible sheaves.
It follows from (1.3.12.5) that the morphism (1.3.12.4) is identified with the compound
\[N_{E_T/T}(\mathcal{L}) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{L}\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \tau\ } \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle\]
(check it for \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}(D)\) with \(D\) disjoint from \(E\)).

Form 1.3.16. For any projective and smooth curve \(f : X \to S\), we have a functor \(\langle\ ,\ \rangle\), of formation compatible with any change of base, which to two invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(\mathcal{M}\) on \(X\) associates a reversing beam \(\langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle\) to \(S\). This functor has the following additional structures and satisfies the following conditions:
1.3.16.1. With the notations of 1.3.8., for \(G = \mathbb{G}_m\), we have
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle = \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}] \;,\]

in particular, \(\langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle\) is biadditivity in \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) in the sense specified in 1.3.8.1 and this biadditivity is compatible with that of the second member of a canonical isomorphism (functional in \(\mathcal{M}\))
\[\langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{M}\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } N_{E/S}(\mathcal{M}) \;.\]
1.3.16.2. We have a symmetry isomorphism
\[\tau : \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \;,\]

functional in \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(\mathcal{M}\), compatible with base changes, biadditivity isomorphisms and satisfying \(\tau^2 = 1\). It makes the following diagram commutative, for \(D\) and \(E\) disjoint divisors:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=2.4em{ N_{D/S}(\mathcal{O}(E)) \ar[d]_{\wr} & \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \ar[l] \ar[rr]^-{\tau} & & \langle\mathcal{O}(E), \mathcal{O}(D)\rangle \ar[r] & N_{E/S}(\mathcal{O}(D)) \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ \mathcal{O} \ar@{=}[rrrr] & & & & \mathcal{O} }\]
These formulas involve the formulas (1.3.12.1) (1.3.12.2) (1.3.13.1) and

(1.3.16.3) For \(a, b \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S^\times)\) we have, denoting by \(\langle a, b\rangle\) the automorphism of \(\langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle\), identified with a section of \(\mathbb{G}_m\), deduced by functoriality of the automorphisms \(a\) and \(b\) of \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(\mathcal{M}\),
\[\langle a, b\rangle = a^{\deg(\mathcal{M})}\, b^{\deg(\mathcal{L})} \;.\]
(1.3.16.4) We have, if \(\mathcal{L}_0\) (resp. \(\mathcal{M}_0\)) is an invertible Module on \(S\), a canonical isomorphism
\[\langle f^*\mathcal{L}_0, \mathcal{M}\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}_0^{\otimes\deg(\mathcal{M})}\]
\[(\text{resp.} \qquad \langle\mathcal{L}, f^*\mathcal{M}_0\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{M}_0^{\otimes\deg(\mathcal{L})} \qquad) \;.\]

1.3.16.5. If \(u : X \to Y\) is a finite and flat morphism of projective and smooth curves on \(S\), then, for \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(X\) and \(\mathcal{M}\) invertible beam on \(Y\), isomorphisms 1.3.8.6
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, u^*\mathcal{M}\rangle \sim \langle N_{X/Y}\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\rangle\]
\[\langle u^*\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \sim \langle\mathcal{M}, N_{X/Y}\mathcal{L}\rangle\]
are deduced from each other by symmetry (1.3.16.2) (see it for \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}(E)\) and \(\mathcal{M} = \mathcal{O}(F)\) with \(E\) and \(F\) disjoint).
1.3.16.6. For \(\mathcal{L}\) an invertible beam on \(X\), the symmetry arrow
\[\tau : \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \to \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}\rangle\]
is multiplication by \((-1)^{\deg(\mathcal{L})}\).
1.3.17. It remains to prove 1.3.16.6. We will give two demonstrations, the first making use of the theory of the determinant (SGA 6 XI).
1st proof: Let \(u : T \to S\) be a locally free finite morphism. If \(\mathcal{L}\) is an invertible beam on \(T\), we have an isomorphism
\[(1.3.17.1)\qquad N_{T/S}(\mathcal{L}) \simeq \det(u_*\mathcal{L})\, \det(u_*\mathcal{O}_T)^{-1}\]
only determined by the following three conditions:
a) its formation is compatible with basic changes;
b) for \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_T\), it is the identical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\);
c) it is functorial in \(\mathcal{L}\).
Let \(D\) and \(E\) be two effective relative divisors on \(X\), \(i\) the inclusion of \(D\) in \(X\) and \(u\) the projection of \(D\) on \(S\). The short exact sequel
\[0 \to \mathcal{O}(-E) \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}_E \to 0\]
allows you to interpret isomorphism
\[\langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(-E)\rangle \simeq N_{D/S}(\mathcal{O}(-E)) \simeq \det(u_*i^*\mathcal{O}(-E))\, \det(u_*i^*\mathcal{O})^{-1}\]
like an isomorphism
\[\langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(-E)\rangle \simeq \det Ru_*(Li^*\mathcal{O}_E)^{-1} \simeq \det Rf_*(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_E)^{-1} \;.\]
or
\[(1.3.17.2)\qquad \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle \simeq \det Rf_*(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_E) \;.\]

For \(D\) and \(E\) disjoint, we have \(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_E = \mathcal{O}\), and the isomorphism of \(\langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(E)\rangle\) with \(\mathcal{O}_S\) which is deduced from it is the one already considered. We deduce that, for disjoint \(D\) and \(E\), the symmetry \(\tau\) and the symmetry isomorphism \(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_E \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{O}_E \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_D\) are compatible via (1.3.17.2). By specialization, this remains true regardless of \(D\) and \(E\).
When \(D = E\) is defined by an ideal \(I\), we have

\[\begin{aligned} \underline{H}^0(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_D) &= \mathcal{O}_D && \text{symétrie} = +1 \\ \underline{H}^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_D) &= I/I^2 && \text{symétrie} = -1 \\ \underline{H}^i(\mathcal{O}_D \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathcal{O}_D) &= 0 && \text{pour } i \neq 0, -1 \;. \end{aligned}\]
From then on, the arrow
\[\tau : \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(D)\rangle \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\mathcal{O}(D), \mathcal{O}(D)\rangle\]
and 1.3.16.6 results.

2nd demonstration: By reduction to a "universal" case, we can assume that \(S\) is reduced. This case reduces to that where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed body \(k\). Let in this case \(X_1\), \(X_2\), \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\) be four divisors on \(X\), \(f\) and \(g\) two rational functions, and suppose that
\[\operatorname{div}(f) = X_2 - X_1\]
\[\operatorname{div}(g) = Y_2 - Y_1\]
\[(X_1 \cup Y_2) \cap (X_2 \cup Y_1) = \varnothing \;.\]
The applications \(f\) and \(g\) then define
\[\langle f, g\rangle : \langle\mathcal{O}(X_1), \mathcal{O}(Y_1)\rangle \to \langle\mathcal{O}(X_2), \mathcal{O}(Y_2)\rangle \;.\]
Both members are canonically isomorphic to \(k\) (1.3.11.1), so that \(\langle f, g\rangle\) identifies with an element of \(k^\times\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.18

We have

\[\langle f, g\rangle = \prod_{x \in X_1 \cup Y_2} (-1)^{v_x(f) v_x(g)} \left(g^{v_x(f)}/f^{v_x(g)}\right)(x) \;.\]

Let us show that (1.3.18) entails (1.3.16.6). We can assume that \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{O}(X) \sim \mathcal{O}(Y)\) with \(X\) and \(Y\) effective, and that \(X \cap Y = \varnothing\). Let \(f\) be a function such as \(\operatorname{div}(f) = Y - X\). The commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \ar[r]^-{\tau} \ar@{=}[d] & \langle\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}\rangle \ar@{=}[d] \\ \langle\mathcal{O}(X), \mathcal{O}(Y)\rangle \ar[r]_-{\langle f, f^{-1}\rangle} & \langle\mathcal{O}(Y), \mathcal{O}(X)\rangle }\]
shows that
\[\tau = \langle f, f^{-1}\rangle = \prod_{x \in X} (-1)^{v_x(f)^2} = (-1)^{\deg(\mathcal{L})} \;.\]
Let's prove 1.3.18. We come back to assuming \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\) very ample. There are then factorizations \(f = f_1 f_2\); \(g = g_1 g_2\) with
\[\begin{aligned} \operatorname{div}(f_1) &= X' - X_1 & \operatorname{div}(g_1) &= Y' - Y_1 \\ \operatorname{div}(f_2) &= X_2 - X' & \operatorname{div}(g_2) &= Y_2 - Y' \;, \end{aligned}\]
the divisors \(X'\), \(Y'\) and \(X_1 \cup X_2\), \(Y_1 \cup Y_2\) being disjoint.
Let us designate by \(\langle\ ,\ \rangle^\times\) the second member of (1.3.18) and prove that
\[\langle f, g\rangle^\times = \langle f_1, g_1\rangle^\times \cdot \langle f_2, g_2\rangle^\times \;.\]
According to Serre [1], we have
We only get out of it
\[\langle f_1, g_1\rangle^\times \cdot \langle f_2, g_2\rangle^\times = g_1(X_1)\, f_1(Y')\, g_2(X')\, f_2(Y_2) \;.\]
\[\prod_{X_1 \cup X' \cup Y' \cup Y_2} (-1)^{v_x(f_1) v_x(g_2)} \left(f_1^{v_x(g_2)} \cdot f_2^{-v_x(f_1)}\right)(x)\]
\[\prod_{X_1 \cup Y_2} (-1)^{v_x(f) v_x(g)} \left((f_1 f_2)^{v_x(g_2)} \cdot (g_1 g_2)^{-v_x(f_1)}\right)(x) = \langle f_1 f_2, g_1 g_2\rangle^\times \;.\]

We have by functoriality of \(\langle\ ,\ \rangle\) that \(\langle f, g\rangle = \langle f_1, g_1\rangle\langle f_2, g_2\rangle\), and we easily verify that \(\langle f_i, g_i\rangle = \langle f_i, g_i\rangle^\times\). The lemma results.
1.4. Strictly commutative Picard fields.
Some "general topology" results will be necessary to express the results of 1.3 by a "universal coefficient formula".
The results of this issue are suggested in a letter from A. Grothendieck addressed to J.L. Verdier (1966).
The notions of associativity and commutativity for bifunctors (see below) were introduced by MAC LANE.
1.4.1. Let \(C\) be a category, \(F : C \times C \to C\) be a functor and
\[\sigma : F(F(X,Y), Z) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(X, F(Y,Z))\]
an isomorphism of trifunctors. We say that the pair \((F, \sigma)\) is an associative functor, or that \(\sigma\) is associativity data on \(F\) if the following condition is verified

(Ass) Whatever the family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(C\), and designating by \(e : I \to M(I)\) the canonical application of \(I\) in the free monoid (without unit) generated by \(I\), there exists an application \(\underline{F} : M(I) \to \operatorname{Ob}C\), of isomorphisms \(a_i : \underline{F}(e_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X_i\) and isomorphisms \(a_{g,h} : \underline{F}(gh) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(\underline{F}(g), \underline{F}(h))\) such as diagrams

\[(1.4.1.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=1.2em{ \underline{F}(f(gh)) \ar[r]^-{a_{f,gh}} \ar@{=}[d] & F(\underline{F}(f), \underline{F}(gh)) \ar[r]^-{a_{g,h}} & F(\underline{F}(f), F(\underline{F}(g), \underline{F}(h))) \\ \underline{F}((fg)h) \ar[r]_-{a_{fg,h}} & F(\underline{F}(fg), \underline{F}(h)) \ar[r]_-{a_{f,g}} & F(F(\underline{F}(f), \underline{F}(g)), \underline{F}(h)) \ar[u]_{\sigma} }\]
be commutative.
We will not have to make use of the fact that the axiom (Ass) is equivalent to the "pentagon axiom", such that the following diagram is commutative
\[\xymatrix@R=2em@C=1em{ & F(F(X,Y), F(Z,T)) \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ F(X, F(Y, F(Z,T))) & & F(F(F(X,Y),Z), T) \ar[d] \\ F(X, F(F(Y,Z), T)) \ar[u] & & F(F(X, F(Y,Z)), T) \ar[ll] }\]
Let us now be given functorial isomorphisms
\[\sigma_{X,Y,Z} : F(F(X,Y), Z) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(X, F(Y,Z))\]
\[\tau_{X,Y} : F(X,Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(Y,X) \;.\]
We will say that \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\) make \(F\) an associative and strictly commutative functor if the following condition is verified.

(Ass.s.Com) Whatever the family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(C\), and designating by \(e : I \to N(I)\) the canonical application of \(I\) in the free abelian monoid (without unit) generated by \(I\), there exists an application \(\underline{F} : N(I) \to \operatorname{Ob}C\), isomorphisms \(a_i : \underline{F}(e_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X_i\) and isomorphisms \(a_{g,h} : \underline{F}(gh) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(\underline{F}(g), \underline{F}(h))\) such that the diagram (1.4.1.1) is commutative, as well as the diagram

\[(1.4.1.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2em{ \underline{F}(gh) \ar[r]^-{a_{g,h}} \ar@{=}[d] & F(\underline{F}(g), \underline{F}(h)) \ar[d]^{\tau} \\ \underline{F}(hg) \ar[r]_-{a_{h,g}} & F(\underline{F}(h), \underline{F}(g)) }\]
We will not have to make use of this as this axiom is equivalent to the conjunction of
1) the pentagon axiom
2) \(\tau_{X,X} : X + X \to X + X\) is the identity
3) \(\tau_{Y,X} \circ \tau_{X,Y} : X + Y \to Y + X \to X + Y\) is the identity
4) the hexagon axiom: the diagram

\[\xymatrix@R=1.8em@C=0.5em{ & X + (Y + Z) \ar[dr]^{\sigma} & \\ (X + Y) + Z \ar[ur]^{\sigma} \ar[d]_{\tau} & & X + (Z + Y) \ar[d]^{\sigma} \\ Z + (X + Y) \ar[dr]_{\sigma} & & (X + Z) + Y \ar[dl]^{\tau} \\ & (Z + X) + Y & }\]
is commutative.
Please note that condition 2) is not very often verified in practice (hence the strictly commutative terminology).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.2

A strictly commutative Picard category \(\mathscr{P}\) is a non-empty category whose morphisms are all isomorphisms, provided with a functor \(+ : \mathscr{P} \times \mathscr{P} \to \mathscr{P} : (X, Y) \mapsto X + Y\) and functorial isomorphisms

\[\sigma : (X + Y) + Z \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X + (Y + Z)\]

\[\tau : X + Y \to Y + X\]

making \(+\) an associative and strictly commutative functor, and such that for all \(X \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}\), the functor \(Y \mapsto X + Y\) is a category equivalence.
The reader will verify that

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.3

If \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) is a family of objects of a strictly commutative Picard category \(\mathscr{P}\), there exists a map \(\Sigma : \mathbb{Z}^{(I)} \to \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}\), isomorphisms \(a_i : \Sigma(e_i) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X_i\) and \(a_{\underline{n},\underline{m}} : \Sigma(\underline{n} + \underline{m}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Sigma(\underline{n}) + \Sigma(\underline{m})\) such that diagrams of the type (1.4.1.1) and (1.4.1.2) are commutative. The system \((\Sigma, (a_i), (a_{\underline{n},\underline{m}}))\) is unique up to unique isomorphism, and is functorial in \((X_i)_{i \in I}\).

1.4.4 A strictly commutative Picard category admits up to a unique isomorphism of one and only one neutral object, which we can here define as a pair \((e, \varphi)\) formed of an object \(e\) and an isomorphism \(\varphi : e + e \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } e\). If \((e, \varphi)\) is a neutral object, there exists one and only one isomorphism of functors
\[\alpha_s : e + X \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X\]
making the diagram commutative
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ e + (e + X) \ar[d]_{e + \alpha_s} & (e + e) + X \ar[l]_-{\sigma} \ar[d]^{\varphi} \\ e + X \ar@{=}[r] & e + X }\]

We similarly define \(\alpha_d : X + e \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X\), and \(\varphi\) is a special case of both \(\alpha_s\) and \(\alpha_d\); \(\tau\) exchanges \(\alpha_s\) and \(\alpha_d\). The group \(\operatorname{Aut}(e)\) is abelian, and for all \(X \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}\), the functors \(X +\) and \(+ X\) establish the same isomorphism between \(\operatorname{Aut}(e)\) and \(\operatorname{Aut}(X)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.5

A strictly commutative Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\) on a site \(\mathscr{S}\) is a groupoid field \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\) (GIRAUD [1]), equipped with a functor \(+ : \mathscr{P} \times_\mathscr{S} \mathscr{P} \to \mathscr{P}\) and of isomorphisms of functors

\[\tau_{x,y} : x + y \to y + x\]

\[\sigma_{x,y,z} : (x + y) + z \to x + (y + z)\]

which, for all \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\), make \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) a strictly commutative Picard category.

In what follows, we will simply speak of Picard field, without specifying "strictly commutative". According to 1.4.4, every Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\) admits a global "neutral object" \(e\), and \(\operatorname{Aut}(e)\) is an abelian beam.
