1.4.6. An additive functor \(F : \mathscr{P}_1 \to \mathscr{P}_2\) between Picard fields on \(\mathscr{S}\) is a \(\mathscr{S}\)-functor (necessarily Cartesian) provided with an isomorphism of functors
\[F(x + y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(x) + F(y)\]
making diagrams commutative
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ F(x + y) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{F(\tau)} & F(x) + F(y) \ar[d]^{\tau} \\ F(y + x) \ar[r] & F(y) + F(x) }\]
and

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em{ F((x + y) + z) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{F(\sigma)} & F(x + y) + F(z) \ar[r] & (F(x) + F(y)) + F(z) \ar[d]^{\sigma} \\ F(x + (y + z)) \ar[r] & F(x) + F(y + z) \ar[r] & F(x) + (F(y) + F(z)) }\]
An morphism of additive functors \(u : F \to G\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{S}\)-functors (automatically an isomorphism) making the diagram commutative
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ F(x + y) \ar[r]^-{u_{x+y}} \ar[d] & G(x + y) \ar[d] \\ F(x) + F(y) \ar[r]_-{u_x + u_y} & G(x) + G(y) }\]
1.4.7. If \(\mathscr{P}_1\) and \(\mathscr{P}_2\) are two Picard fields on \(\mathscr{S}\), the Picard field \(\operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2)\) is the following Picard field:
a) if \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\), its objects on \(U\) are the additive functors of \(\mathscr{P}_1|U\) in \(\mathscr{P}_2|U\); morphisms are morphisms of additive functors;
b) we define the sum of two additive functors \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) by the formula \((F_1 + F_2)(x) = F_1(x) + F_2(x)\); the structural isomorphism is the one which makes the diagram commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=1.5em@R=3em{ (F_1 + F_2)(x + y) \ar[rr]^-{\text{structural}} \ar@{=}[d] & & (F_1 + F_2)(x) + (F_1 + F_2)(y) \ar@{=}[d] \\ F_1(x + y) + F_2(x + y) \ar[dr] & & F_1(x) + F_2(x) + F_1(y) + F_2(y) \\ & F_1(x) + F_1(y) + F_2(x) + F_2(y) \ar[ur]_-{\tau} & }\]
c) the associativity and commutativity isomorphisms are defined via the analogous isomorphisms in \(\mathscr{P}_2\).

1.4.8. If \(\mathscr{P}_1\), \(\mathscr{P}_2\) and \(\mathscr{P}\) are three Picard fields, a biadditive functor of \(\mathscr{P}_1 \times \mathscr{P}_2\) in \(\mathscr{P}\) is a \(\mathscr{S}\)-functor \(F\) of \(\mathscr{P}_1 \times_\mathscr{S} \mathscr{P}_2\) in \(\mathscr{P}\), provided with isomorphisms of functors
such as
a) For \(x_1\) (resp. \(x_2\)) fixed, \(F(x_1, \times)\) (resp. \(F(\times, x_2)\)) checks the compatibilities of 1.4.3.
b) For \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\), \(x_1, y_1 \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}_1(U)\) and \(x_2, y_2 \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}_2(U)\), the diagram
is commutative.
For example, the canonical functor
\[\operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2) \times \mathscr{P}_1 \to \mathscr{P}_2 : (F, x) \mapsto F(x)\]
is biadditive.
We show as in 1.4.7 that the biadditive functors of \(\mathscr{P}_1 \times \mathscr{P}_2\) in \(\mathscr{P}\) form a Picard field \(\operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2 ; \mathscr{P})\).

We will see in (1.4.20) that there exists a "2-universal" biadditive functor: \(\mathscr{P}_1 \times \mathscr{P}_2 \to \mathscr{P}_1 \otimes \mathscr{P}_2\); more precisely, there exists a Picard field \(\mathscr{P}_1 \otimes \mathscr{P}_2\) and a biadditive functor \(\otimes : \mathscr{P}_1 \times \mathscr{P}_2 \to \mathscr{P}_1 \otimes \mathscr{P}_2\) such that for any Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\), the functor defined by \(\otimes\):
\[(1.4.8.1)\qquad \operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1 \otimes \mathscr{P}_2, \mathscr{P}) \to \operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2 ; \mathscr{P})\]
or an equivalence. This field is unique up to unique equivalence (up to unique isomorphism).

1.4.9. Let \(u : \mathscr{S}_1 \to \mathscr{S}_2\) be a morphism of sites. The direct image \(u_*\mathscr{P}\) of a Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(\mathscr{S}_1\) is the Picard field on \(\mathscr{S}_2\) defined by
\[u_*\mathscr{P}(V) = \mathscr{P}(u^*V) \;.\]

Its objects on \(V \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}_2\) are the objects of \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(u^*V\); its morphisms, its addition law and its compatibility isomorphisms are those of \(\mathscr{P}\). the direct image \(u_*\) is a 2-functor.

1.4.10. A Picard prefield \(\mathscr{P}\) on a site \(\mathscr{S}\) is a prefield \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\) (Giraud [1]), equipped with a functor \(+ : \mathscr{P} \times_\mathscr{S} \mathscr{P} \to \mathscr{P}\) and of isomorphisms of associativity and commutativity \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\), such that for each \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\), \(\mathscr{P}(U)\) is a Picard category (strictly commutative). If \(\mathscr{P}_1\) and \(\mathscr{P}_2\) are two Picard prefields, we define in an obvious way (see 1.4.6, 1.4.7) the additive functors of \(\mathscr{P}_1\) in \(\mathscr{P}_2\) and the Picard prefield \(\operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2)\) that they form. If \(\mathscr{P}\) is a Picard prefield, and \(j : \mathscr{P} \to a\mathscr{P}\) the field that it generates, there exists with essentially unique equivalence up to one and only one pair formed of a Picard field structure on \(a\mathscr{P}\) and an additive functor structure on \(j\); provided with these data, the pair \((j, a\mathscr{P})\) is called the Picard field generated by \(\mathscr{P}\). For any Picard field \(\mathscr{P}_1\), we have an equivalence
\[(1.4.10.1)\qquad \operatorname{HOM}(a\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{P}_1) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{P}_1) \;.\]

1.4.11. We will designate by \(C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) the category of abelian bundle complexes \(K\) on \(\mathscr{S}\) such as \(K^i = 0\) for \(i \notin [-1, 0]\). To any complex \(K \in \operatorname{Ob}C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\)
is associated with a Picard prefield \(\operatorname{pch}(K)\) following:
(I) For \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\), we have \(\operatorname{Ob}\operatorname{pch}(K)(U) = K^0(U)\).
(II) If \(x, y \in K^0(U)\), an arrow of \(x\) in \(y\) is an element \(f\) of \(K^{-1}(U)\) such that \(df = y - x\).
(III) The law of composition of arrows is the law of addition in \(K^{-1}(U)\).
(IV) The functor \(+\) is given by the law of addition in \(K^0(U)\) and \(K^{-1}(U)\).
(V) The associativity and commutativity isomorphisms are provided by the null element of \(K^{-1}(U)\).

We will designate by \(\operatorname{ch}(K)\) the Picard field generated (1.4.10) by the Picard prefield \(\operatorname{pch}(K)\). The beam \(\underline{H}^0(K)\) is interpreted as the following associated beam
\[(1.4.11.1)\qquad \underline{H}^0(K) \simeq a(U \mapsto \text{groupe des classes d'isomorphisme d'objets de } \operatorname{ch}(K)(U)) \;.\]
On the other hand,
\[(1.4.11.2)\qquad \underline{H}^{-1}(K) \simeq \operatorname{Aut}(e)\]
1.4.12. Any complex morphism \(f : K \to L\) induces an additive functor \(\operatorname{pch}(f) : \operatorname{pch}(K) \to \operatorname{pch}(L)\), hence an additive functor
\[\operatorname{ch}(f) : \operatorname{ch}(K) \to \operatorname{ch}(L)\]

It follows from (1.4.11.1) and (1.4.11.2) that \(\operatorname{ch}(f)\) is an equivalence if and only if \(f\) is a quasi-isomorphism. We deduce from 1.4.10.1 that for two morphisms \(f, g : K \to L\), we have
\[\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{ch}(f), \operatorname{ch}(g)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{pch}(f), \operatorname{pch}(g)) \;.\]
A morphism of functor \(h : \operatorname{pch}(f) \to \operatorname{pch}(g)\) is a morphism of sheaves \(h : K^0 \to L^{-1}\) such that
and such that for any triple \((x, y, u)\) with \(y - x = du\), we have
That \(h\) is a morphism of additive functors means that
The condition 1.4.12.3 allows to rewrite (1.4.12.2) as
the conditions (1.4.12.1) to (1.4.12.3) therefore mean that \(h\) is a homotopy of \(f\) to \(g\), and
\[(1.4.12.4)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{ch}(f), \operatorname{ch}(g)) \simeq \{H : \text{homotopie } K \to L \mid g - f = dH + Hd\}\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.13

(I) For any Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\), there exists a complex \(K\) such that \(\mathscr{P} \simeq \operatorname{ch}(K)\).

(II) For any additive functor \(F : \operatorname{ch}(K) \to \operatorname{ch}(L)\), there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(k : K' \to K\) and a morphism \(\ell : K' \to L\) such that \(F \simeq \operatorname{ch}(\ell)\operatorname{ch}(k)^{-1}\).
Let us prove (I). Let \((k_i, U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family such that
(a) \(k_i \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{P}(U_i)\);
(b) any object of \(\mathscr{P}\) is locally isomorphic to an inverse image of one of \(k_i\).

Let's set \(K^0 = \bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathbb{Z}_{U_i}\) . We deduce from 1.4.3 that there exists an additive functor \(F\) of \(\operatorname{ch}(0 \to K^0)\) in \(\mathscr{P}\) sending the basis \(e_i\) of \(\mathbb{Z}_{U_i}\) to \(k_i\). Let \(K^{-1}\) be the sheaf of pairs formed by a local section \(x\) of \(K^0\) and an isomorphism \(t : F(0) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(x)\). 1.4.14. Let \(\operatorname{ch}^\flat(\mathscr{S})\) be the category whose objects are the small Picard fields on \(\mathscr{S}\), and whose arrows are the isomorphy classes of additive functors. The construct \(\operatorname{ch}\) defines a functor of \(C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) (1.4.11) in \(\operatorname{ch}^\flat(\mathscr{S})\). Let \(D^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) be the subcategory of the category derived from \(\mathscr{S}\) formed from the complexes \(K\) such as \(H^i(K) = 0\) for \(i \neq 0\) or \(-1\). It follows from 1.4.12 and 1.4.13 that

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4.15

The functor \(\operatorname{ch}\) induces an equivalence of categories

\[\operatorname{ch} : D^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{ch}^\flat(\mathscr{S}) \;.\]

We will designate by \(\flat\) the inverse equivalence of \(\operatorname{ch}\). For any Picard field \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(\mathscr{S}\), \(\mathscr{P}^\flat \in \operatorname{Ob}D^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) determines \(\mathscr{P}\) up to equivalence isomorphy class.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.16

Let \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) such that \(L^{-1}\) is injective.

(I) \(\operatorname{pch}(L)\) is already a field.

(II) For \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\), any additive functor \(F : \operatorname{ch}(K) \to \operatorname{ch}(L)\) is isomorphic to a functor \(\operatorname{ch}(f)\) for \(f\) morphism from \(K\) into \(L\).

(I) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{S}\) and \(x \in L^0(U)\), the sheaf of couples formed from a local section \(\ell\) of \(L^0|U\) and an isomorphism between \(x\) and \(\ell\) is a homogeneous principal space under \(L^{-1}\); it therefore admits a section and \(j : \operatorname{pch}(L) \to \operatorname{ch}(L)\) is surjective on the classes of isomorphies of objects, therefore an equivalence.

(II) Let \(L'\) be a complex of injectives, equipped with a quasi-isomorphism \(\varphi : L \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } L'\), such that \(\varphi^i\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leqslant -1\). According to 1.4.13, and [C.D], there exists a commutative diagram up to homotopy
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ L \ar[r]^-{\sim}_-{\varphi} & L' \\ K' \ar[r]_-{\sim}^-{\psi} \ar[u]^{f_0} & K \ar[u]_{f_1} }\]

such as \(F \simeq \operatorname{ch}(f_0)\, \operatorname{ch}(\psi)^{-1}\). Additionally, \(K = \tau_{\leqslant 0}K\) and \(L = \tau_{\leqslant 0}L'\), so that \(f_1\) factors into \(f : K \to L\), and \(F \simeq \operatorname{ch}(f)\).
The lemma allows us to specify 1.4.15 by the following consequence of 1.4.12.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.17

The construct \(\operatorname{ch}\) defines a 2-category equivalence between

a) the 2-category of Picard fields on \(\mathscr{S}\).

b) the 2-category having for objects and for 1-arrows the objects and arrows of the full subcategory of \(C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) formed of the complexes \(L\) with \(L^{-1}\) injective, and having for 2-arrows the homotopies between arrows: \(\operatorname{Hom}(f, g) = \{H \mid g - f = dH + Hd\}\).
Build 1.4.18. We have
\[\operatorname{HOM}(\mathscr{P}_1, \mathscr{P}_2)^\flat \simeq \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathscr{P}_1^\flat, \mathscr{P}_2^\flat) \;.\]
Let \(K, L \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{[-1,0]}(S)\), with \(L^{-1}\) injective. According to 1.4.16 (II) and 1.4.12, we have
\[(1.4.18.1)\qquad \operatorname{ch}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{ch}(K), \operatorname{ch}(L))\]
and we deduce a functorial construction 1.4.18.
Construction 1.4.19. For \(f : \mathscr{S}_1 \to \mathscr{S}_2\) a morphism of sites, we have
\[(f_*\mathscr{P})^\flat \simeq \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathscr{P}^\flat) \;.\]
Let \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{[-1,0]}\) with \(L^{-1}\) injective. According to 1.4.16 (I), we have
\[(1.4.19.1)\qquad \operatorname{ch}(f_*L) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f_*\operatorname{ch}(L)\]
and we deduce a functorial construction 1.4.19.
We will not have to use the
Construction 1.4.20. The tensor product promised in 1.4.8 exists, and
\[(\mathscr{P}_1 \otimes \mathscr{P}_2)^\flat \simeq \tau_{\geqslant -1}\, \mathscr{P}_1^\flat \overset{L}{\otimes} \mathscr{P}_2^\flat \;.\]
We check as in 1.4.12 and 1.4.13 (II) that, whatever \(K_1\), \(K_2\) and \(L\) in \(C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\), we have

(a) Any morphism \(f \in \operatorname{Hom}(K_1 \otimes K_2, L) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(\tau_{\geqslant -1}(K_1 \otimes K_2), L)\) defines a biadditive functor \(\operatorname{ch}(f) \in \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{ch}(K_1), \operatorname{ch}(K_2) ; \operatorname{ch}(L))\); moreover the morphisms of functors are identified with homotopies;

(b) For any biadditive functor \(F : \operatorname{ch}(K_1), \operatorname{ch}(K_2) \to \operatorname{ch}(L)\), there exist quasi-isomorphisms \(k_i : K_i' \to K_i\) and \(f : K_1' \otimes K_2' \to L\) such that \(F \simeq \operatorname{ch}(f) \circ (\operatorname{ch}(k_1), \operatorname{ch}(k_2))^{-1}\).

If now \(K_1^0\) or \(K_2^0\) is flat, as well as \(K_1'^0\) or \(K_2'^0\), then \(k_1 \otimes k_2 : \tau_{\geqslant -1}\, K_1' \otimes K_2' \to \tau_{\geqslant -1}\, K_1 \otimes K_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism, and we deduce that \(\operatorname{ch}(\tau_{\geqslant -1}(K_1 \otimes K_2))\) satisfies the universal property (1.4.8.1), hence the existence of \(\otimes\) and a functorial construction 1.4.20.

1.4.21. Let \(G\) be an abelian beam, and \(G[1]\) be the complex reduced to \(G\) placed in degree \(-1\). The prefield \(\operatorname{pch}(G[1])\) is identified with the prefield of trivial torsors under \(G\), and therefore \(\operatorname{ch}(G[1])\) "is none other" than the field of torsors under \(G\), with its usual addition law.
1.4.22. Let \(K \in C^{[-1,0]}(\mathscr{S})\) and \(G\) be an abelian sheaf. The extensions \(E\) of \(K\) by \(G\) form a Picard field, \(\operatorname{EXT}(K, G)\), for the Brauer addition.
\[E : 0 \to G[0] \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } E \xrightarrow{\ \beta\ } K \to 0 \;.\]

Each extension \(E\) is associated with an additive functor of \(\operatorname{ch}(K)\) in \(\operatorname{ch}(G[1])\), which, applied to a local section \(x\) of \(K^0\) provides the torso \(\beta^{-1}(x)\). The reader will verify that

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.4.23

The construction sketched above is an equivalence of Picard fields

\[\operatorname{EXT}(K, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{ch}(K), \operatorname{ch}(G[1])) \;.\]

1.4.24. Let \(F\) be an object of the site \(\mathscr{S}\). We will also designate by \(F\) the beam defined by \(F\), and we will designate by \(\mathbb{Z}^{(F)}\) the generated abelian beam. Let \(C\) be a Picard field on \(\mathscr{S}\). We easily verify (see 1.4.3) that it “is the same thing” to give oneself either
a) an additive functor \(H : \operatorname{ch}(\mathbb{Z}^{(F)}) \to C\)
b) a field morphism:
(locals field of \(F\), morphisms \(=\) identities) \(\to C\)
c) an object of \(C(F)\).

Suppose (to be able to apply definition 1.4.9) that the fiber products exist in \(\mathscr{S}\), and let \(f\) be the canonical site morphism \(f : \mathscr{S}/F \to \mathscr{S}\). The previous construction provides an equivalence
\[(1.4.24.1)\qquad \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{ch}(\mathbb{Z}^{(F)}), C) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f_*f^*C \;.\]
The isomorphism which is deduced from (1.4.24.1) by the construction \(\flat\)
\[\tau_{\leqslant}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbb{Z}^{(F)}, C^\flat) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*f^*C^\flat \;,\]
can also be deduced from the more general isomorphism
\[(1.4.24.2)\qquad R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbb{Z}^{(F)}, K) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf_*f^*K \qquad (K \in \operatorname{Ob}D^+(\mathscr{S})) \;.\]
1.5. The formula for universal coefficients.

1.5.1. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a smooth projective curve on \(S\). We will designate by \(\operatorname{PIC}(X/S)\) the Picard field on the large fppf site of \(S\) (XVII 0.10) direct image (1.4.9) of the field of invertible beams on \(X\). We have
\[(1.5.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{PIC}(X/S) = f_*\operatorname{ch}\mathbb{G}_m[1]\]
\[(1.5.1.2)\qquad \operatorname{PIC}(X/S)^\flat = \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathbb{G}_m[1]) \;.\]

Each section \(t\) of \(X\) defines an invertible beam \(\mathcal{O}(t)\) to \(X\). With the notations of 1.4.24 (cf. 1.4.24. a\(\leftrightarrow\)b), this construction, being compatible with any change of base, defines a morphism of Picard fields on \(S\)
\[(1.5.1.3)\qquad \operatorname{ch} \mathbb{Z}^{(X)} \to \operatorname{PIC}(X/S) \;.\]
If \(C\) is any Picard field on \(S\), the morphisms (1.5.1.3) and (1.4.24.1) define an additive functor
\[(1.5.1.4)\qquad \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(X/S), C) \to \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{ch}(\mathbb{Z}^{(X)}), C) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f_*f^*C \;.\]
By application of the construction \(\flat\), functor 1.5.1.3 defines a morphism
\[(1.5.1.5)\qquad \mathbb{Z}^{(X)} \to \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_* \mathbb{G}_m[1] \to Rf_* \mathbb{G}_m[1] \;,\]
and the functor 1.5.1.4 has as analogue, by 1.4.24.2, a morphism
\[(1.5.1.6)\qquad R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathbb{G}_m[1]), K) \to Rf_*f^*K \;.\]

If \(C\) is the field \(\operatorname{ch}(G[1])\) of the torsors under a group \(G\) satisfying (1.3.1.1), theorem 1.3.10 asserts that the functor 1.5.1.4 is an equivalence, the inverse equivalence being that which has a torsor \(K\) under \(G_X\) associates the additive functor \(\langle\times, K]\). That (1.5.1.4) is an equivalence amounts to saying that the morphism deduced from 1.5.1.4 or 1.5.1.6
\[(1.5.1.7)\qquad \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathbb{G}_m[1]), G) \to \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, Rf_*f^*G\]
is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.5.2

(universal coefficient formula).

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a projective and smooth curve on \(S\), \(C\) a Picard field on \(S_{\mathrm{fppf}}\) and \(K = C^\flat\) (1.4.15). We assume that \(K\) is locally isomorphic (in \(D^+(S_{\mathrm{fppf}})\)) to complexes of the form \(G_{-1} \to G_0\), where the sheaves \(G_i\) satisfy (1.3.1.1). So

(I) The functor 1.5.1.4

\[(1.5.2.1)\qquad \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(X/S), C) \to f_*f^*C\]

is an equivalence of Picard fields.

(II) The morphism deduced from (1.5.1.6) or (1.5.1.4)

\[(1.5.2.2)\qquad \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathbb{G}_m[1]), K) \to \tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*f^*K\]

is an isomorphism.
This theorem makes \(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*(\mathbb{G}_m[1])\) play the role that homology plays in the classic formula of universal coefficients.

It is clear that, for each field \(C\), we have (I)\(\leftrightarrow\)(II), and both (I) and (II) are local in nature on \(S\). If \(C\) is of the form \(\operatorname{ch}(G[1])\), then the theorem follows from 1.3.10, as noted above. It remains to show that if \(K\) is of the form
and if (1.5.2.2) is an isomorphism for \(G_{-1}[1]\) and \(G_0[1]\), then (1.5.2.2) is an isomorphism for \(K\). To verify this, simply compare the exact long cohomology sequence
to the analogous long exact sequence for the first member of (1.5.2.2), and to apply the five lemma.
1.5.3. Let \(u\) be a flat morphism of projective and smooth curves on \(S\)
\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.4em{ X \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[dr]_{f_1} & & Y \ar[dl]^{f_2} \\ & S & }\]
The additive functor diagram
\[(1.5.3.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathbb{Z}^{(X)} \ar[r] \ar[d]_{u} & \operatorname{PIC}(X/S) \ar[d]^{N_{X/Y}} \\ \mathbb{Z}^{(Y)} \ar[r] & \operatorname{PIC}(Y/S) }\]
is then essentially commutative.
For any Picard field \(C\) on \(S\), the diagram

\[(1.5.3.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(Y/S), C) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{HOM}(N_{X/Y}, C)} & f_{2*}f_2^*C \ar[d]^{u^*} \\ \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(X/S), C) \ar[r] & f_{1*}f_1^*C }\]
is therefore essentially commutative, and for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob}D^+(S)\), the diagram

\[(1.5.3.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.6em{ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_{2*}\mathbb{G}_m[1], K) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(N_{X/Y}, K)} & Rf_{2*}f_2^*K \ar[d]^{u^*} \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_{1*}\mathbb{G}_m[1], K) \ar[r] & Rf_{1*}f_1^*K }\]
is commutative.
Let now \(G\) be an abelian sheaf on \(S\) satisfying (1.3.1.1). Trace XVII 6.3 provides us with an additive functor
\[(1.5.3.4)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u : f_{1*}f_1^* \operatorname{ch}(G[1]) \to f_{2*}f_2^* \operatorname{ch}(G[1])\]
and 1.3.8.6 provides us with an isomorphism making the diagram essentially commutative

\[(1.5.3.5)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em{ \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(X/S), \operatorname{ch}(G[1])) \ar[d]_{\operatorname{HOM}(u^*, \operatorname{ch}(G[1]))} & f_{1*}f_1^* \operatorname{ch}(G[1]) \ar[l] \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_u} \\ \operatorname{HOM}(\operatorname{PIC}(Y/S), \operatorname{ch}(G[1])) & f_{2*}f_2^* \operatorname{ch}(G[1]) \ar[l] }\]
The diagram

\[(1.5.3.6)\qquad \xymatrix@C=1.6em{ \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_{1*}\mathbb{G}_m[1], G) \ar[d]_{R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(u^*, G)} & \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, Rf_{1*}f_1^*G \ar[l]_-{\sim} \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_u} \\ \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\tau_{< 0}\, Rf_{2*}\mathbb{G}_m[1], G) & \tau_{\leqslant 1}\, Rf_{2*}f_2^*G \ar[l]^-{\sim} }\]
is therefore commutative.
1.5.4. With the notations of 1.5.2, let \(G\) be an abelian sheaf on \(S\) satisfying 1.3.1.1. Locally on \(S\) for the sparse topology, we have (non-canonically)
\[\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*\mathbb{G}_m[1] = f_*\mathbb{G}_m[1] + R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m \;.\]

At the level of the Picard fields, we in fact define a section \(\operatorname{ch}(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m) \to f_*\operatorname{ch}(\mathbb{G}_m[1])\) by associating with each isomorphy class of invertible beams the invertible beam belonging to this class stiffened along suitable sections of \(X/S\).
Isomorphism 1.5.2.2 therefore provides an isomorphism
\[(1.5.4.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_S(X, G)\]
and an exact sequence
\[(1.5.4.2)\qquad 0 \to \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m, G) \to R^1f_*G \to \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*\mathbb{G}_m, G) \to 0 \;.\]
The morphisms of 1.5.4.1 and 1.5.4.2 are interpreted as follows
a) Morphism 1.5.4.1 and first morphism 1.5.4.2 are defined by the canonical application of \(X\) in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\).
b) The second morphism 1.5.4.2 is the degree 1.3.7.2.
Locally on \(S\) for the sparse topology, we have (non-canonically) \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S} \sim \operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S} \times \mathbb{Z}^k\); we therefore have
\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S}, G)\]
and (1.5.4.2) can still be written
\[(1.5.4.3)\qquad 0 \to \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S}, G) \to R^1f_*G \to \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_*\mathbb{G}_m, G) \to 0 \;.\]

1.5.5. When, in 1.5.2.2, we do \(K = \mathbb{G}_m[1]\), theorem 1.5.2 appears as a self-duality theorem for \(\tau_{\leqslant 0}(Rf_*\mathbb{G}_m[1])\). Any complex representing \(\tau_{\leqslant 0}\, Rf_*\mathbb{G}_m[1]\) admits a canonical filtration in three steps, the successive quotients being, up to unique quasi-isomorphism
\[\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}/\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S} \;, \quad \operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S} \;, \quad (f_*\mathbb{G}_m)[1] \;.\]

The previous self-duality corresponds to a duality (with values ​​in \(\mathbb{G}_m\)) between \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}/\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S}\) and \(f_*\mathbb{G}_m\), and a self-duality (with values ​​in \(\mathbb{G}_m[1]\)) on \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S}\) (Jacobian self-duality).

1.5.6. The situation is clearly less good for smooth curves on \(S\): \(f : X \to S\) which are not assumed to be clean on \(S\). It seems that the additive group is behaving uncontrollably. The preceding arguments however extend to the case of smooth curves which are deduced from a proper curve on \(S\) by subtraction of a finite part on \(S\), for \(G\) a flat torsion beam, of first torsion with the residual characteristics of \(S\). We can hope that the flat \(p\)-torsion beams also behave reasonably; see SERRE [1] ch VI n°11 p.126.
The role of theorem 1.2.2 will be played here by the acyclicity theorem XV 2.2, having as a corollary

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5.7

Let \(p : X \to S\) be the projection of a typical affine space on \(S\) and \(G\) be an abelian torsion beam on \(S_{\text{ét}}\), prime with the residual characteristics of \(S\). We also designate by \(G\) the reciprocal image of \(G\) on the site \(S_{\mathrm{fppf}}\). Then, the functor \(p^*\) is a category equivalence between the category of torsors under \(G\) on \(S_{\text{ét}}\) (or on \(S_{\mathrm{fppf}}\), which amounts to the same thing (Grothendieck [1])), and the category of torsors under \(G_X\) on \(X\).

1.5.8. Let \(\bar{f} : \overline{X} \to S\) be a clean morphism of finite presentation with purely fibers of dimension one, \(Y\) a subschema of \(\overline{X}\) finite on \(S\), defined by an ideal \(\underline{m}\) and suppose that \(X = \overline{X} - Y\) is a smooth curve on \(S\). Let on the other hand \(G\) be a torsion beam on \(S_{\text{ét}}\), prime with the residual characteristics of \(S\). We will again designate by \(G\) the beam on the large fppf site of \(S\) (XVII 0.10) reciprocal image of \(G\).

Let \(\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) be the sub-sheaf of \(\mathbb{G}_{mX}\) formed of sections worth 1 on \(Y\). A torsor under \(\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) is identified with an invertible sheaf on \(\overline{X}\), trivialized on \(Y\). Any relative divider on \(X\), finite on \(S\), defines such an invertible beam. Locally on \(S\) for the flat topology, there exist such dividers giving rise to relatively large invertible beams.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5.9

Under hypotheses 1.5.8, with \(S\) affines, let \(\mathcal{O}_{\overline{X}}(1)\) be a relatively large invertible beam on \(\overline{X}\), trivialized along \(Y\), and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be an invertible beam on \(\overline{X}\), trivialized along \(Y\). For any point \(s \in S\), there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that for any integer \(n \geqslant n_0\) and any section \(m_s\) of \(\mathcal{M}(n) \otimes k(s)\) on the fiber \(\overline{X}_s\), equal to 1 over \(Y_s\), there exists a section \(m\) of \(\mathcal{M}(n)\) worth 1 over \(Y\), which falls under \(m_s\).

We return to the \(S\) Noetherian case (because a family of surjective morphisms remains a family of surjective morphisms by base changes). Let \(\mathcal{N}\) be the coherent sheaf of local sections of \(\mathcal{M}\) null on \(\overline{X}_s\) and \(Y\). For each \(n\), the local solutions to the problem posed form a homogeneous main space under \(\mathcal{N}(n)\), so that the obstruction to be raised \(m_s\) is found in \(H^1(X, \mathcal{N}(n)) = H^0(S, R^1f_*\mathcal{N}(n))\). However, for \(n\) large enough, \(R^1f_*\mathcal{N}(n) = 0\) (EGA III 2.2.1).
1.5.10. Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be an invertible beam on \(\overline{X}\) trivialized along \(Y\), and \(s \in S\). Let's consider the conditions

(1.5.10.1) Any section of \(\mathcal{L} \otimes k(s)\) on \(X_s\), worth 1 on \(Y_s\), is raised to a section of \(\mathcal{L}\) on the reciprocal image \(U\) of a neighborhood of \(s\), worth 1 on \(Y|U\).
(1.5.10.2) There are 4 sections \(m_i\) of \(\mathcal{L} \otimes k(s)\) over \(X_s\), worth 1 over \(Y\), and linearly independent at the generic point of any irreducible component of \(X_s\).
Consider the following conditions, relating respectively to a section \(m\) of \(\mathcal{L}\), a pair \((m_1, m_2)\) and a triple \((m_1\, m_2\, m_3)\) of sections of \(\mathcal{L}\).

(1.5.10.3) \(m\) is 1 on \(Y\) and the subscheme \(Z(m)\) of \(X\) of equation \(m = 0\) is a relative divisor on \(S\) (which means that it is quasi-finite on \(S\), so ends on \(S\), since \(X = \overline{X} - Y\) is smooth on \(S\)).
(1.5.10.4) Let \(\lambda\) be the canonical coordinate on the affine line \(g : \mathbb{E}^1_S \to S\). Section \(\lambda m_1 + (1 - \lambda)m_2\) of \(g'^*\mathcal{L}\) checks (1.5.10.3).
(1.5.10.5) Likewise, \(\lambda m_1 + \mu m_2 + (1 - \lambda - \mu)m_3\) checks (1.5.10.3).
We leave it to the reader to verify, through a general position argument, the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5.11

Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be an invertible beam on \(X\), trivialized along \(Y\), and \(\bar{s}\) be a geometric point of \(S\) of image \(s\) in \(S\). If \(\mathcal{L}\) checks (1.5.10.1) and (1.5.10.2) in \(s\), then:

(I) If \(n\) sections \(m_i\) of \(\mathcal{L}\) satisfy (1.5.10.3), there exists an equal neighborhood \(U\) of \(\bar{s}\) and a section \(k\) of \(\mathcal{L}|f^{-1}(U)\) such that the \((m_i, k)\) pairs verify (1.5.10.4).

(II) Let \(m_1\), \(m_2\), \(k_1\), \(k_2\) be four sections of \(\mathcal{L}\) such that the pairs \((m_i, k_j)\) satisfy (1.5.10.4). There then exists a slack neighborhood \(U\) of \(\bar{s}\) and a section \(k\) of \(\mathcal{L}|f^{-1}(U)\) such that the triples \((m_i, k_j, k)\) satisfy (1.5.10.5).
For \(k(s)\) infinite, we could simply take a neighborhood of Zariski for \(U\).

1.5.12. Now let \(\mathcal{L}\) be an invertible beam on \(\overline{X}\) stiffened along \(Y\), and \(K\) be a \(G\)-torsor on \(X\), where \(G\) is like in 1.5.7. We propose to define a \(G\)-torsor \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) on \(S\), generalizing the one studied in 1.3, and additive in \(\mathcal{L}\).

Let \((m_1, m_2)\) be a pair of sections of \(\mathcal{L}\) which satisfies (1.5.10.4). With the notations of 1.5.10.3 and 1.5.10.4, the trace, from \(\lambda m_1 + (1 - \lambda)m_2 = 0\) to \(\mathbb{E}^1_S\), of the torso \(g'^*K\) is a torso \(K_{1,2}\) on \(\mathbb{E}^1_S\). Its reciprocal image by the section \(\lambda = 0\) (resp. \(\lambda = 1\)) is the torso \(K_2 = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_2)/S}(K)\) (resp. \(K_1 = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_1)/S}(K)\)). According to 1.5.7, the torso \(K_{1,2}\) is canonically the reciprocal image of a torso under \(G\) on \(S\); hence a canonical isomorphism between \(K_1\) and \(K_2\).
If \(m_1\), \(m_2\) and \(k\) are three sections of \(\mathcal{L}\) such that the pairs \((m_i, k)\) satisfy (1.5.10.4), we still obtain an isomorphism by composition
\[(1.5.12.1)\qquad \Psi_k : K_1 = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_1)/S}(K) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } K_2 = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_2)/S}(K)\]
If \(E\) is a relative divisor on \(X\), finite on \(S\), the sections \(m_i' = m_i \otimes 1\) of \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}(E)\) still satisfy (1.5.10.3), we have (for \(i = 0, 1\))
\[(1.5.12.2)\qquad K_i' = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_i')/S}(K) \simeq K_i + \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K)\]
and the isomorphism \(\Psi_{k \otimes 1} : K_1' \to K_2'\) is deduced from \(\Psi_k\):
\[(1.5.12.3)\qquad \Psi_{k \otimes 1} = \Psi_k + \operatorname{id}_{\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K)} \;.\]

To check that \(\Psi_k\) does not depend on \(k\), it suffices to do it in the even neighborhood of each point \(s\) of \(S\), and, by 1.5.12.3 and 1.5.9, we return to the case where the conditions (1.5.10.1) are verified. (1.5.10.2). With notations of 1.5.11. (II), \(\Psi_{\lambda k_i + (1 - \lambda)k} - \Psi_{k_i}\) is a morphism from \(\mathbb{E}^1_S\) into \(G\), null to \(\lambda = 1\), therefore null, and \(\Psi_{k_i} = \Psi_k\), therefore \(\Psi_{k_1} = \Psi_{k_2}\).

Let \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) be two sections of \(\mathcal{L}\) checking 1.5.10.3 and \(K_i = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m_i)/S}(K)\). To define an isomorphism \(\Psi_{m_2, m_1} : K_1 \to K_2\), it suffices to do it in the slack neighborhood of each point \(s\) of \(S\), in a manner compatible with the change of base. If \(E\) is a relative divisor on \(X\), finite on \(S\), such that \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}(E)\) satisfies (1.5.10.1) (1.5.10.2) (see 1.5.9), and if the pairs \((m_i \otimes 1, k)\) satisfies (1.5.10.4), we pose (see 1.5.12.3)
\[\Psi_{m_2, m_1} + \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K) = \Psi_k \;.\]

This defines \(\Psi_{m_2, m_1}\) and we verify by (1.5.11. (I)) that \(\Psi_{m_3, m_2}\, \Psi_{m_2, m_1} = \Psi_{m_3, m_1}\). These constructions are compatible with base changes. If there is a section \(m\) of \(\mathcal{L}\) checking (1.5.10.3), we will ask
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, K] = \operatorname{Tr}_{Z(m)/S}(K)\]
and, according to the above, this torsor does not depend, up to canonical isomorphism, on the choice of \(m\).

Let us note, in the general case, that it suffices to define \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) to define it in the even neighborhood of each point \(s\) of \(S\), in a manner compatible with the change of base. If, as above, \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}(E)\) verifies (1.5.10.1) (1.5.10.2), then \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}(E)\) admits sections verifying (1.5.10.3), and we set
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, K] = \langle\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}(E), K] - \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(K) \;.\]

1.5.13. Under assumptions 1.5.8, if \(x\) and \(y\) are two sections of \(f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\), then \(\lambda x + (1 - \lambda)y\) is still a section for \(\lambda\) in an open with non-empty fibers of the affine line on \(S\).
We can translate this by saying that
(1.5.13.1) "\(f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) is arc connected."

Any morphism of an open with non-empty fibers of the affine line in \(G\) is constant. There is therefore no non-trivial homomorphism of \(f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) into \(G\), and the automorphisms of \(\mathcal{L}\) act trivially on \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\). The torso \(\langle\mathcal{L}, K]\) therefore only depends on \(K\) and the section of \(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) defined by \(\mathcal{L}\). By globalization, this allows you to define a symbol \(\langle\lambda, K]\) for \(\lambda\) section of \(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\). The torsor \(\langle\lambda, K]\) is additive in \(\lambda\), and therefore corresponds to an extension \(e(K)\) of \(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) by \(G\) (see 1.4.23 or SGA 7 VII 1.1.6 and 1.2).
The formation of \(e(K)\) is compatible with any change of base and the addition of torsors, hence an additive functor
\[e : f_*\operatorname{ch}(G[1]) \to \operatorname{EXT}(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \;.\]
Let \(j\) be the canonical application of \(X\) in \(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\), \(j : t \mapsto (\text{classe de } \mathcal{O}(t))\). The inverse image by \(j\) is an additive functor
\[j^* : \operatorname{EXT}(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \to f_*\operatorname{ch}(G[1])\]
and we trivially have \(j^*e \sim \operatorname{Id}\). We check as in 1.3.10 and 1.5.2 that, in fppf topology

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.5.14

Under assumptions 1.5.8, the functors \(e\) and \(j\) of 1.5.13 are inverse equivalences of each other. We deduce by 1.4.19, 1.4.23 an isomorphism

\[\operatorname{Ext}^1(R^1f_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^1(X, G) \;.\]
1.5.15. Let \(f_i : X_i \to S\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) be two compactified curves as in 1.5.8.

Let \(\bar{u} : \overline{X}_1 \to \overline{X}_2\) be a morphism such that \(Y_1\) is a subschema of the reciprocal image of \(Y_2\) and which induces a finite and flat morphism of \(X_1\) in \(X_2\). We then have, for \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(X_2\) and \(K\) twister on \(X_1\),
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, \operatorname{tr}_{X_1/X_2}(K)] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle\bar{u}^*\mathcal{L}, K] \;,\]
and we deduce the commutativity of the diagram

\[(1.5.15.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{Ext}^1(R^1f_{1*}\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Ext}^1(u^*, \operatorname{Id})} & H^1(X_1, G) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{tr}_{X_1/X_2}} \\ \operatorname{Ext}^1(R^1f_{2*}\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & H^1(X_2, G) }\]

If \(\bar{u} : \overline{X}_1 \to \overline{X}_2\) is finite and flat, and satisfies \(Y_1 \supset \bar{u}^{-1}Y_2\) (as diagrams), we have for \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible beam on \(X_1\) and \(K\) twister on \(X_2\),
\[\langle\mathcal{L}, u^*K] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \langle N_{\overline{X}_1/\overline{X}_2}\mathcal{L}, K]\]
and we deduce the commutativity of the diagram

\[(1.5.15.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{Ext}^1(R^1f_{2*}\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Ext}^1(N_{X_1/X_2}, \operatorname{Id})} & H^1(X_2, G) \ar[d]^{u^*} \\ \operatorname{Ext}^1(R^1f_{1*}\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}), G) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & H^1(X_1, G) }\]
We could of course specify this result in terms of Picard fields.
1.6. An erasure theorem.
This no., from 1.6.6 (2nd demonstration) to the end, can be read independently of no. 2 to 5.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.1

Let \(X\) be a schema and \(Y\) be a closed subschema of \(X\) with complement \(U\), defined by an ideal \(\underline{m}\).

\[U \xrightarrow{\ j\ } X \xleftarrow{\ i\ } Y\]

Let \(\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) be the fpqc beam whose sections on a \(X\)-schema \(X_1\) are the sections of \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}^\times\) equal to 1 on the reciprocal image of \(Y\). For \(n \geqslant 1\) invertible on \(X\), the rest

\[0 \to j_!\mu_n \to \mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \xrightarrow{\ n\ } \mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \to 0\]

is an exact sequence of beams on the large flat site (XVII 0.10) of \(X\).
Let \(i\) be the morphism of large slack sites \(i : Y \to X\) induced by \(i\):
The functor \(i_*\) is exact. In the diagram

\[(1.6.1.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=1.8em{ & 0 \ar[d] & 0 \ar[d] & 0 \ar[d] & \\ 0 \ar[r] & j_!\mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \ar[r]^-{n} \ar[d] & \mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]^-{n} \ar[d] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & i_*\mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & i_*\mathbb{G}_m \ar[r]_-{n} \ar[d] & i_*\mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & 0 & 0 & 0 & }\]
the columns are correct, as well as the last two lines (IX 3.2). The first line is therefore correct.
1.6.2. Let \(k\) be a body and

an exact sequence of abelian beams on the large fppf site of \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\). We recall that if \(G_1'\) and \(G_2'\) are representable of finite type, and \(G_2''\) and \(G_1''\) locally representable of finite type, then \(G\) is representable and locally of finite type.
If \(P\) is an algebraic group of finite commutative type connected on \(k\), and \(n\) an invertible integer in \(k\), the sequence
\[(1.6.2.1)\qquad 0 \to {}_n P \to P \xrightarrow{\ n\ } P \to 0\]
is correct. If \(G\) is a finite commutative group spread over \(k\), canceled by \(n\), the first group and the last arrow of the exact sequence
\[\operatorname{Hom}(P, G) \to \operatorname{Hom}({}_n P, G) \xrightarrow{\ j\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(P, G) \xrightarrow{\ n\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(P, G)\]
are zero, hence an isomorphism
\[(1.6.2.2)\qquad j : \operatorname{Hom}({}_n P, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(P, G) \;.\]
1.6.3. Under the assumptions of 1.6.1, suppose \(X\) proper on the spectrum of a body \(k\)
The exact sequel
\[0 \to \mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \to \mathbb{G}_m \to i_*\mathbb{G}_m \to 0\]
defines an exact cohomology sequence of fppf sheaves on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), which is easy to interpret directly

\[(1.6.3.1)\qquad 0 \to p_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \to p_*\mathbb{G}_{mX} \to (pi)_*\mathbb{G}_{mY} \xrightarrow{\ j\ } R^1p_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m}) \to \operatorname{Pic}_{X/k} \to \operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k} \;.\]

The sheaves \(p_*\mathbb{G}_{mX}\) and \(p_*\mathbb{G}_{mY}\) can be represented by finite type group diagrams. The sheaves \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}\) are representable by group diagrams locally of finite type. According to 1.6.2, the beams \(p_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) and \(R^1p_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\) are representable. According to (1.3.13.1), the first is representable by a connected group diagram (and of finite type); the second is locally of finite type.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.6.4

Under the previous hypotheses, we denote by \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, X/k}\) the group diagram which represents \(R^1p_*\mathbb{G}_m(\underline{m})\). We denote by \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{\underline{m}, X/k}\) its neutral component and by \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau_{\underline{m}, X/k}\) the reciprocal image in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, X/k}\) of the torsion subgroup of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, X/k}/\operatorname{Pic}^0_{\underline{m}, X/k}\).

The diagram \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, X/k}\) represents the fppf beam generated by the pre-bundle which at each section \(S\) on \(k\) associates all the isomorphy classes of invertible modules on \(S \times_k X\), trivialized along \(S \times_k Y\) (1.5.8).
The exact continuation of Kummer 1.6.1 provides, for \(k\) algebraically closed and \(n\) invertible in \(k\), an isomorphism
\[(1.6.4.1)\qquad H^1_c(U, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } {}_n\operatorname{Pic}^\tau_{\underline{m}, X/k}(k) \;.\]

1.6.5. Let \(X\) be a smooth curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(\overline{X}\) the projective and smooth curve containing \(X\) as open dense, \(Y\) a subschema of \(\overline{X}\) such as \(X = \overline{X} - Y\), \(\underline{m}\) the ideal which defines \(Y\), \(n\) an invertible integer in \(k\) and \(G\) a finite abelian group killed by \(n\).

The group \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}\) is non-canonically the product of the subgroup \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}\) and a free abelian group. According to 1.6.4.1, 1.6.2.2, and 1.5.14, we therefore have a compound canonical isomorphism

\[\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Hom}(H^1_c(X, \mu_n), G) &\xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}({}_n\operatorname{Pic}^\tau_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}(k), G) = \operatorname{Hom}({}_n\operatorname{Pic}^0_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}, G) \\ &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}, G) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline{m}, \overline{X}/k}, G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^1(X, G) \end{aligned}\]
If \(u : X \to Y\) is a finite morphism between smooth curves on \(k\), we deduce from XVII 6.3. and (1.5.15.2) the commutativity of the diagram

\[(1.6.5.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{Hom}(H^1_c(Y, \mu_n), G) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Tr}_u, G)} & H^1(Y, G) \ar[d]^{u^*} \\ \operatorname{Hom}(H^1_c(X, \mu_n), G) \ar[r]_-{\sim} & H^1(X, G) }\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.6

Let \(X\) be a smooth curve on an algebraically closed body \(k\), \(n\) an invertible integer in \(k\) and \(u : X' \to X\) a moderate flat covering. Morphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : H^1_c(X', \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\]

is then (non-canonically) isomorphic to the transpose of the morphism

\[u^* : H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1(X', \mathbb{Z}/n) \;.\]
1st proof. Since \(\mu_n\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\), 1.6.6 is a special case of 1.6.5.1 for \(G = \mathbb{Z}/n\).

2nd proof. If \(k\) is the field \(\mathbb{C}\) of complex numbers, then, by the comparison theorems, the morphisms \(\operatorname{Tr}_u\) and \(u^*\) are identified with analogous morphisms, defined by the continuous application between topological spaces \(u : X'(\mathbb{C}) \to X(\mathbb{C})\):
\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : H^1_c(X'(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{Z}/n) \quad\text{et}\]
\[u^* : H^1(X(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1(X'(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{Z}/n) \;.\]
These two homomorphisms are transposed from one to the other by Poincaré duality.
By the Lefschetz principle, assertion 1.6.6 is still true for \(k\) of characteristic 0 (the cohomology groups considered are in fact invariant by change of algebraically closed base field).

If \(k\) is characteristic \(p > 0\), let \(W(k)\) be the Witt vector ring on \(k\). Let \(\overline{X}\) be the projective and smooth curve completed with \(X\). According to (SGA 1 III 7.4), the curve \(\overline{X}\) rises into a projective and smooth curve \(\overline{X}_1\) on \(W(k)\). Since \(\overline{X}\) is smooth, each point \(s_i \in S = \overline{X} - X\) rises in a section \(s_i'\) from \(\overline{X}\) to \(W(k)\); we set \(S_1 = \bigcup_i s_i'(\operatorname{Spec}(W(k)))\) and \(X_1 = \overline{X}_1 - S_1\).

Recall (SGA 1 XII) that the covering \(X'\) of \(X\) rises into a flat covering \(X_1'\) of \(X_1\), and that \(X_1'\) is deduced from a projective and smooth curve \(\overline{X}_1'\) on \(W(k)\) by subtraction of the union \(S_1'\) of a finite number of disjoint sections.
\[\xymatrix@R=2.4em@C=1.4em{ X_1 & & X \ar[ll] \\ X_1' \ar[u]^{u_1} \ar[d]_{f_1'} & & X' \ar[u]_{u} \ar[ll] \ar[d]^{f_1} \\ \operatorname{Spec}(W(k)) & & \operatorname{Spec}(k) \ar[ll] }\]

The spread sheaves \(R^1f'_{1*}(\mathbb{Z}/n)\), \(R^1f'_{1!}(\mathbb{Z}/n)\), \(R^1f_{1!}(\mathbb{Z}/n)\) and \(R^1f'_{1!}(\mathbb{Z}/n)\) are locally constant in formation compatible with base changes, as shown by the exact sequences connecting the cohomologies of \(\overline{X}_1\), \(X_1\) and \(S_1\), or \(\overline{X}_1'\), \(X_1'\) and \(S_1'\).
Special or generic geometric fibers of morphisms
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{u_1} : R^1f'_{1!} \mathbb{Z}/n \to R^1f_{1!} \mathbb{Z}/n\]
or
are therefore isomorphic, and this brings us back to the case already treated where \(k\) has zero characteristics.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.7

Let \(X\) be a connected smooth curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\) with characteristic exponent \(p\), and \(n\) be a prime integer at \(p\). There exists a main cover (\(=\) finite Galoisian (surjective)) \(u : X' \to X\), of degree dividing a power of \(n\), such that the trace morphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : H^1_c(X', \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\]

or zero.

Recall that the group \(H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\) is finite (IX 4.6 or XVI 5.2). Let \(u : X' \to X\) be the largest connected main abelian covering of \(X\) of Galois group killed by \(n\) (its Galois group is noted \(H_1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\)). By construction, homomorphism
\[u^* : H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1(X', \mathbb{Z}/n)\]
is rubbish. According to 1.6.6, homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : H^1_c(X', \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\]
is therefore null.
Recall the following lemma (EGA IV 15.6.5):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.8

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a smooth morphism and \(x\) be a section of \(f\). There then exists a Zariski open \(U\) of \(X\), containing \(x\), and such that the geometric fibers of \(f|U\) are connected.

blocks.title: Fundamental lemma
blocks.html:
Fundamental Lemma 1.6.9

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a smooth curve (1.1.2) compactifiable (XVII 3.2.), \(x\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(s\) its image in \(S\) and \(n \geqslant 1\) an invertible integer on \(S\). There exists a slack neighborhood \(V\) of \(s\) in \(S\) and a slack neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) in \(f^{-1}(V) = V \times_S X\)

\[(1.6.9.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=2.4em{ x \ar[r] \ar[d] & U \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_{f'} & f^{-1}(V) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{f_V} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ s \ar[r] & V \ar@{=}[r] & V \ar[r] & S \;, }\]

such that we have \(R^0f'_! \mathbb{Z}/n = 0\), that the arrow XVII 6.2

\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : R^1f'_! \mathbb{Z}/n \to R^1f_{V!} \mathbb{Z}/n\]

is zero, and the morphism traces

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{f'} : R^2f'_! \mathbb{Z}/n(1) \to \mathbb{Z}/n\]

or an isomorphism.

For \(R^0f'_!\mathbb{Z}/n = 0\), it is enough for \(f'\) to be quasi-affine; for \(\operatorname{Tr}_{f'}\) to be an isomorphism, it is sufficient for the geometric fibers of \(f'\) to be connected (1.1.9); thanks to 1.6.8, these properties of \(f'\) are easy to obtain, locally on \(S\) for the sparse topology. It remains to demonstrate for \(X\) with connected geometric fibers, the existence of a diagram (1.6.9.1) for which the arrow \(\operatorname{Tr}_u\) considered is zero: it will suffice to shrink \(U\) and \(V\) so that the two other required properties of \(f'\) are verified.

The problem is local on \(S\) in the vicinity of \(s\); by XVII 5.2.6 (change of basis), we immediately return to the \(S\) Noetherian case. By passing to the limit, and thanks to XVII 5.3.6 (constructibility) we return to the case \(S\) strictly local Noetherian, of closed point image of \(s\).

Let \(t\) be a geometric point of \(S\). According to 1.6.7, there exists a primary covering of prime degree at the characteristic exponent \(p\) of \(k(s)\) \(u : X_t^1 \to X_t\), such that the arrow
\[\operatorname{Tr}_u : H^1_c(X_t^1, \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X_t, \mathbb{Z}/n)\]

or zero. According to the acyclicity theorem XV 2.1 (or XV 2.6), there exists an equal neighborhood \(v : U \to X\) of \(x\) In \(X\) such as \(X_t^1\) split above \(U_t\), so that: \(v_t : U_t \to X_t\) is factorized by \(X_t^1\). We therefore still have
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{v_t} = 0 : H^1_c(U_t, \mathbb{Z}/n) \to H^1_c(X_t, \mathbb{Z}/n) \;.\]
For any geometric point \(t\) of \(S\), there therefore exists an equal neighborhood of \(x\) in \(X\)
\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.6em{ U \ar[rr]^-{v} \ar[dr]_{f'} & & X \ar[dl]^{f} \\ & S & }\]
such as the fiber in \(t\) of
\[(1.6.9.2)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_v : R^1f'_! \mathbb{Z}/n \to R^1f_! \mathbb{Z}/n\]
or zero (XVII 5.2.6).

For variable \(U\), the images of the arrows (1.6.9.2) form a decreasing filtering system of sub-beams of the constructible beam \(R^1f_! \mathbb{Z}/n\), which becomes zero at any geometric point of \(S\). By Noetherian recurrence, we verify that such a system is always stationary, with zero stationary value. There therefore exists \(U\) such that (1.6.9.2) is zero, and this completes the proof of 1.6.9.
2. The trace morphism
This § is devoted to the proof of theorems 2.9 and 2.12. The proof of 2.9 only uses n°1 of § 1.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism (XVII 3.2.1) of relative dimension \(\leqslant d\) and \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open subscheme of finite type of \(X\), whose complement is fiber by fiber of dimension \(< d\) (by example: \(U\) dense fiber by fiber in \(X\)). If \(F\) is a torsion beam on \(X\), the canonical arrow

\[R^{2d}(fj)_! j^*F \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^{2d}f_! F\]

is an isomorphism.
Let \(i\) be the inclusion of \(Y = X - U\) in \(X\). The exact sequence (XVII 5.1.16.2) provides us with an exact sequence
whose extreme terms are zero under (XVII 5.2.8.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism of relative dimension \(\leqslant d\), and \(u_i : X_i \to X\) a family of splayed, separated morphisms of finite type; or \(u_{ij} : X_{ij} = X_i \times_X X_j \to X\). For any torsion beam \(F\) on \(X\), the following

\[\bigoplus_{i,j} R^{2d}(fu_{ij})_! u_{ij}^*F \rightrightarrows \bigoplus_i R^{2d}(fu_i)_! u_i^*F \to R^{2d}f_! F \to 0\]

of arrows (XVII 6.2.7.2) is correct.
Results immediately from the exactness on the right of the functor \(R^{2d}f_!\) and from XVII 6.2.9.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a flat morphism of finite presentation. The open of \(X\) on which \(f\) is Cohen-Macaulay (EGA IV 6.8.1 and 12.2.1(vii)) is relatively finite and dense fiber by fiber.

The question is local on \(S\), which we can assume is Noetherian, hence the 1st assertion. The second is verified fiber by fiber, therefore for \(S\) spectrum of a body. The local rings of \(X\) at the maximum points of \(X\) are Artinian, therefore Cohen-Macaulay, hence the assertion since "Cohen-Macaulay" is an "open" property.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a flat morphism of finite presentation and Cohen-Macaulay, with \(S\) quasi-separated. Every point of \(X\) has an open (Zariski) neighborhood \(U\) such that there exists a \(S\)-quasi-finite and flat morphism of finite presentation of \(U\) in the typical vector bundle \(\mathbb{E}^d_S\).
This is a special case of EGA IV 15.4.3.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5

Let \(\underline{u} = (u_1, \ldots, u_d)\) and \(\underline{v} = (v_1, \ldots, v_d)\) be two systems of parameters of a local Cohen-Macaulay ring \(A\), of dimension \(d\) and of maximal ideal \(\underline{m}\). There exists a sequence \(\underline{w}_0 \ldots \underline{w}_n\) of parameter systems of \(A\), such that \(\underline{w}_0 = \underline{u}\), \(\underline{w}_n = \underline{v}\) and each \(\underline{w}_{i+1}\) is deduced from \(\underline{w}_i\), by modifying only one of the parameters.

We reason by induction on \(d\), the assertion being empty for \(d < 2\); we therefore assume \(d \geqslant 2\). For an element \(x \in \underline{m}\) to be \(A/u_1\) - regular (resp. \(A/v_1\) - regular) it is necessary and sufficient that \(x\) does not belong to any element of \(\operatorname{Ass}(A/u_1)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Ass}(A/v_1)\)); no finite union of prime ideals \(\neq \underline{m}\) being equal to \(\underline{m}\), there exists \(x \in \underline{m}\) which is \(A/u_1\) - and \(A/v_1\) - regular, therefore also systems of parameters \(\underline{a} = (u_1, x, \ldots)\) and \(\underline{b} = (v_1, x, \ldots)\). We conclude by applying the recurrence hypothesis to \(\underline{u} - \{u_1\}\) and \(\underline{a} - \{u_1\}\) in \(A/u_1\), to \(\underline{a} - \{x\}\) and \(\underline{b} - \{x\}\) in \(A/x\) and to \(\underline{b} - \{v_1\}\) and \(\underline{v} - \{v_1\}\) in \(A/v_1\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.6

Let \(X\) be a finite type Cohen-Macaulay scheme on an algebraically closed field \(k\) and \(u, v : X \to \mathbb{E}^d_k\) two quasi-finite and flat morphisms of \(X\) in a standard vector bundle. Any closed point \(x\) of \(X\) has an open (Zariski) neighborhood \(U\), such that there exists a sequence of morphisms \(w_0 \ldots w_n : U \to \mathbb{E}^d_k\), such that \(w_0 = u|U\), \(w_n = v|U\) and that \(w_{i+1}\) does not differ from \(w_i\) only by a single coordinate.

Let's first assume that \(u(x) = v(x) = 0\). The seeds in \(x\) of flat quasi-finite morphisms of \(X\) in \(\mathbb{E}^d_k\), sending \(x\) to 0, are then identified with the parameter systems of the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) and 2.6 results from 2.5. In the general case, we start to join \(u\) and \(u - u(x)\) (resp. \(v\) and \(v - v(x)\)) by the chain of morphisms
\[u - (u_1(x), \ldots, u_d(x), 0 \ldots 0) \quad (\text{resp. } v - (v_1(x), \ldots, v_d(x), 0 \ldots 0)) \;.\]

2.7. Consider a pair of composable \(S\)-compactifiable morphisms \(X \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ f\ } S\). Suppose \(f\) is of relative dimension \(\leqslant d\) and \(g\) is of relative dimension \(\leqslant e\). For any torsion beam \(F\) on \(X\), the spectral composition sequence
\[(2.7.1)\qquad R^pf_! R^qg_! F \Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(fg)_! F\]
then provides us (maximum cycle argument) with an isomorphism
2.8. Either
\[a^d : \mathbb{E}^d_S \to S\]

the typical vector bundle on a coherent scheme (i.e. quasi-compact quasi-separated) \(S\). If \(F\) is a torsion beam on \(S\), prime with the residual characteristics of \(S\), we define as follows, by induction on \(d\), a trace morphism
\[(2.8.1)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{a^d} : R^{2d}a^d_! F(d) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F \;.\]
For \(d = 0\), \(\operatorname{Tr}_{a^0}\) is the identity. For \(d = 1\), \(\operatorname{Tr}_{a^1}\) is the isomorphism (1.1.6) (cf. 1.1.9). Finally, we have a canonical isomorphism
\[\mathbb{E}^{d+1}_S \simeq \mathbb{E}^1_S \times_S \mathbb{E}^d_S = \mathbb{E}^1_{\mathbb{E}^d_S}\]

and we define \(\operatorname{Tr}_{a^{d+1}_S}\) as the compound of the isomorphisms \(\operatorname{Tr}_{a^d_S}\) and \(Ra^d_{S!}(\operatorname{Tr}_{a^1_{\mathbb{E}^d_S}})\). (see diagram Var 3 of 2.9).

The source (and purpose) of 2.8.1 is formation compatible with any base change. Any algebraic group on \(S\) acting on \(\mathbb{E}^d_S\) therefore acts on these sheaves; a connected algebraic group necessarily acts in a trivial way. The group of coordinate permutations, contained in the affine group, therefore acts trivially on the source (and goal) of 2.8.1.
(2.8.2) Isomorphism 2.8.1 is invariant under coordinate permutation.
This point could also be verified by interpreting (2.8.1) in terms of cup-products (XVII 5.4.2.2 and 5.4.3.5.).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.9

Consider the triples \((f, d, F)\) formed of a compactifiable morphism (XVII 6.3.) \(f : X \to Y\), of an integer \(d\) and of a torsion beam \(F\) on \(Y\) prime with the residual characteristics of \(Y\), the morphism \(f\) checking the condition: \((*)_d\) There exists an open \(U\) of \(X\) such that the restriction of \(f\) to \(U\) is a flat morphism of finite presentation with fibers of dimension \(\leqslant d\), and such that the fibers \(X - U\) are of dimension \(< d\).

It is in one and only one way possible to associate a trace morphism with each triple \((f, d, F)\) as above

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : R^{2d}f_! f^*F(d) \to F\]

such that the following conditions are verified:

(Var 1) (Functoriality). The trace morphism is functorial in \(F\).

(Var 2) (Base change compatibility). Whatever the Cartesian square

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ Y' \ar[r]_-{g} & Y }\]

with \(Y'\) quasi-compact quasi-separated, the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em{ R^{2d}f'_! f'^* g^*F(d) \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_{f'}} & R^{2d}f'_! g'^* f^*F(d) & g^* R^{2d}f_! f^*F(d) \ar[l]_-{\sim} \ar[d]^{g^*(\operatorname{Tr}_f)} \\ g^*F \ar@{=}[rr] & & g^*F }\]

in which the horizontal isomorphism is the base change arrow (XVII 5.5.2.1), is commutative.

(Var 3) (Composition compatibility). Whatever the pair of composable \(Z\)-compactifiable morphisms

\[X \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ f\ } Z \;,\]

the integers \(d\) and \(e\) such that \(f\) satisfies \((*)_d\) and that \(g\) satisfies \((*)_e\), and the torsion beam \(F\) on \(Z\), the compound morphism \(fg\) checks \((*)_{d+e}\) and diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2em{ R^{2d}f_! R^{2e}g_! g^*f^*F(d)(e) \ar[r]^-{R^{2d}f_!(\operatorname{Tr}_g)} \ar[d]_{\wr} & R^{2d}f_! f^*F(d) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_f} \\ R^{2(d+e)}(fg)_! (fg)^*F(d+e) \ar[r]_-{\operatorname{Tr}_{fg}} & F }\]

in which the vertical isomorphism is the arrow (2.7.2), is commutative.

(Var 4) (Normalizations).

(I) If \(f\) is finite locally free of rank \(n\) and if \(d = 0\), the compound morphism

\[F \to f_*f^*F = f_!f^*F \xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{tr}_f\ } F\]

is multiplication by \(n\).

(II) If \(f\) is the affine line type \(f : \mathbb{E}^1_Y \to Y\), and if \(d = 1\), the trace morphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f \quad R^2f_!f^*F(1) \to F\]

is the isomorphism (2.8.1)

a) For \(d = 0\), the condition \((*)_d\) is that \(f\) is a quasi-finished dish of finished presentation. By virtue of (XVII 6.2.3), for \(d = 0\), the trace morphism must coincide with the constructed morphism in loc. cit.
When \(f\) is the projection of the type affine space \(\mathbb{E}^d_S\) onto \(S\), it follows from (Var 4 (II)) and (Var 3) that \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) must be the isomorphism (2.8.1) (2.8.1).

b) Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a compactifiable morphism verifying the condition: \((**)_d\) There exists a \(Y\)-quasi-finite and flat morphism of finite presentation \(u\) of \(X\) in the standard vector bundle \(a^d : \mathbb{E}^d_Y \to Y\).
Let \(t(f, u)\) be the morphism "composed of the trace morphisms (2.8.1) and (SGA XVII 6.2.3) (cf. (Var 3))
\[t(f, u) = \operatorname{Tr}_{a^d} \circ R^{2d}a^d_!(\operatorname{Tr}_u) \;.\]

This morphism \(t(f, u)\) verifies (Var 1) and (Var 2). By change of base, to verify that \(t(f, u)\) only depends on \(f\) and \(d\), and not on \(u\), it suffices to verify it for \(Y\) spectrum of an algebraically closed body \(k\). By 2.2 the question is local to \(X\), so by 2.6 it is enough to show that \(t(f, u) = t(f, v)\) when \(u\) and \(v\) differ by only one coordinate which, by 2.8.2, we can assume to be the first. Setting \(E = \mathbb{E}^{d-1}_Y\), we therefore have a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix@R=2em@C=2.2em{ X \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[dr]|(0.4){v} \ar[ddr]|(0.55){x} \ar[dddr]_{f} & \mathbb{E}^1_E \ar[dd]|{a} \ar[r]^-{g} & \mathbb{E}^1_E \\ & & \\ & E \ar[d]|{w} & \\ & Y & }\]
We have by construction (2.8)
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{wa} = \operatorname{Tr}_w \circ R^{2(d-1)}w_!(\operatorname{Tr}_a) \;;\]
we deduce that \(t(f, u)\) is the "compound" of \(\operatorname{Tr}_w\) and \(t(x, u)\):

\[\begin{aligned} t(f, u) &= \operatorname{Tr}_{wa} \circ R^{2d}(wa)_! (\operatorname{Tr}_u) \\ &= \operatorname{Tr}_w \circ R^{2(d-1)}w_! (\operatorname{Tr}_a) \circ R^{2d}(wa)_! \operatorname{Tr}_u \\ &= \operatorname{Tr}_w \circ R^{2(d-1)}w_! (\operatorname{Tr}_a \circ R^2a_! (\operatorname{Tr}_u)) \;. \end{aligned}\]
Moreover (1.1.7), \(t(x, u)\) is none other than the morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_x\) of 1.1.6; we therefore have \(t(x, u) = t(x, v) = \operatorname{Tr}_x\) and \(t(f, u) = t(f, v)\).

We will designate by \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) the morphism \(t(f, u)\) constructed above. According to XVII 6.2.3, this trace morphism satisfies (Var 1), (Var 2), (Var 3) for \(e = 0\), and (Var 4). When it is defined, it necessarily coincides with the trace morphism sought, if the latter exists.

c) Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a compactifiable Cohen-Macaulay morphism, flat of finite presentation, purely of relative dimension \(d\). Let \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a covering of \(X\) by quasi-compact open sets, such that there exists a quasi-finite and flat \(Y\)-morphism of \(U_i\) in \(\mathbb{E}^d_Y\) (2.4). According to 2.2 and b), there then exists one and only one morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) making the following diagram commutative, where the notations of 2.2 are used:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=3em{ \bigoplus_{i,j} R^{2d}(fu_{ij})_!(fu_{ij})^*F(d) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \bigoplus_i R^{2d}(fu_i)_!(fu_i)^*F(d) \ar[r] \ar[dr]_{\bigoplus_i \operatorname{Tr}_{fu_i}} & R^{2d}f_!f^*F(d) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_f} \\ & & F }\]

This trace morphism does not depend on the chosen open covering (compare the two to the sum covering); it checks the conditions (Var 1), (Var 2), (Var 3) for \(e = 0\), (Var 4), as follows immediately from b).

d) Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a compactifiable morphism satisfying \((*)_d\), and \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the open promised by \((*)_d\). According to 2.3, even if it means replacing \(U\) by a smaller open, we can assume that \(fj\) is Cohen-Macaulay, and purely of dimension \(d\).
According to 2.1, the canonical morphism
\[R^{2d}(fj)_!(fj)^*F(d) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^{2d}f_!f^*F(d)\]
is an isomorphism, and we define \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) to be the compound
\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : R^{2d}f_!f^*F(d) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } R^{2d}(fj)_!(fj)^*F(d) \xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{Tr}_{fj}\ } F \;.\]

It is immediately, thanks to (Var 3) for \(e = 0\) established in c), that \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) does not depend on the choice of \(U\). The trace morphism verifies (Var 1), (Var 2), (Var 3) for \(e = 0\), (Var 4), and is the only one able to verify the imposed conditions. It remains to prove that it verifies (Var 3), and to do this we reduce ourselves, by the preceding arguments, to only considering pairs \((f, d)\), \((g, e)\) verifying \((**)_d\) and \((**)_e\).
The reader who would like to polish the reasoning which follows will first check the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9.1

Let \((f, g, h)\) be three composable \(T\)-compactifiable morphisms, satisfying \((**)_k\), \((**)_\ell\) and \((**)_m\).

\[X \xrightarrow{\ h\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Z \xrightarrow{\ f\ } T \;.\]

If the pairs \((f, g)\) and \((g, h)\) verify (Var 3), then for \((f, gh)\) to verify (Var 3), it is necessary and sufficient that \((fg, h)\) verifies (Var 3).
The condition considered in the conclusion still means that \(\operatorname{Tr}_{fgh}\) is "composed" of \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\), \(\operatorname{Tr}_g\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_h\).
e) Consider a commutative diagram:
\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=2.4em{ X \ar[r]^-{v} \ar[d]_{g} & \mathbb{E}^e_Y \ar[r]^-{u'} \ar[d]|{c} & \mathbb{E}^{d+e}_Z \ar[d]^{b} \\ Y \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & \mathbb{E}^d_Z \ar[d]^{a} & \\ & Z & }\]

The trace morphism relating to \(fg\) is the "compound" of the trace morphisms relating to \(ab\) and \(u'v\); it is again, by construction for \(ab\), and according to XVII 6.2.3 for \(u'v\), the "compound" of trace morphisms relating to \(a\), \(b\), \(u'\) and \(v\). On the other hand, the "compound" of the trace morphisms relating to \(f\) and \(g\) is the "compound" of those relating to \(a\), \(u\), \(e\) and \(v\). It is therefore a question of verifying that for any Cartesian diagram
\[(2.9.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathbb{E}^e_S \ar[r]^-{u'} \ar[d]_{b'} & \mathbb{E}^e_T \ar[d]^{b} \\ S \ar[r]_-{u} & T }\]
with \(u\) quasi-finite and flat finite presentation, the diagram of trace morphisms (2.8.1) and XVII 6.2. )

\[(2.9.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.6em{ R^{2e}b_! u'_! u'^*b^*F(e) \ar[r]^-{R^{2e}b_!(\operatorname{Tr}_{u'})} \ar@{=}[d] & R^{2e}b_! b^*F(e) \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{Tr}_b} \ar@{=}[d] & F \ar@{=}[d] \\ u_! R^{2e}b'_! b'^* u^*F(e) \ar[r]_-{u_!(\operatorname{Tr}_{b'})} & u_! u^*F \ar[r]_-{\operatorname{Tr}_u} & F }\]
is commutative.

By change of basis, it suffices to verify it for \(T\) spectrum of an algebraically closed body \(k\). If \(S\) is the sum of diagrams \(S_i\), we reduce ourselves to verifying the commutativity of (2.9.3) only for the basic diagrams (2.9.2) \(u_i : S_i \to T\). This allows us to suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a \(k\)-local Artinian algebra \(A\), of finite degree \(n = [A : k]\). If we then identify the sheaves on \(T\) (resp. on \(\mathbb{E}^e_T\)) with the sheaves on \(S\) (resp. on \(\mathbb{E}^e_S\)) by the functor \(u^*\) (resp. \(u'^*\)) (VIII 1.1), the morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_b\) identifies with the morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_{b'}\) (compatibility of (2.8.1) with base changes), while \(\operatorname{Tr}_u\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_{u'}\) identify with multiplication by \(n\), hence the assertion. This completes the demonstration of 2.9.
We saw from the demonstration that we have:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.10

The trace morphisms XVII 6.2.3, 1.1.6 and (2.8.1) are special cases of the trace morphism 2.9.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 2.10.1

We can show without difficulty that the trace morphism of 2.9 is an isomorphism if and only if the geometric fibers of \(f\) have exactly one irreducible component of dimension \(d\), and the "multiplicity" of this is prime to \(n\). We will not give here the direct demonstration of this fact which will result immediately from the "global duality theorem" of § 3.
2.11. Let \(f : X \to S\) and \(g : Y \to S\) be two \(S\)-compactifiable morphisms satisfying respectively \((*)_d\) and \((*)_e\).
\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.4em{ & X \times_S Y \ar[dl]_{\operatorname{pr}_1} \ar[dd]|{f \times g} \ar[dr]^{\operatorname{pr}_2} & \\ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & }\]
The Kunneth isomorphism (XVII 5.4.3)
\[Rf_!(\mathbb{Z}/n) \overset{L}{\otimes}_{\mathbb{Z}/n} Rg_!(\mathbb{Z}/n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R(f\times g)_!(\mathbb{Z}/n)\]
induces an isomorphism
\[(2.11.1)\qquad R^{2d}f_!\mathbb{Z}/n \otimes R^{2e}g_!\mathbb{Z}/n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^{2(d+e)}(f\times g)_!(\mathbb{Z}/n)\]
It is clear that \(f\times g\) checks \((*)_{d+e}\); we have compatibility:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.12

The following diagram

\[(2.12.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ R^{2d}f_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \otimes R^{2e}g_!\mathbb{Z}/n(e) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f \otimes \operatorname{Tr}_g} & R^{2(d+e)}(f\times g)_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d+e) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_{f\times g}} \\ \mathbb{Z}/n \ar@{=}[r] & \mathbb{Z}/n \;, }\]

in which the first horizontal arrow is a twisted form of (2.11.1), is commutative.
To verify this, simply use (Var 3), and the asymmetric interpretation of Kunneth's arrow used in the proof of XVII 5.4.3. (XVII 5.4.3.5).
2.13. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a \(S\)-compactifiable morphism satisfying \((*)_d\), \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of rings on \(S\) and \(n\) an invertible integer on \(S\) such as \(n\mathcal{A} = 0\).
For all \(K \in D(S, \mathcal{A})\), we have (XVII 5.2.6)
\[(2.13.1)\qquad K \overset{L}{\otimes}_{\mathbb{Z}/n} Rf_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf_!(f^*K(d)) \;.\]
Trace morphism
\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : R^{2d}f_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \to \mathbb{Z}/n\]
can still be interpreted as a morphism
\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : Rf_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d)[2d] \to \mathbb{Z}/n \;.\]
By tensorization with \(K\), this morphism defines, via (2.13.1), a trace morphism
\[(2.13.2)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_f : Rf_!(f^*K(d)[2d]) \to K \;.\]
As we will see in 3.2.5. the following theorem is essentially equivalent to the global duality theorem in equal cohomology.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.14

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a smooth and compactifiable morphism purely of relative dimension \(d\), and \(n\) be an integer \(\geqslant 1\) invertible on \(S\). Whatever the geometric point \(x\) of \(X\) of image \(s\) in \(S\), there exists a slack neighborhood \(V\) of \(s\) in \(S\) and a slack neighborhood \(U\) from \(x\) in \(f^{-1}(V)\)

\[(2.14.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=2.4em{ U \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[d]_{f'_V} & f^{-1}(V) \ar[r] \ar[d]|{f_V} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ V \ar@{=}[r] & V \ar[r] & S }\]

such as the morphism (XVII 6.2.7.2)

\[R^i f'_{V!} \mathbb{Z}/n \to R^i f_V \mathbb{Z}/n\]

be zero for \(i < 2d\), and that the morphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{f'_V} : R^{2d}f'_{V!} \mathbb{Z}/n(d) \to \mathbb{Z}/n\]

or an isomorphism.
Let's first prove it

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.14.2

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category, \(k\) be an integer \(\geqslant 0\), \((K_i)_{0 \leqslant i \leqslant 2k}\) be objects of \(D^b(\mathcal{A})\) such that \(H^p(K_i) = 0\) for \(p \notin [0, k]\), \(f_i : K_i \to K_{i+1}\) (\(0 \leqslant i \leqslant 2k-1\)) morphisms and \(f\) their compound. If \(H^p(f_i) = 0\) for \(p < k\), then there exists in \(D^b(\mathcal{A})\) a morphism \(\varphi\) of the complex \(H^k(K_0)[-k]\), reduced to \(H^k(K_0)\) placed in degree \(k\), in \(K_{2k}\), which renders commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ K_0 \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d] & K_{2k} \\ \sigma_{\geqslant k}(K_0) \ar@{=}[r] & H^k(K_0)[-k] \ar[u]_{\varphi} }\]

The lemma is trivial for \(k = 0\). Let's prove it by induction on \(k\). The recurrence hypothesis applied to complexes \(\sigma_{\geqslant 1}(K_i)[1]\) provides the existence of a morphism \(\varphi' : H^k(K_0)[-k] \to \sigma_{\geqslant 1} K_{2k-2}\) which makes the following diagram commutative, where \(f' = f_{2k-3}\ldots f_0\):
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ K_0 \ar[r]^-{f'} \ar[d] & \sigma_{\geqslant 1}K_{2k-2} \\ \sigma_{\geqslant k}(K_0) \ar@{=}[r] & H^k(K_0)[-k] \ar[u]_{\varphi'} }\]
For any complex \(L\), the distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix@R=1.4em@C=0.8em{ & \sigma_{\geqslant 1}(L) \ar[dl] & \\ \sigma_{\leqslant 0}(L) \ar[rr] & & L \ar[ul] }\]
provides, for any complex \(M\), an exact sequence

\[\ldots \to \operatorname{Hom}(M, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}(L)) \to \operatorname{Hom}(M, L) \to \operatorname{Hom}(M, \sigma_{\geqslant 1}L) \xrightarrow{\ j\ } \operatorname{Ext}^1(M, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}L) \;.\]
In particular, we have a diagram

\[(2.14.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=0.9em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Hom}(\sigma_{\geqslant k}K_0, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(\sigma_{\geqslant k}K_0, K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(\sigma_{\geqslant k}K_0, \sigma_{\geqslant 1}K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Ext}^1(\sigma_{\geqslant k}K_0, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}K_{2k-2}) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}(K_0, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(K_0, K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(K_0, \sigma_{\geqslant 1}K_{2k-2}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Ext}^1(K_0, \sigma_{\leqslant 0}K_{2k-2}) }\]

which is sent in the analogous diagrams relating to \(K_{2k-1}\) and \(K_{2k}\). By hypothesis, the morphism \(f'\) in ① has an image in ② which is raised in ③. The obstruction ④ to the element ③ rising at ⑤ has a zero image in the diagram (2.14.3) relating to \(K_{2k-1}\). In this new diagram, ③ is therefore raised at ⑤, and the difference between ① and the image of ⑤ has a zero image at ⑥, therefore is the image of an element ⑦. This element ⑦ has a null image in the diagram (2.14.3) relating to \(K_{2k}\); in this new diagram, \(f\) is therefore image of ⑤, and this solves the problem posed.
Let us prove that, for a given value of \(d\), statement 2.14 implies the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.14.4

Under the hypotheses of 2.14, there exists a diagram (2.14.1) such that the morphism XVII 6.2.4, in \(D^b(V, \mathbb{Z}/n)\) admits a factorization

\[\xymatrix@R=2.4em@C=1em{ Rf'_{V!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[rr] \ar[dr]_{t} & & Rf_{V!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \\ & \mathbb{Z}/n[-2d] \ar[ur]_{\varphi} & }\]

where \(t\) is the arrow

\[t : Rf'_{V!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \to \sigma_{\geqslant 2d}Rf'_{V!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) = H^{2d}(Rf'_{V!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d))[-2d] \xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{Tr}\ } \mathbb{Z}/n[-2d] \;.\]
Let's set \(f'_0 = f\) and construct, by induction, \(4d\) diagrams
checking 2.14. Let's set \(V = V'_{4d}\) and \(U_i = U'_i \times_{V'_i} V\). We obtain a diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=2.4em{ U_{4d} \ar[r] \ar@/_/[drrr]_{f_{4d}} & \cdots \ar[r] & U_1 \ar[r] \ar[dr]|{f_1} & U_0 \ar[r] \ar[d]|{f_0} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ & & & V \ar@{=}[r] & S \;, }\]
with \(U_0 = f^{-1}(V)\), such as arrows
\[R^i f_{k+1!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \to R^i f_{k!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d)\]
are zero and the arrow
\[\operatorname{Tr} : R^{2d}f_{4d!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \to \mathbb{Z}/n\]
or an isomorphism. According to 2.14.2, the corollary is verified for \(U = U_{4d}\).
We are now ready to prove 2.14 by induction on \(d\). The case \(d = 0\) is trivial and the case \(d = 1\) is none other than 1.6.9. So suppose \(d \geqslant 2\).

The problem being of a local nature on \(X\) and \(S\), we can suppose that \(f\) admits a factorization \(f = gh\), with \(g\) and \(h\) smooth compactifiables, purely of dimension \(d'\) and \(d''\) and that \(d'\), \(d'' < d = d' + d''\). Let's apply 2.14.4 to \(g\) and to the morphisms deduced from \(h\) by base change, so as to obtain a diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=2.2em{ U \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[dr]_{h'_0} & f^{-1}W \ar[r] \ar[d]|{h_0} & X \ar[d]^{h} \\ V \ar[r]^-{v} \ar[dr]_{g'_0} & g^{-1}W \ar[r] \ar[d]|{g_0} & Y \ar[d]^{g} \\ W \ar@{=}[r] & W \ar[r] & S \;, }\]
such that morphisms (XVII 6.2.7.3) admit factorizations
\[Rh'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d'') \xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{Tr}\ } \mathbb{Z}/n[-2d''] \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } Rh_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d'')\]
\[Rg'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d') \xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{Tr}\ } \mathbb{Z}/n[-2d'] \xrightarrow{\ \psi\ } Rg_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d')\]
and let \(\operatorname{Tr}_{h_0}\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_{g_0}\) be isomorphisms.
Let \(h_1\) and \(h_1'\) be the morphisms deduced from \(h_0\) and \(h'\) by the change of base \(V\), and let \(f' = g_0'h_1'\), \(f_W = g_0h_0\) .
The following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em{ Rf'_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[rr] \ar@{=}[d] & & Rf_{W!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar@{=}[d] \\ Rg'_{0!} Rh'_{1!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[r] & Rg_{0!} Rh'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[r] & Rg_{0!} Rh_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \;, }\]
as well as the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em@R=1.8em{ Rg'_{0!} Rh'_{1!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar@{=}[d] \ar@/_5em/[dddd] & & \\ Rg'_{0!} v^* Rh'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[rr]^-{Rg'_{0!}v^*(t)(d')} & & Rg'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d')[-2d''] \ar[d]^{t[-2d'']} \\ & & \mathbb{Z}/n[-2d] \ar[d]^{\psi[-2d'']} \\ & & Rg_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d')[-2d''] \ar[dr]^{Rg_{0!}(\psi)(d')} & \\ Rg_{0!} Rh'_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) \ar[urr]^-{Rg_{0!}(t)(d')} \ar[rrr] & & & Rg_{0!} Rh_{0!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d) }\]
For the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=2.2em{ U_1 \ar[r] \ar[dr]_{f'} & f^{-1}W \ar[r] \ar[d]|{f_W} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ & W \ar@{=}[r] & S \;, }\]

the canonical arrow of \(Rf'_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d)\) in \(Rf_{W!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d)\) is therefore factorized by \(\mathbb{Z}/n[-2d]\); the arrows of \(R^i f'_!\mathbb{Z}/n(d)\) in \(R^i f_{W!}\mathbb{Z}/n(d)\) are therefore zero for \(i < 2d\). Finally, \(\operatorname{Tr}_{f'}\) is an isomorphism as a compound of the isomorphisms \(\operatorname{Tr}_{g'_0}\) and \(Rg'_{0!} \operatorname{Tr}_{h'_1}\).
This completes the demonstration of 2.14.
3. The global duality theorem
3.1. The functor \(Rf^!\)
We will need a variation of the canonical flaccid resolutions.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.1

Let \(X\) be a coherent scheme (i.e. quasi-compact quasi-separated). The functor \(\varinjlim\) induces an equivalence between the category of Ind-objects of the category of constructible sheaves of sets (resp. of groups, resp. of abelian groups) and the category of sheaves of sets (resp. of finite Ind-groups, resp. abelian torsion groups).
Results from IX 2.7.2 and IX 2.7.3.

3.1.2. Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be an abelian torsion beam on a diagram \(X\), filtering inductive limit of constructible beams \((\mathfrak{F}_i)_{i \in I}\), and \(X_0\) be a conservative set of points of \(X\). We define the modified canonical flaccid resolution of \(\mathfrak{F}\) by the formula
\[C^*_\ell(\mathfrak{F}) = \varinjlim_i C^*(\mathfrak{F}_i) \;,\]

where the \(C^*(\mathfrak{F}_i)\) are the canonical flaccid resolutions of XVII 4.2.2. According to 3.1.1., for consistent \(X\), this definition does not depend on the choice of \(\mathfrak{F}_i\). It also switches to localization; this fact makes it possible to define \(C^*_\ell(\mathfrak{F})\) by globalization for \(\mathfrak{F}\) torsion beam on an arbitrary \(X\) diagram, not necessarily coherent.

The complex \(C^*_\ell(\mathfrak{F})\) is a resolution of \(\mathfrak{F}\), functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\), whose formation commutes to localization and filtering inductive limits; \(C^n_\ell\) functors are exact.

It follows that for a sheaf of rings \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(X\), the functors \(C^n_\ell\) transform \(\mathcal{A}\)-Modules into \(\mathcal{A}\)-Modules; they likewise transform torsion beams into torsion beams. It is well known that

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.3

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an \(\mathcal{U}\)-abelian category having a small generating set and such that the inductive limits indexed by a small filtering ordered set are representable in \(\mathcal{A}\), and are exact.
