For a functor \(F\) of \(\mathcal{A}^o\) in (Ens) to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that it transforms small inductive limits (any) into projective limits.
Cf. e.g. SGA 4 I 8.12.5, where we do not assume abelian \(\mathcal{A}\) nor exact \(\varinjlim\) filters.

We will apply this lemma to the category \(\operatorname{Mod}(X, \mathcal{A})\) of bundles of modules on a ringed site \((X, \mathcal{A})\). In this particular case, if the functor \(F\) is represented by the sheaf \(\underline{F}\), we have
\[(3.1.3.1)\qquad \underline{F}(U) = F(\mathcal{A}_U) \;.\]
This description of \(\underline{F}\) allows a direct verification of 3.1.3 in the considered case.
Recall that if \(f : X \to S\) is a compactifiable morphism (XVII 3.2.), then \(S\) is quasi-compact quasi-separated (sic), and the dimension of the fibers of \(f\) is bounded.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1.4

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism and \(\mathcal{A}\) be a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S\). So, the functor

\[(3.1.4.1)\qquad Rf_! : D(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \to D(S, \mathcal{A})\]

admits a partial right-wing deputy

\[(3.1.4.2)\qquad Rf^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) :\]

for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\), we have a functorial isomorphism

\[(3.1.4.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!K, L) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(K, Rf^!L) \;.\]

Equipped with the translation morphism making the diagram commutative

\[(3.1.4.4)\qquad \xymatrix@C=1.4em{ \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!(K[1]), L) \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(K[1], Rf^!L) \ar[l]_-{\text{adj.}} \ar@{=}[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(K, (Rf^!L)[-1]) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!K[1], L) \ar@{=}[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!K, L[-1]) \ar[r]^-{\text{adj.}} & \operatorname{Hom}(K, Rf^!(L[-1])) }\]

this functor \(Rf^!\) is a triangulated functor.
N.B. The notation \(Rf^!\) is abusive in that \(Rf^!\) is in general not the derivative of a functor \(f^!\).
Demonstration. Let \(d\) be an integer such that every fiber of \(f\) is of dimension \(< d\), and let's choose a compactification
\[\xymatrix@R=2em@C=1.6em{ X \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[dr]_{f} & \overline{X} \ar[dl]^{\overline{f}} \\ & S }\]
If \(K\) is a complex of \(f^*\mathcal{A}\)-Modules on \(X\), we have
\[Rf_! K \simeq R\overline{f}_*(j_! K) \;.\]
For any beam \(F\) on \(X\), the components \(F^i\) of the complex \(\tau_{\leqslant 2d}C^*_\ell j_! F\) verify
\[(3.1.4.5)\qquad R^k \overline{f}_* F^i = 0 \text{ pour } k > 0 \;.\]
Indeed, for \(i \neq 2d\), \(F^i\) is the inductive limit of flaccid beams, and for \(i = 2d\) and \(k > 0\), we have (XVII 5.2.8.1)

The single complex associated with the double complex truncated modified canonical flaccid resolution of \(K\) is a resolution of \(K\). By virtue of (3.1.4.5), we therefore have (notation of XVII 1.1.15)
\[(3.1.4.6)\qquad R\overline{f}_*(j_! K) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \overline{f}_* \tau''_{\leqslant 2d} C^*_\ell j_! K \;.\]
Let us designate by \(f_!^i\) the functor of the sheaves of modules on \(X\) in the complexes of sheaves on \(S\) defined by
\[(3.1.4.7)\qquad f_!^\bullet(F) = \overline{f}_* \tau_{\leqslant 2d} C^*_\ell j_! F \;.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.4.8

(i) The functors \(f_!^i\) are exact and commute at filtering inductive limits; we have \(f_!^i = 0\) for \(i \notin [0, 2d]\).

(ii) The functor \(f_!^\bullet\), being bounded to exact components, derives (XVII 1.2.10) trivially into \(Rf_!^\bullet\) or simply \(Rf_! : D(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \to D(S, \mathcal{A})\). Arrow 3.1.4.6 is an isomorphism of functors

\[Rf_!^\bullet \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf_! \;.\]

The functors \(C_\ell^i\) are exact and commute at the filtering inductive limits (3.1.2). They transform any beam into an acyclic beam. The functors \(\overline{f}_*\) and \(j_!\) commute at the filtering inductive limits. We deduce that
a) the functors \(\overline{f}_*C_\ell^i j_!\), therefore also the functors \(f_!^i\), commute at the filtering inductive limits;
b) for \(i < 2d\), the functors \(f_!^i = \overline{f}_*C_\ell^i j_!\) are exact. For \(i = 2d\), we check that \(f_!^i\) is exact by (3.1.4.5).
The remaining assertions of 3.1.4.8 are trivial.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.1.4.9

The definition of \(f_!^i\) is independent of the ring sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\), and retains meaning for any abelian sheaf. Assertion 3.1.4.8 (i) remains valid in the categories of abelian torsion sheaves on \(X\) and \(S\).
According to 3.1.4.8 and 3.1.3, the functor
\[f_!^i : \operatorname{Mod}(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \to \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A})\]
has a deputy on the right \(f_i^!\); since \(f_!^i\) is exact, \(f_i^!\) transforms injectives into injectives.
With the sign convention (XVII 1.1.12), the functors \(f_i^!\) form a complex of left-exact functors, zero in cohomological degree \(n \notin [-2d, 0]\):
\[(3.1.4.10)\qquad f_\bullet^! : \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A}) \to C^b(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \;.\]
The functor \(f_\bullet^!\) is derived (XVII 1.2.10) into a triangulated functor
\[(3.1.4.11)\qquad Rf^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \;,\]

right adjoint to the functor \(Rf_!\) (\(= Rf_!^\bullet = Lf_!^\bullet\)) ("trivial duality formula"). More precisely, if \(I \in \operatorname{Ob}C^+(S, \mathcal{A})\) is a complex of injective bundles of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules, the \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules \(f_i^! I^n\) are injective, and we have an isomorphism of triple complexes (XVII 1.1.12)
\[(3.1.4.12)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f_!^\bullet K, I) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K, f_\bullet^! I) \;.\]
Passing to \(H^0\) of the associated simple complex (calculated by products), we obtain the addition statement. We leave it to the reader to check compatibility (3.1.4.4).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.1.5

Suppose that the functor \(Rf^!\) has finite cohomological amplitude. According to XVII 1.2.10 the functor \(f_\bullet^!\) then admits a derivative

\[(3.1.5.1)\qquad Rf^! : D(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D(X, \mathcal{A}) \;.\]

This derivative is still appended on the right to the functor \(Rf_!\) (3.1.4.1). In fact, we have

\[\operatorname{Hom}_{D(S)}(Rf_!K, L) \simeq \varinjlim_{L \to L'} \operatorname{Hom}_{K(S)}(f_!^\bullet K, L') \simeq \varinjlim_{\substack{K' \to K \\ L \to L'}} \operatorname{Hom}_{K(S)}(f_!^\bullet K', L')\]

\[\operatorname{Hom}_{D(X)}(K, Rf^!L) \simeq \varinjlim_{K' \to K} \operatorname{Hom}_{K(X)}(K', Rf^!L) \simeq \varinjlim_{\substack{K' \to K \\ L \to L'}} \operatorname{Hom}_{K(X)}(K', f_\bullet^! L')\]

and, by addition,

\[\operatorname{Hom}_{K(S)}(f_!^\bullet K', L') = H^0 \operatorname{Hom}(f_!^\bullet K', L') \simeq H^0 \operatorname{Hom}(K', f_\bullet^! L') = \operatorname{Hom}_{K(X)}(K', f_\bullet^! L') \;.\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.6

The triangulated functor

\[Rf^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\]

(or, under the assumptions of 3.1.5,

\[Rf^! : D(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \;),\]

right adjoint to the functor \(Rf_!\) (3.1.4.1) is called the extraordinary inverse image functor.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1.7

Assume the compactifiable morphism \(f : X \to S\) of relative dimension \(\leqslant d\).

(i) If \(L \in \operatorname{Ob}D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\) checks \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i \leqslant k\), then \(H^i Rf^!L = 0\) for \(i \leqslant k - 2d\).

(ii) For \(L \in \operatorname{Ob}D^b(S, \mathcal{A})\), we have

\[\dim \operatorname{inj} (Rf^!L) \leqslant \dim \operatorname{inj} (L) \;.\]

The functor \(Rf^!\) is the right derivative of \(f_\bullet^! : \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A}) \to C^b \operatorname{Mod}(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\). Assertion (i) follows from \((f_\bullet^!)^i = 0\) for \(i < -2d\), and assertion (ii) results from \((f_\bullet^!)^i = 0\) for \(i > 0\), and that the functors \(f_i^!\) transform injective into injective.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1.8

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a quasi-finite compactifiable morphism.

(i) The functor \(f_! : \operatorname{Mod}(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \to \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A})\) has a right adjoint \(f^! : \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A}) \to \operatorname{Mod}(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\), and \(Rf^!\) is the functor derived from the functor \(f^!\).

(ii) If \(f\) is a closed immersion, then \(f^!\) is the "supported sections in \(X\)" functor.

(iii) If \(f\) is equal, then \(f^!\) identifies with the functor \(f^*\), with

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : f_! f^* F \to F\]

as an addition morphism.

Assertion (i) immediately follows from the particular case \(d = 0\) of the demonstration of 3.1.4: we have \(f_!^\bullet = f_!\) and \(f_\bullet^! = f^!\). Equivalently, the existence of an adjoint functor \(f^!\) results from 3.1.3 and from the fact that \(f_!\) is exact and commutes at filtering inductive limits; the addition \((f_!, Rf^!)\) is a "trivial duality formula".
Assertion (ii) is clear, and (iii) is XVII 6.2.
3.1.9. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S\), and let us anneal \(X\) by the reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{A}\).
Let \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\), \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) and let us represent \(L\) by a lower bounded complex of injective sheaves, so that
\[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L) \;.\]
The complex \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L)\) has flaccid components, and therefore
\[(3.1.9.1)\qquad Rf_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L) \simeq f_*\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L) \;.\]
Let's choose a compactification of \(f\) and an integer \(d\) as in the proof of 3.1.4, hence a functor \(f_!^\bullet\) (3.1.4.7).

\[(3.1.9.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=2em{ X_V \ar[r]^-{k_X} \ar[d]_{j_V} & X \ar[rr]^-{j} \ar[dd]|(0.35){f} & & \overline{X} \ar[dd]^{\overline{f}} \\ \widetilde{X}_V \ar[d]_{f_V} \ar@/^1.5em/[urrr]^{k_{\overline{X}}} & & & \\ V \ar[rrr]_-{k} & & & S \;. }\]
For all \(V \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\text{ét}}\), we have an isomorphism
\[(3.1.9.3)\qquad k^*f_!^\bullet \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f_{V!}^\bullet k_X^* \;,\]
so that
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^\bullet(f_!^\bullet K, f_\bullet^! L)(V) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f_{V!}^\bullet(k_X^* K), f_{V!}^\bullet(k_X^* L)) \;.\]
The functionality of \(f_{V!}^\bullet\) provides us
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^\bullet(K, L)(X_V) = \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(k_X^* K, k_X^* L) \to \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f_{V!}^\bullet(k_X^* K), f_{V!}^\bullet(k_X^* L)) \;,\]
hence an arrow, at the level of the complexes,
\[(3.1.9.4)\qquad f_*\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^\bullet(K, L) \to \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^\bullet(f_!^\bullet K, f_!^\bullet L) \;.\]
According to (3.1.9.1), this arrow derives in
\[(3.1.9.5)\qquad Rf_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L) \to R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rf_!K, Rf_!L) \;.\]
Now are \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X, \mathcal{A})\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\). The addition arrow of \(Rf_!Rf^!L\) in \(L\) and (3.1.9.4) provide a compound arrow
\[Rf_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, Rf^!L) \to R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rf_!K, Rf_!Rf^!L) \to R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rf_!K, L) \;,\]
local form on \(S\) of the addition isomorphism:
\[(3.1.9.6)\qquad Rf_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, Rf^!L) \to R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rf_!K, L) \;.\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1.10

Arrow 3.1.9.6 is an isomorphism.
Let \(V \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\text{ét}}\) and let's use the notations of (3.1.9.2). Isomorphism (“transitivity”)
whose existence is the root of the functor theory \(Rf_!\), transposes into a location isomorphism (3.1.8.(iii))
represented at the complex level by a morphism
\[k_X^* f_\bullet^! \to f_{V\bullet}^! k^* \;.\]
To verify that 3.1.9.6 is an isomorphism, it suffices to verify that for all \(V \in \operatorname{Ob}S_{\text{ét}}\), (3.1.9.6) induces an isomorphism

\[(3.1.10.2)\ ;\quad \operatorname{Hom}(k_X^* K, k_X^* Rf^!L) \simeq H^0(V, Rf_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, Rf^!L)) \to H^0(V, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Rf_!K, L)) \simeq \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(k^*Rf_!K, k^*L)\]
For \(V = X\), this is none other than 3.1.4.3. The general case results via the following compatibility, which the editor has not verified:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.10.3

The diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em{ \operatorname{Hom}(k_X^* K, k_V^* Rf^!L) \ar[r]^-{(3.1.10.2)} \ar@{=}[d]^{(3.1.10.1)} & \operatorname{Hom}(k^* Rf_!K, k^*L) \ar@{=}[d]^{\text{(localisation)}} \\ \operatorname{Hom}(k_X^* K, Rf_V^! k_X^* L) & \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_{V!} k_X^* K, k^*L) \ar[l]_-{\sim}^-{\text{(3.1.4.3 sur } V)} }\]

is commutative.
3.1.11. Let us be a Cartesian square
\[(3.1.11.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_-{g} & S \;, }\]

with \(f\) compactifiable, \(\mathcal{A}\) a bundle of torsion rings on \(S\), \(\mathcal{B}\) a bundle of torsion rings on \(S'\) and \(M\) a complex of \((f'^*\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B})\) - bimodules, bounded above. We then have, at the complex level, a morphism of functors into \(K\) (\(K \in \operatorname{Ob}C(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\))
\[(3.1.11.2)\qquad M \otimes_\mathcal{B} g'^*f_!^\bullet K \to f'^{!}_{\bullet}(f'^*M \otimes_\mathcal{A} g'^*K) \;,\]
which transposes into a morphism of functors in \(L\) (\(L \in \operatorname{Ob}C(S', \mathcal{B})\))
\[(3.1.11.3)\qquad g'_! \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(f'^*M, f'^{!}_{\bullet}L) \to f^! g_* \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(M, L) \;.\]
This morphism derives into a morphism of functors in \(L\), from \(D^+(S', \mathcal{B})\) in \(D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\):
\[(3.1.11.4)\qquad Rg'_* R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(f'^*M, Rf'^!L) \to Rf^! Rg_* R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(M, L) \;,\]
which makes the following diagram commutative, where \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S', \mathcal{B})\):

\[\xymatrix@C=0.7em@R=2.4em{ \operatorname{Hom}(K, Rg'_* R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(f'^*M, Rf'^!L)) \ar[r]^-{①} \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(K, Rf^! Rg_* R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(M, L)) \ar[r]^-{\text{adj}} & \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!K, Rg_* R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(M, L)) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}(g'^*K, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(f'^*M, Rf'^!L)) \ar[d] & & \operatorname{Hom}(g^*Rf_!K, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_\mathcal{B}(M, L)) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}(f'^*M \overset{L}{\otimes}_\mathcal{A} g'^*K, Rf'^!L) & \operatorname{Hom}(Rf'_!(f'^*M \overset{L}{\otimes}_\mathcal{A} g'^*K), L) \ar[l]_-{\text{adj}} \ar[r]^-{②} & \operatorname{Hom}(M \overset{L}{\otimes}_\mathcal{B} g^*Rf_!K, L) \;. }\]
In this diagram, ② (deduced from arrow XVII 5.2.2.1) is an isomorphism (XVII 5.2.6). Arrow ① is therefore an isomorphism whatever \(K\), and this implies that (3.1.11.4) is an isomorphism.
The base change theorem for a proper morphism is therefore transposed into

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1.12

Arrow 3.1.11.4 is an isomorphism.
We call this isomorphism the induction isomorphism.
Here are 3 particular cases.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.1.12.1

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) be two sheaves of torsion rings on \(S\), and \(\varphi : \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{A}\) be a homomorphism. Let us denote by \(\rho\) the restriction of scalars from \(\mathcal{B}\) to \(\mathcal{A}\). We then have, for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S, \mathcal{B})\)

\[\rho Rf^!K \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf^!(\rho K) \;.\]
In 3.1.11.3 it is enough to take \(S = S'\), \(X = X'\), \(\mathcal{A} = \mathcal{A}\), \(\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B}\), \(M = \mathcal{A}\).
We therefore see that the ring bundle plays a bogus role in the construction of \(Rf^!\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.1.12.2

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) be a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S\) and \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(S, \mathcal{A})\), \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\). We have the induction formula

\[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*K, Rf^!L) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf^! R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K, L)\]

(do \(S = S'\), \(X = X'\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.1.12.3

For all square 3.1.11, and all \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S', \mathcal{A})\), we have

\[Rg'_* Rf'^! L \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf^! Rg_* L\]

(do \(\mathcal{B} = f^*\mathcal{A} = M\)).
3.1.13. For a compound \(gh\) of \(S\)-compactifiable morphisms
\[X \xrightarrow{\ h\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Z\]
composition isomorphism
\[c_{g,h} : Rg_! Rh_! \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R(gh)_!\]
transposes into a composition morphism
\[(3.1.13.1)\qquad c_{g,h}^! : Rh^! Rg^! \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R(gh)^!\]

verifying the usual cocycle condition for a triple compound. In other words, the categories \(D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) form the fibers of a category fibered and co-fibered on the category of objects the \(S\)-schemas \(f : X \to S\), having as arrows the \(S\)-compactifiable morphisms, the direct image functors (resp. reciprocal image) being the functors \(Rf_!\) (resp. \(Rf^!\)). For any commutative square of compactifiable morphisms,
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{g} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_-{g'} & S \;, }\]
we therefore have a base cochange morphism (XVII 2.1.3)
3.1.14. Consider a Cartesian square
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ X' \ar[r]^-{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_-{g} & S }\]

with \(f\) compactifiable, and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a beam of torsion rings on \(S\). We anneal \(S'\), \(X\) and \(X'\) by the reciprocal images of \(\mathcal{A}\), still designated by \(\mathcal{A}\) by abuse of notation. The change of base isomorphism
\[(3.1.14.1)\qquad g^*Rf_! \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf'_! g'^*\]

allows us to consider the categories \(D(S, \mathcal{A})\), \(D(X, \mathcal{A})\), \(D(S', \mathcal{A})\) and \(D(X', \mathcal{A})\) as the fibers of a category co-fibered on the category of the commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[d] & X \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ X & X \ar[l] }\;,\]

the direct image functors being the functors \(Rf_!\), \(Rf'_!\), \(g^*\) and \(g'^*\) (sic). When we restrict ourselves to the categories \(D^+\), we even obtain a fibered and co-fibered category on this diagram, the direct image functors being the functors \(Rf^!\), \(Rf'^!\), \(Rg_*\) and \(Rg'_*\) (sic). We deduce
a) a “transitivity isomorphism” (sic) for “inverse image” functors
\[Rg_* Rf^! \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf^! Rg_*\]
already obtained in 3.1.12.3 (with the same definition by transposition of (3.1.14.1)).
b) a “base change morphism” (XVII 2.1.3)
which, according to loc. cit., can be described in the following two ways:
a) by addition, giving yourself a morphism (3.1.14.2) amounts to giving yourself a morphism
The change of base theorem provides such a morphism as compound
\[Rf'_! g'^* Rf^! \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } g^* Rf_! Rf^! \xrightarrow{\ g^* \times \text{adj.}\ } g^* \;.\]
b) by addition, giving yourself a morphism (3.1.14.2) amounts to giving yourself a morphism
The isomorphism (3.1.12.3) provides such a morphism as a compound
\[Rf^! \xrightarrow{\ Rf^! \times \text{adj.}\ } Rf^! Rg_* g^* \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } Rg'_* Rf'^! g^* \;.\]

According to their interpretations in terms of fibered and cofibered categories, it is clear that the morphisms (3.1.12.3), (3.1.13.2) and (3.1.14.2) each verify two compatibility of type XII 4.4 for \(f\) or \(g\) composed of two morphisms.
The following general topology results will be used in the next issue.
3.1.15. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of sites. Let's designate by \(\Gamma(f)\) the following site
a) The category \(\Gamma(f)\) is the category of triples \((U, V, \varphi)\) with
\[U \in \operatorname{Ob}(X), \quad V \in \operatorname{Ob} S, \quad \varphi \in \operatorname{Hom}_X(U, f^*V) \;.\]
b) A family of morphisms \((u_i, v_i) : (U_i, V_i, \varphi_i) \to (U, V, \varphi)\) is covering if, in \(X\), the morphisms \(u_i : U_i \to U\) covering \(U\).
We then have
(3.1.15.1) The functor of \(\Gamma(f)\) in \(X\) given by
\[(U, V, \varphi) \mapsto U\]
is an equivalence of sites \(\gamma : X \to \Gamma(f)\).
(3.1.15.2) The functor \(f_\Gamma^*\) of \(S\) in \(\Gamma(f)\)
\[V \mapsto (f^*V, V, \operatorname{Id}_{f^*V})\]

is a site morphism, denoted \(f_\Gamma : \Gamma(f) \to S\), or simply \(f\) by notation abuse. It checks for \(f_\Gamma\gamma = f\) . The functor \(f_\Gamma^*\) admits a right adjoint \(f_{\Gamma*}\):
\[f_{\Gamma*} : (U, V, \varphi) \mapsto V \;.\]
The functor \(f_\Gamma\) is therefore a site comorphism of \(\Gamma(f)\) in \(S\), and defines the same topos morphism as the site morphism \(f_\Gamma\).
\[(3.1.15.3)\qquad \xymatrix@R=2em@C=1em{ X \ar[rr]^-{\approx} \ar[dr]_{f^*} & & \Gamma(f) \ar[dl]|{f_\Gamma^*} \ar@/^2em/[dll]^{f_\Gamma} \\ & S & \;. }\]
We deduce the following rule to calculate the inverse image of a beam
(3.1.15.4) Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a beam on \(S\), \(f_\Gamma^*\mathfrak{F}\) the pre-beam on \(\Gamma(f)\) given by
\[f_\Gamma^*\mathfrak{F}(U, V, \varphi) = \mathfrak{F}(V) \;,\]
and \(af_\Gamma^*\mathfrak{F}\) the generated beam. So
\[f^*\mathfrak{F}(U) \simeq af_\Gamma^*\mathfrak{F}(U, V, \varphi) \;.\]

3.1.16. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of sites, \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of rings on \(S\), \(\mathcal{B}\) a sheaf of rings on \(X\) and \(d\) an integer. Consider an object \(K\) of the following type

(3.1.16.1) \(K\) associates with each object \(U\) of \(X\) a complex \(K(U)\) of \((\mathcal{A}, f_*(\mathcal{B}|U))\) -modules on \(S\), zero in degree \(> d\), and \(K(U)\) is a covariant functor of \(U\).

For any beam of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules \(\underline{F}\) on \(S\), we will designate by \(K^!\underline{F}\) the complex of beams on \(X\) generated by the complex of pre-bundles of \(\mathcal{B}\)-modules:
\[U \mapsto \operatorname{Hom}_\mathcal{A}(K(U), \underline{F}) \;.\]
We will designate by \(RK^!\) the derivative of \(K^!\)
\[RK^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, \mathcal{B}) \;.\]
If \(U : K_1 \to K_2\) is an object morphism (3.1.11.1), then \(U\) induces functor morphisms
\[{}^t u : K_2^! \to K_1^! \quad\text{et}\quad RK_2^! \to RK_1^! \;.\]
Under the previous hypotheses, we have

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1.17

(i) For any point \(x\) of \(X\) and for all \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\), we have, denoting by \(V(x)\) the category of neighborhoods of \(x\)

\[(3.1.17.1)\qquad \underline{H}^n(RK^!L)_x \simeq \varinjlim_{U \in \operatorname{Ob}V(x)} \operatorname{Ext}^n(K(U), L) \;.\]

(ii) We therefore have a spectral sequence

\[(3.1.17.2)\qquad E_2^{pq} = \varinjlim_{U \in \operatorname{Ob}V(x)} \operatorname{Ext}^p(\underline{H}^{-q}(K(W)), L) \Longrightarrow \underline{H}^n(RK^!L)_x \;.\]

(iii) If \(X^0\) is a conservative set of points of \(X\), and \(u : K_1 \to K_2\) a morphism of objects (3.1.11.1), then, so that

\[{}^t u : RK_2^! \to RK_1^!\]

be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(n\) and all \(x \in X^0\), the morphism of pro-objects of \(\operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A})\)

\[(3.1.17.3)\qquad u_x : \text{"}\varprojlim\text{"}_{U \in \operatorname{Ob}V(x)} \underline{H}^n K_1(U) \to \text{"}\varprojlim\text{"}_{U \in \operatorname{Ob}V(x)} \underline{H}^n K_2(U)\]

or an isomorphism.
To prove (i), we take for \(L\) a complex of injectives, so that (3.1.17.1) is trivial; it is clear that (i)\(\Longrightarrow\)(ii).

That the condition (3.1.17.3) of (iii) is sufficient follows immediately from (3.1.17.2). We will not have to make use of what it is necessary. If \({}^t u\) is an isomorphism, then, according to (3.1.16.1), for any injective beam \(I\) on \((S, \mathcal{A})\), we have
\[\operatorname{Hom}(\text{"}\varprojlim\text{"}_{V(x)} \underline{H}^n K_2(U), I) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}(\text{"}\varprojlim\text{"}_{V(x)} \underline{H}^n K_1(U), I) \;,\]

and we conclude by noting that in any abelian category having enough injectives (here, \(\operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A})\)), the functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(\times, I)\) for \(I\) injective of \(\mathcal{A}\) form a conservative system of functors on \(\operatorname{Pro}(\mathcal{A})\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.1.18

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable morphism of schemes, and \(\mathcal{A}\) be a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S\). We anneal \(X\) by \(f^*\mathcal{A}\). Let's choose an integer \(d\) which increases the dimension of the fibers by \(f\), as well as a compactification of \(f\), and let's set (3.1.4.7))

\[(3.1.18.1)\qquad K(U) = f_!^\bullet((f^*\mathcal{A})_U) \;.\]

According to formula (3.1.3.1) for the adjoint of \(f_!^\bullet\), we have an isomorphism

\[(3.1.18.2)\qquad f_\bullet^! \simeq K^! : \operatorname{Mod}(S, \mathcal{A}) \to C^b \operatorname{Mod}(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \;.\]

and therefore

\[(3.1.18.3)\qquad Rf^! \simeq RK^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \;.\]

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.1.19

Let \(f : X \to S\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(d\) as in 3.1.16. Let \(K\) be an object of the following type

(3.1.19.1) For all \(W = (U, V, \varphi) \in \operatorname{Ob}\Gamma(f)\) (3.1.15), \(K(W)\) is a complex of \((\mathcal{A}_V, \varphi_*\mathcal{B}|U)\)-Modules on \(V\), functorial in \(W\): for any morphism \((u, v) : W_1 = (U_1, V_1, \varphi_1) \to W_2 = (U_2, V_2, \varphi_2)\) it is given

\[K(u, v) : K(W_1) \to v^*K(W_2) \;, \quad\text{i.e.}\]

\[K(u, v) : v_! K(W_1) \to K(W_2) \;.\]

Each object \(K\) (3.1.19.1) is associated with an object \(K'\) (3.1.16.1) relating to \(f_\Gamma : \Gamma(f) \to S\), given by

\[(3.1.19.2)\qquad K'(U, V, \varphi) = j_! K(U, V, \varphi)\]

for \(j\) "include morphism" from \(V\) into \(S\). We pose

\[K^! \underset{\text{defn}}{=} K'^! \quad\text{et}\quad RK^! \underset{\text{defn}}{=} RK'^! \;.\]

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.1.20

With the notations of 3.1.19, let's do \(\mathcal{B} = f^*\mathcal{A}\) and set

\[(3.1.20.1)\qquad K(U, V, \varphi) = \mathcal{A}_V \quad (\text{en degré } 0)\]

According to 3.1.15.4, we then have isomorphisms of functors

\[(3.1.20.2)\qquad K^! \simeq f^*\]

\[(3.1.20.3)\qquad RK^! \simeq f^* \;.\]
3.2. Poincaré duality.
3.2.1. Let \(f\) be a purely compactifiable flat morphism of relative dimension \(d\) (or, more generally, satisfying the condition \((*)_d\) of 2.9):
\[\xymatrix@R=2em@C=1.6em{ X \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[dr]_{f} & \overline{X} \ar[dl]^{\overline{f}} \\ & S & \;. }\]

Let \(n\) be an integer \(\geqslant 1\) invertible on \(S\) and suppose \(S\) ringed by a sheaf of rings \(\mathcal{A}\) such that \(n\mathcal{A} = 0\). According to 3.1.18, the functor \(Rf^! : D^+(S, \mathcal{A}) \to D^+(X, \mathcal{A})\) can be calculated by the method of 3.1.16, for \(K\) given by (3.1.18.1).

Let \(K'\) be the functor of type (3.1.19.1) which to \((U, V, \varphi) \in \operatorname{Ob}\Gamma(f)\) associates the complex of sheaves \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-d)[-2d]\) on \(V\), reduces \(\mathbb{Z}/n(-d)\) placed in degree \(2d\), and let \(K''\) the object of type (3.1.16.1) corresponding (3.1.19.2), relating to \(f : \Gamma(f) \to S\).

We can look at \(K\) and \(K''\) as 3.1.16.1 objects relating to \(\Gamma(f) \to S\). Moreover, for any \((U, V, \varphi) \in \operatorname{Ob}\Gamma(f)\), trace morphism 2.9 defines a sheaf morphism on \(V\)
\[\operatorname{Tr} : R^{2d}f_!\mathbb{Z}/n \to \mathbb{Z}/n(-d) \;,\]
hence, denoting by \(j\) the morphism of \(V\) in \(S\), a morphism of functors

\[(3.2.1.1)\qquad \begin{aligned} K(U) = \overline{f}_* \tau_{\leqslant 2d} C^*_\ell \mathbb{Z}/n_U &\to \underline{H}^{2d}(K(U))[-2d] \simeq \\ R^{2d}f_!(\mathbb{Z}/n)_U = j_! R^{2d}f_!^V \mathbb{Z}/n &\xrightarrow{\ \operatorname{Tr}_\varphi\ } j_! \mathbb{Z}/n(-d) = K''(U, V, \varphi) \;. \end{aligned}\]
A twisted form of 3.1.20 shows that
According to 3.1.16, we therefore have a compound morphism
\[(3.2.1.2)\qquad t_f : f^*(d)[2d] \simeq RK''^! \to RK^! \simeq Rf^! \;.\]

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.2.2

If the functor \(Rf^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension, then \(t\) is still defined as a morphism between functors of \(D(S, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\), (definition in 3.1.5).
We have another method for constructing an arrow (3.2.1.2), by addition from (2.13.2)
\[\operatorname{Tr} : Rf_!(f^*L(d)[2d]) \to L \;.\]
These two arrows coincide:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.3

For \(L \in \operatorname{Ob}D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\) (resp. \(D(S, \mathcal{A})\) if the functor \(Rf^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension), the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ Rf_!(f^*L(d)[2d]) \ar[r]^-{Rf_!(t_f)} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & Rf_! Rf^!L \ar[d]^{\text{adjonction}} \\ L \ar@{=}[r] & L }\]

is commutative.

Let us first give a variant of the definition of \(t\), in terms of the functor \(f_\bullet^!\) (3.1.4.10). Let \(U \in \operatorname{Ob}X_{\text{ét}}\), \(V \in \operatorname{Ob}S_{\text{ét}}\) and \(\varphi \in \operatorname{Hom}_f(U, V)\). By definition,
\[f_\bullet^!(L)(U) = \operatorname{Hom}(f_!^\bullet\mathcal{A}_U, L) \;;\]
the trace morphism defines a morphism
\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : f_!^\bullet\mathcal{A}_U \to \mathcal{A}_V(-d)[-2d]\]
which transposes into
\[t_\varphi : f_\bullet^!(L)(U) \leftarrow L(d)[2d](V) \;.\]
Morphisms \(t_\varphi\) define a morphism
\[t_f : f^*L(d)[2d] \to f_\bullet^!L\]

which we leave to the reader to verify that, in the derived category, it coincides with (3.2.1.2). This description shows that the following diagrams are commutative at the complex level, for \(L\) complex of Modules:

\[\xymatrix@C=2em{ \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(\mathcal{A}_V, L) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f^*\mathcal{A}_V(d)[2d], f^*L(d)[2d]) \ar[d]^{t_f} \\ \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f_!^\bullet\mathcal{A}_V(d)[2d], L) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f^*\mathcal{A}_V(d)[2d], f_\bullet^!L) \;. }\]
By functoriality of the arrows of this diagram in \(\mathcal{A}_V\), we deduce that for any beam, or any complex of beams \(K\), the following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=2em{ \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K, L) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f^*K(d)[2d], f^*L(d)[2d]) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f_!^\bullet K(d)[2d], L) & \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(f^*K(d)[2d], f_\bullet^!L) \ar[l]_-{\sim} }\;.\]
Taking \(K = L\) and \(\operatorname{Id} : L \to L\), we find 3.2.3 at the complex level.
I would be grateful to anyone who understood this demonstration to explain it to me.

3.2.4. We immediately deduce from this lemma and the definition (3.1.13) of composition isomorphisms by addition that if \(f\) is the compound of two flat morphisms of finite compactifiable presentation and purely of relative dimension \(d_1\) and \(d_2\): \(f = gh\), and if \(d = d_1 + d_2\), then the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ h^*(g^*L(d_2)[2d_2])(d_1)[2d_1] \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{t_h * t_g} & (gh)^*L(d)[2d] \ar[d]^{t_{gh}} \\ Rh^! Rg^! L \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R(gh)^!L }\]
is commutative.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2.5

(Poincaré duality). Let \(f : X \to S\) be a compactifiable smooth morphism (\(S\) is therefore quasi-compact quasi-separated(sic)), \(d\) the locally constant function on \(X\) "relative dimension of \(f\)", \(n \geqslant 1\) an invertible integer on \(S\), and \(\mathcal{A}\) a ring bundle on \(S\) verifying \(n\mathcal{A} = 0\). Then the functor

\[Rf_! : D(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \to D(S, \mathcal{A})\]

admits as right adjoint the functor of \(D(S, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\):

\[K \mapsto f^*K(d)[2d]\]

with the morphism (2.13.2)

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : Rf_! f^*K(d)[2d] \to K\]

for addition arrow:

\[\operatorname{Hom}(K, f^*L(d)[2d]) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Hom}(Rf_!K, L) \;.\]

Demonstration. We return to the constant case \(d\). According to the description 3.2.1 of the arrow \(t_f\) (3.2.1.2), theorem 2.14, attached to criterion 3.1.17. (iii), implies that the arrow \(t_f\) is an isomorphism (for \(L \in D^+(S, \mathcal{A})\))
\[t_f : f^*L(d)[2d] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } Rf^!L \;.\]

In particular, the functor \(Rf^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension, therefore is defined on the entire derived category. The morphism \(t_f\) remains an isomorphism for \(L \in D(S, \mathcal{A})\).

By definition, \(Rf^!\) is the right adjoint of the functor \(Rf_!\); according to 3.2.3, the addition arrow of \(Rf_! Rf^! K\) in \(K\) is identified, via \(t_f\), with \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\), and this completes the demonstration of 3.2.5.
We will now identify using \(t_f\) the functors \(f^*L(d)[2d]\) and \(Rf^!L\).

3.2.6. Let \(S\) be the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(X\) a compactifiable and smooth scheme on \(k\) purely of relative dimension \(d\), \(n\) an invertible integer in \(k\) and \(F\) a bundle of locally constant constructible \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules, i.e. a "system of coefficients" killed by \(n\). Designating by \(\vee\) the \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-dual, we have
\[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F, \mathbb{Z}/n) = F^\vee \quad (\text{en degré } 0) \;.\]
This formula results from the fact that \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is an \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-injective module. Isomorphism 3.2.5.1, or rather the isomorphism which is deduced from it
\[R\Gamma\, R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F, \mathbb{Z}/n(d)[2d]) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(R\Gamma_c(F), \mathbb{Z}/n) \;,\]
is therefore written here
\[(3.2.6.1)\qquad R\Gamma\, (F^\vee(d)[2d]) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(R\Gamma_c(F), \mathbb{Z}/n) \;.\]
Moving to cohomology groups, and again using that \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is an injective \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-module, this gives
\[(3.2.6.2)\qquad H^{2d-i}(X, F^\vee(d)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^i_c(X, F)^\vee \;,\]
which is the usual form of Poincaré duality.
